Let N(h) be the minimum number of keys in an AVL tree of height h, counting the empty tree as height −1.  
The worst-case balance of an AVL node forces its two children to have heights h−1 and h−2, giving the recurrence N(h)=1+N(h−1)+N(h−2) with N(−1)=0 and N(0)=1.  
This is the Fibonacci recurrence, so N(h)=F_{h+3}−1 where F_k is the k-th Fibonacci number.  
Because F_k ≥ φ^{k−2} for k≥2 with φ=(1+√5)/2≈1.618, we have N(h) ≥ φ^{h}.  
Given n keys, n ≥ N(h) ≥ φ^{h} implies h ≤ log_φ n.  
Changing bases, h ≤ (log₂ n)/(log₂ φ) ≈ 1.44 log₂ n.  
Therefore the height of any AVL tree with n nodes is at most 1.44 log₂(n) + O(1), so it is Θ(log n).

Let N(h) be the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h; clearly N(0)=1, N(1)=2 and, because the two sub-trees of an AVL node differ in height by at most 1, N(h)=1+N(h−1)+N(h−2).  
Unwinding gives N(h)+1 ≥ F_{h+2}, where F_k is the k-th Fibonacci number.  
Since F_k ≥ φ^{k−2} for all k≥2, with φ=(1+√5)/2≈1.618, we have N(h)+1 ≥ φ^{h}.  
Thus h ≤ log_φ(N(h)+1) ≤ log_φ(n+1), where n is the tree’s actual node count.  
Changing logarithm bases yields h ≤ log₂(n+1)/log₂φ ≈ 1.440 log₂(n+1).  
Therefore every AVL tree with n nodes has height h ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2) − 1 = Θ(log n).

Let N(h) be the minimum number of internal nodes in an AVL tree of height h.  
Because the two child sub-trees at every node differ in height by at most 1, the smallest tree of height h has one child of height h−1 and one of height h−2, giving the recurrence N(0)=1, N(1)=2 and for h≥2, N(h)=1+N(h−1)+N(h−2).  
Solving yields N(h)=F_{h+3}−1, where F_k denotes the Fibonacci numbers.  
Using F_k ≥ φ^{k−2} with φ=(1+√5)/2, we have n ≥ N(h) ≥ φ^{h+1}/√5 −1.  
Rearranging gives h ≤ log_φ((n+1)√5) −1.  
Changing logarithm base: h ≤ (1/log_2 φ)·log_2((n+1)√5) −1 ≈ 1.440·log_2(n+2) −1.  
Therefore the height of any AVL tree with n nodes satisfies h < 1.44·log_2(n+2), i.e., h = O(log n).

Let N(h) be the minimum number of keys that an AVL tree of height h can contain.  
Because the two sub-trees of an AVL node differ in height by at most 1, the smallest tree of height h consists of a root, a subtree of height h-1, and a subtree of height h-2, so  
     N(0)=1, N(1)=2, and N(h)=1+N(h-1)+N(h-2)  for h≥2.  
This recurrence is identical to that of the Fibonacci numbers, giving N(h)=F_{h+2}−1.  
With φ=(1+√5)/2≈1.618 and the bound F_k ≥ φ^{k−2}, we get N(h) ≥ φ^{h} / √5 − 1.  
If an AVL tree has n keys, then n ≥ N(h), hence n+1 ≥ φ^{h}/√5 and  
     h ≤ log_φ((n+1)√5).  
Converting the logarithm base gives h ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2) ≈ 1.4405 log₂n for n≥1.  
Thus the height of an AVL tree grows only logarithmically with its size, guaranteeing O(log n) search, insertion and deletion times.

Let N(h) be the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h, with N(0)=1 and N(1)=2.  
Because the taller child of any AVL root may be at height h−1 while the shorter is at least h−2,  
N(h)=1+N(h−1)+N(h−2).  
This recurrence matches the Fibonacci sequence, so N(h) ≥ F_{h+3}−1.  
Since F_k ≥ φ^{k−2} for φ=(1+√5)/2≈1.618, we get N(h) ≥ φ^{h}.  
Thus any AVL tree with n nodes satisfies n ≥ φ^{h}, giving h ≤ log_φ n.  
Converting bases, h ≤ log₂ n / log₂ φ ≈ 1.4405 log₂ n.  
More precisely, h ≤ 1.4405 log₂(n+2) − 1.328.  
Hence the height of an AVL tree is Θ(log n) and never exceeds about 1.44·log₂ n.

Let n(h) be the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h; then n(0)=1, n(1)=2 and, for h ≥ 2, n(h)=1+n(h−1)+n(h−2).  
This recurrence gives n(h)=F_{h+2}−1, where F_k is the kth Fibonacci number.  
Using F_k ≥ φ^{k−2} with φ=(1+√5)/2, we get n(h) ≥ φ^{h}−1.  
Thus h ≤ log_φ(n+1).  
Converting bases, h ≤ log_2(n+1)/log_2 φ < 1.44 log_2(n+2)−0.328.  
Hence an AVL tree with n nodes has height O(log n) and, more precisely, never exceeds 1.44 log_2(n+2)−0.328.

Let N(h) be the minimum number of nodes an AVL tree of height h can contain.  
Because the two sub-trees of an AVL node differ in height by at most 1, the worst case (minimum nodes) occurs when one child has height h-1 and the other h-2, giving the recurrence  
 N(0)=1, N(1)=2, N(h)=1+N(h-1)+N(h-2) for h≥2.  
If Fk denotes the Fibonacci numbers (F0=0, F1=1), the above recurrence implies by easy induction that N(h) ≥ F_{h+2} − 1.  
Using the closed form Fk = (φ^k − ψ^k)/√5 with φ=(1+√5)/2≈1.618 and |ψ|<1, we have F_{h+2} ≥ φ^{h}/√5, so  
 n ≥ N(h) ≥ φ^{h}/√5.  
Hence h ≤ log_φ(n√5) = log_2(n√5) / log_2(φ).  
Because 1/log_2(φ) ≈ 1.4405, an AVL tree with n nodes satisfies  
 h ≤ 1.4405·log₂(n) + O(1),  
showing the height is Θ(log n) with a constant no larger than about 1.44 in base-2 logarithms.

An AVL tree is rebalanced with four canonical rotations.  
1. Right Rotation (LL case): when a node’s left child’s left subtree causes imbalance; pivot on left child, making it the new root of the unbalanced subtree.  
2. Left Rotation (RR case): symmetrical to the right rotation; applied when a node’s right child’s right subtree is heavy.  
3. Left-Right (LR) Double Rotation: used for an LR imbalance where a node’s left child’s right subtree is heavy; first rotate the left child left, then rotate the node right.  
4. Right-Left (RL) Double Rotation: used for an RL imbalance where a node’s right child’s left subtree is heavy; first rotate the right child right, then rotate the node left.  
Each rotation restores the balance factor of every affected node to −1, 0, or +1 while preserving in-order sequence.

AVL rebalancing relies on four O(1) pointer operations.  
1. Single right rotation (LL case): when a node becomes left-heavy because its left child’s left subtree grew; the left child rises and the node falls to its right.  
2. Single left rotation (RR case): mirror image of the above, used when the right child’s right subtree causes right-heaviness; the right child rises and the node moves to its left.  
3. Left-right double rotation (LR case): imbalance comes from the left child’s right subtree; first rotate left on the left child, then rotate right on the unbalanced node.  
4. Right-left double rotation (RL case): imbalance comes from the right child’s left subtree; first rotate right on the right child, then rotate left on the unbalanced node.  
Each rotation preserves in-order key order and restores every affected node’s balance factor to −1, 0, or +1, thereby re-establishing the AVL height bound.

AVL trees are rebalanced with four basic rotations.  
1. Right (single) rotation: applied to a left-left case where a node’s left child is left-heavy; the left child becomes the new root of the subtree, the original root becomes its right child.  
2. Left (single) rotation: applied to a right-right case where a node’s right child is right-heavy; the right child becomes the new root of the subtree, the original root becomes its left child.  
3. Left-Right (double) rotation: applied to a left-right case where a node’s left child is right-heavy; first perform a left rotation on the left child, then a right rotation on the node.  
4. Right-Left (double) rotation: applied to a right-left case where a node’s right child is left-heavy; first perform a right rotation on the right child, then a left rotation on the node.

An AVL tree restores balance with four rotation patterns that realign subtree heights:

1. Right Rotation (LL case): performed when a node’s left child has a higher left subtree; pivot is the left child, which becomes the new parent, and the original parent moves to its right.  
2. Left Rotation (RR case): symmetric to right rotation; applied when a node’s right child’s right subtree is heavier, pivoting around the right child.  
3. Left-Right Rotation (LR case): used when a node’s left child has a higher right subtree; first execute a left rotation on the left child, followed by a right rotation on the original node.  
4. Right-Left Rotation (RL case): mirror of LR; a right rotation on the right child precedes a left rotation on the original node when its right child’s left subtree is heavier.

These rotations locally adjust heights in O(1) time, guaranteeing the AVL balance factor (-1, 0, +1) across the tree while preserving in-order key order.

AVL rebalancing uses four basic rotations:  

1. Right (single) rotation, applied when a node’s left child has a left-heavy subtree (LL case); it promotes the left child and demotes the unbalanced node to its right.  
2. Left (single) rotation, applied when a node’s right child has a right-heavy subtree (RR case); it promotes the right child and demotes the unbalanced node to its left.  
3. Left-Right (double) rotation, applied when a node’s left child has a right-heavy subtree (LR case); it first performs a left rotation on the left child, then a right rotation on the node.  
4. Right-Left (double) rotation, applied when a node’s right child has a left-heavy subtree (RL case); it first performs a right rotation on the right child, then a left rotation on the node.  

These rotations restore the balance factor of every affected node to –1, 0, or 1 while preserving the binary-search-tree order.

AVL trees rebalance with four localized rotations.  
1) Single Right (LL) rotation corrects a left–left imbalance by lifting the left child and sliding the original parent to its right.  
2) Single Left (RR) rotation, the mirror image, fixes a right–right imbalance by promoting the right child and moving the original parent left.  
3) Left-Right (LR) rotation treats a left-right imbalance: first rotate left on the left child, then right on the parent.  
4) Right-Left (RL) rotation resolves a right-left imbalance: rotate right on the right child, then left on the parent.  
These O(1) operations restore height balance while preserving the binary-search-tree order.

AVL rebalancing relies on four rotation patterns.  
1) Right (single) rotation is used for a left-left imbalance, moving the left child up and the root down to its right.  
2) Left (single) rotation is used for a right-right imbalance, moving the right child up and the root down to its left.  
3) Left-Right (double) rotation handles a left-right imbalance: perform a left rotation on the left child, then a right rotation on the root.  
4) Right-Left (double) rotation handles a right-left imbalance: perform a right rotation on the right child, then a left rotation on the root.  
Each rotation rearranges at most four nodes and their subtrees, restoring every affected node’s balance factor to −1, 0, or +1 while keeping in-order (BST) ordering intact.

Binary search starts with the whole list, discards half each step, and repeats until at most one element remains. If the list has n items, count how many times you can cut n in half before you reach 1; that count is the time taken. The mathematical word for “how many halvings until 1” is a base-2 logarithm, written log₂(n). For example, a list of 16 items needs 4 halvings (16→8→4→2→1), so log₂(16)=4. In general, doubling the list size adds only one extra halving step: 32 items need 5 steps, 64 need 6, and so on. Because the number of steps grows very slowly compared with the list length itself, we describe the running time as proportional to log₂(n). Using big-O notation, the worst-case time complexity of binary search is O(log n).

Binary search on a sorted list of n items first inspects the middle item, then continues in only the half that could still contain the target. Each step therefore cuts the remaining portion in half: n → n/2 → n/4 → n/8, and so on. The number of cuts you can make before only one item is left is “the logarithm base 2 of n,” written log₂(n); think of it as simply “how many times can you keep halving n until you reach 1.” Because this count rises very slowly—adding one step each time n doubles—binary search needs at most about log₂(n)+1 comparisons. Dropping the minor constant, computer scientists summarize the running time as O(log n). For example, a million-item list is resolved in roughly 20 checks, and even a billion-item list needs only about 30.

Binary search works by repeatedly cutting a sorted list in half to locate a target item. Each cut removes about half of the remaining items, letting the search zone shrink very quickly. To count how many cuts (steps) are needed for a list of length n, we introduce a logarithm. A base-2 logarithm is simply the number of times you can divide a quantity by 2 before it reaches 1. For instance, 16 can be halved four times (16 → 8 → 4 → 2 → 1), so log₂(16) = 4. In general, the maximum steps binary search needs is the base-2 logarithm of n (rounded up). Using Big-O notation, this time complexity is O(log n). Thus, even very large lists are searched quickly, since doubling the list size adds only one additional step.

Binary search repeatedly cuts a sorted list in half and keeps only the half that might contain the target.  
Starting with n items, one step leaves roughly n/2, two steps n/4, and after k steps n/2ᵏ remain.  
We stop when just 1 item is left, so we need k such that n/2ᵏ = 1, which means 2ᵏ = n.  
The number k is called the base-2 logarithm of n, written log₂(n); it simply counts how many times you must double 1 to reach n (or equivalently, how many times you must halve n to get down to 1).  
Thus binary search requires about log₂(n) comparisons in the worst case.  
Ignoring the constant implied by the base of the logarithm, the time complexity is O(log n).  
This runtime grows very slowly: even for one million items, binary search needs only about 20 steps.

Binary search halves the remaining portion of a sorted list at each step. Starting with n items, after one step at most n/2 remain, then n/4, n/8, and so on until only one item is left. The number of steps is therefore how many times you can cut n in half before you reach 1. This count is called log₂ n (read “log base 2 of n”). A logarithm answers the question “to what power must a base be raised to obtain a given number?”; for base 2, it asks how many times you multiply 2 by itself to reach n. Because each step of binary search does constant work and there are log₂ n steps, its time cost grows proportionally to log₂ n. In Big-O notation the complexity is O(log n). Doubling the list length adds only one extra comparison, so the algorithm scales very efficiently.

Binary search repeatedly halves the remaining portion of a sorted list until the target is found or no elements remain. Starting with N items, the inspected portion shrinks to N/2, then N/4, N/8, and so forth. The number of halving steps k needed to reduce N to 1 satisfies 2^k ≈ N, because doubling the final single item k times rebuilds the original list size. Thus k equals “the number of times you must multiply 2 by itself to reach N,” a quantity called the base-2 logarithm of N, written log₂N. Consequently, binary search performs about log₂N comparisons in the worst case. This count grows very slowly: doubling N adds only one extra comparison. Therefore, the algorithm’s time complexity as a function of list length N is O(log N).

Binary search repeatedly divides the sorted list in half, keeping only the half that could contain the target. Starting with n items, one comparison leaves about n/2 items, the next leaves n/4, then n/8, and so on. The question is how many halvings are needed until just one item remains. That count is called the base-2 logarithm of n, written log₂ n; for example, log₂ 8 = 3 because 8 → 4 → 2 → 1 takes three cuts. Thus, binary search finishes in about log₂ n comparisons in the worst case. In Big-O notation, constants and the base of the logarithm are ignored, so the time complexity is O(log n). Consequently, doubling the list size adds only one additional comparison, illustrating the method’s efficiency.

QuickSort works by recursively partitioning the input around a pivot.  
With balanced partitions (random or median‐like pivots), each level handles Θ(n) work and there are Θ(log n) levels, giving best‐ and average‐case time complexity Θ(n log n).  
If every pivot is the smallest or largest element, partitions become maximally unbalanced, producing Θ(n) levels and worst‐case time complexity Θ(n²).  
Using randomised pivot selection makes the Θ(n log n) behaviour hold in expectation.  
In‐place implementations need only the call stack: Θ(log n) extra space on average and Θ(n) in the worst case when recursion degenerates.

QuickSort partitions an n-element array around a pivot in Θ(n) time and then recursively sorts the two subarrays.  
If each partition splits the array roughly in half, the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n) yields T(n)=Θ(n log n), which is the best and average (expected) case.  
Randomized pivot selection or median-of-three heuristics make these balanced splits highly probable, so the expected running time is Θ(n log n).  
In the worst case—e.g., already sorted input with consistently poor pivot choices—the recurrence becomes T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n), giving T(n)=Θ(n²).  
Thus, QuickSort’s time complexity is Θ(n log n) on average/best case and Θ(n²) in the worst case, with no additional guarantees.

QuickSort’s running time depends on how evenly the partition step splits the input.  
• Best case: Each partition divides the array into two nearly equal halves; recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n) solves to Θ(n log n).  
• Average case: Over all possible pivot arrangements, the expected recurrence is the same as the best case, giving expected Θ(n log n) time.  
• Worst case: If every partition chooses the smallest or largest element as pivot (e.g., already-sorted data with poor pivot choice), the recurrence becomes T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n), yielding Θ(n²).  
• Using randomized pivot selection makes the worst-case occur with extremely low probability, so the algorithm has Θ(n log n) expected time.  
• The partition operation itself is Θ(n) and dominates each level of recursion.

QuickSort’s running time depends on how evenly the pivot splits the array.  
1. Best case (and average case for random or median‐of‐three pivoting): the pivot divides the n keys into two subarrays of about n/2 each, giving the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n), which solves to Θ(n log n).  
2. Expected case for randomized QuickSort is also Θ(n log n) because every element is equally likely to become a pivot, and the probability distribution over partition sizes yields an average depth of O(log n).  
3. Worst case arises when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g., already sorted input with naive first-element pivot), producing the recurrence T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n) and a total cost of Θ(n²).  
4. Space complexity is O(log n) expected due to recursion depth, but can degrade to O(n) in the worst case.

QuickSort partitions the input into two subarrays in Θ(n) time, then recursively sorts each part.  

If the pivot always splits the array roughly in half (best and expected cases with random or median-of-three pivots), the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n) solves to Θ(n log n).  

When the pivot selection is random, this Θ(n log n) bound holds in expectation.  

If the pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element (e.g., already sorted input with naive pivot choice), the recursion becomes T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n), giving a worst-case time of Θ(n²).  

Thus: best-case Θ(n log n), average/expected Θ(n log n), worst-case Θ(n²).

QuickSort scans the array once per partition, costing O(n) work per level of recursion.  
If each pivot splits the array about in half, the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) solves to Θ(n log n), giving both best-case and average-case time.  
When the pivot selection is random (or the input is random), the expected running time is also Θ(n log n).  
In the worst case—e.g., always choosing the smallest or largest element as pivot—the recurrence becomes T(n)=T(n−1)+O(n), yielding Θ(n²) time.  
Such worst-case behavior occurs on already sorted or adversarially arranged data when a deterministic bad pivot rule is used.  
Randomized pivot choice prevents any input from guaranteeing the quadratic bound, keeping the expected cost at Θ(n log n).  
QuickSort’s auxiliary space equals the recursion depth: Θ(log n) on average and Θ(n) in the worst case.

QuickSort performs a Θ(n) partition at each recursive level.  
If the pivot splits the array evenly (best and average cases), the recursion depth is Θ(log n), yielding Θ(n log n) time.  
With an unlucky pivot that leaves one side empty (e.g., already-sorted input and first/last-element pivot), the depth becomes Θ(n), producing Θ(n²) time.  
Randomized pivot selection or median-of-three sampling makes the Θ(n²) case occur with very low probability, so the expected running time remains Θ(n log n).  
In-place implementations use Θ(log n) extra space on average for the recursion stack, but Θ(n) in the worst case.  
Thus, QuickSort is typically fast—Θ(n log n) on average—yet has a quadratic worst-case bound.

QuickSort terminates because each recursive call operates on two proper sub-segments of the current segment, so the segment sizes decrease strictly and reach 0 or 1.  
During a call on A[l..r], Partition places a chosen pivot at index p and guarantees (i) every element in A[l..p-1] ≤ pivot and (ii) every element in A[p+1..r] ≥ pivot.  
Thus the pivot is already in its final sorted position.  
Induction basis: a segment of size 0 or 1 is trivially sorted.  
Induction hypothesis: QuickSort correctly sorts every segment of size < n.  
Inductive step: for a segment of size n ≥ 2, the two recursive invocations receive segments whose sizes are each < n, so by the hypothesis they return those segments in sorted order.  
Because all elements in the left segment are ≤ pivot ≤ all elements in the right segment, concatenating “sorted left, pivot, sorted right” yields a completely sorted segment A[l..r].  
Therefore, by induction, every call to QuickSort returns its segment sorted, and the top-level call sorts the entire array.  
Termination plus this partial-correctness proof give total correctness of the QuickSort algorithm.

Let QS(A) denote QuickSort applied to array A.  
1. Termination: each recursive call is made on proper sub-arrays whose total length is strictly less than |A|, so after finitely many splits all sub-arrays have size 0 or 1 and the recursion stops.  
2. Permutation property: the partition step only swaps elements, never creates or destroys them, and the recursive calls operate on disjoint subsets of the original elements; therefore the multiset of elements in the output equals that of the input.  
3. Sortedness is proved by induction on n = |A|.  
   • Base n ≤ 1: the array is trivially sorted.  
   • Inductive step: assume QS correctly sorts every array of length < n; let A have length n, pick pivot p, and partition A into L, p, R with x ≤ p for x∈L and x ≥ p for x∈R.  
     By the inductive hypothesis QS(L) and QS(R) return sorted arrays.  
     Because every element of QS(L) ≤ p ≤ every element of QS(R), concatenating QS(L), p, QS(R) yields a fully non-decreasing sequence.  
4. Combining the permutation property with sortedness shows the result is a sorted permutation of the input, hence QuickSort is correct.

QuickSort terminates because each recursive call handles strictly shorter subarrays until size 0 or 1 is reached.  
Proof of correctness uses induction on subarray length n.  
Base case: for n ≤ 1, the array is already sorted, and QuickSort returns it unchanged.  
Inductive hypothesis: assume QuickSort sorts any array of length < n.  
For length n, Partition places a pivot at position p so that every element left of p is ≤ pivot and every element right of p is ≥ pivot, while only swapping elements and thus preserving the multiset of keys.  
Both resulting subarrays have length < n, so by the hypothesis the recursive calls return them in sorted order.  
After the recursion, the full array consists of a sorted left part, the pivot, and a sorted right part, with all elements in the left part ≤ pivot ≤ all elements in the right part.  
Hence the whole array is sorted.  
By induction, QuickSort produces a sorted permutation of its input for every finite array, establishing its correctness.

Let QS(A,l,r) be QuickSort on sub-array A[l..r]; we prove by strong induction on n = r–l+1.  
Base: n ≤ 1 ⇒ array is already sorted, so QS returns it unchanged.  
Inductive step: assume QS correctly sorts every sub-array of length < n; for length n≥2 let p be the pivot chosen by partition.  
Partition is a permutation of A[l..r] in which every element ≤p is placed left of some final position k and every element ≥p is placed right of k, so (i) the multiset of elements is preserved and (ii) A[l..k–1] ≤ p ≤ A[k+1..r].  
QS then recursively sorts A[l..k–1] and A[k+1..r]; by the induction hypothesis these two segments emerge sorted and unchanged in content.  
Because all elements of the left segment are ≤p and all elements of the right segment are ≥p, concatenating “sorted-left, p, sorted-right” yields a globally non-decreasing sequence.  
Thus QS returns a sorted permutation of its input.  
Each recursive call handles a strictly smaller sub-array (either length < n or empty), so the recursion depth is finite and the algorithm terminates.  
Therefore QuickSort is correct: it terminates and outputs the input elements in non-decreasing order.

Let n be the number of elements in the sub-array A[l…r].  
Termination: every recursive call is made on sub-arrays strictly smaller than n (elements < pivot or > pivot), so the recursion bottoms out at n ≤ 1, guaranteeing finite completion.  
Partial correctness is proved by strong induction on n.  
Base case n ≤ 1: the sub-array is already sorted and is obviously a permutation of the input.  
Inductive step: assume QuickSort correctly sorts any sub-array of size < n.  
Partition places a chosen pivot p so that every element in L lies before p and is ≤ p, every element in G lies after p and is ≥ p, and together L, p, and G are a permutation of the original sub-array.  
By the induction hypothesis the recursive calls QuickSort(L) and QuickSort(G) return sorted sequences that are permutations of L and G, respectively.  
Because all elements of L are ≤ p ≤ all elements of G, concatenating the sorted L, the pivot, and the sorted G yields an overall sorted sequence that is a permutation of the original sub-array.  
Thus QuickSort terminates and always returns a sorted permutation of its input, i.e., QuickSort is correct.

Partition places the chosen pivot at index p so that every element left of p is ≤ pivot and every element right of p is ≥ pivot.  
Arrays of size 0 or 1 are already sorted; this is QuickSort’s base case.  
Induction hypothesis: QuickSort correctly sorts any array of length < n.  
For an array of length n > 1, after partition we recursively QuickSort the two subarrays whose lengths are each < n, so by the hypothesis they return sorted permutations of their inputs.  
Because all elements in the left subarray are ≤ pivot and all elements in the right subarray are ≥ pivot, concatenating “sorted-left, pivot, sorted-right” yields a globally sorted sequence containing exactly the original elements.  
Thus QuickSort outputs a sorted permutation of the input for every n by induction.  
Termination is guaranteed because each recursive call handles a strictly smaller subarray, reaching the base case after at most n levels.  
Therefore, QuickSort is correct.

Let A be the input array and n its length.  
Partition places every element x left of the pivot p with x ≤ p, every element right of p with x ≥ p, and keeps every element’s multiplicity, so the array after partitioning is a permutation of A that satisfies: (∀i<k) A[i] ≤ p ≤ (∀j>k) A[j] for the pivot’s final index k.  
Induct on n to show sortedness.  
Base case n ≤ 1 is trivially sorted.  
Induction step: assume QuickSort correctly sorts any array of size < n; after partitioning an n-element array, the subarrays L and R on either side of k have sizes < n, so by the induction hypothesis the recursive calls return sorted L and R.  
Because every element of L is ≤ p and every element of R is ≥ p, concatenating sorted L, the pivot(s), and sorted R yields a fully sorted permutation of the original array.  
Thus partial correctness holds.  
Each recursive call operates on a proper subarray (|L|+|R| = n−|E| < n), so the recursion depth is at most n and the algorithm terminates.  
Termination plus partial correctness gives total correctness: QuickSort always halts and returns the input’s elements in non-decreasing order.

In an unsorted list, finding a target requires inspecting each element until the item is found or the list ends, yielding Θ(n) time in the worst case.  
In a sorted list you can instead perform binary search: compare the target with the middle element, discard the half that cannot contain the target, and repeat, cutting the problem size in half each step.  
Binary search therefore finishes in Θ(log n) time.  
The fundamental difference is the additional information carried by ordering; knowing the relative order of items lets you rule out large portions of the data after a single comparison.  
Without that order, no element can be safely ignored until it has been examined, so every position is a potential match.  
Accordingly, sorted lists enable logarithmic-time search, while unsorted lists are limited to linear-time search.

Searching an unsorted list by scanning element-by-element takes Θ(n) time because, lacking any order, the algorithm may have to inspect every item to decide whether the target is present.  
A sorted list supports binary search, which repeatedly halves the remaining search interval and therefore finishes in Θ(log n) time on average and in the worst case.  
The speedup arises from the ordering property: knowing that all elements to the left of a midpoint are smaller (and all to the right larger) lets the algorithm safely discard half the candidates after each comparison, something impossible when no order exists.  
Thus, the difference in time complexity—Θ(log n) versus Θ(n)—is directly attributable to the extra information conveyed by sorted order, which reduces the uncertainty about an element’s location much more rapidly.

Searching a key in an unsorted list needs linear scan, giving Θ(n) time in the worst and average cases.  
A sorted list supports binary search (when random access is available), which discards half of the remaining candidates after each comparison, yielding Θ(log n) time.  
The order in the sorted list encodes information about relative positions, letting one comparison eliminate large portions of the data, while an unsorted list provides no such guidance.  
Thus, as n grows, linear search time increases proportionally, whereas binary search time rises only logarithmically.  
For example, doubling n doubles linear-search work but adds just one extra comparison to binary search.

Searching an unsorted list requires a linear scan, giving Θ(n) time in the worst and average cases.  
A sorted list permits binary search, which halves the remaining search interval each step and runs in Θ(log n) time.  
The speedup arises because ordering lets each comparison eliminate half of the remaining candidates, exponentially shrinking the search space.  
Without order, no element can be ruled out without direct inspection, so every item may need to be checked.  
Thus, the presence of sorted order is the key factor that reduces search complexity from linear to logarithmic time.

Searching an unsorted list requires examining each element sequentially, so the time complexity is Θ(n).  
A sorted list stored in a random-access structure (e.g., array) supports binary search, which repeatedly halves the remaining interval and finishes in Θ(log n) time.  
The improvement occurs because the order of elements lets each comparison eliminate half of the still-possible positions, whereas in an unsorted list a comparison provides no information about other elements, forcing inspection of all candidates.  
If the sorted data reside in a structure without random access (e.g., singly linked list), you still need Θ(n) time because you cannot jump to midpoints, underscoring that both sorting and efficient indexing are required for the logarithmic bound.  
Hence, with suitable structure, sorted lists enable logarithmic search, while unsorted lists (or sorted lists lacking random access) remain linear.

Searching an unsorted list requires scanning each element sequentially, giving a worst-case and average-case time of Θ(n).  
A sorted list stored in a random-access structure (array, vector) supports binary search, which halves the search space at every step and runs in Θ(log n) time.  
The speedup exists because element order lets each comparison discard half of the remaining candidates, whereas an unsorted list provides no information to rule out items without inspecting them individually.  
If the sorted list lacks efficient random access (e.g., a singly linked list), binary search cannot jump to the midpoint quickly, so its search time reverts to Θ(n).  
Thus, ordering plus fast indexable access reduces search complexity from linear to logarithmic.

Searching an unsorted list generally requires examining each element until the target is found or the list ends, leading to Θ(n) time in both average and worst cases.  
A sorted list allows use of binary search, repeatedly halving the search interval and reaching the answer (or concluding absence) in Θ(log n) time.  
The asymptotic improvement arises because order provides information: every comparison in a sorted list eliminates half of the remaining candidates, while in an unsorted list it eliminates only the single inspected element.  
Consequently, as n grows large, the ratio of required comparisons becomes roughly n / log n, giving the sorted list a dramatic speed advantage.  
Both methods use O(1) extra space when implemented iteratively.

Accessing the i-th element in an array takes Θ(1) time because the address of the element is computed directly by offsetting from the base address.  
Accessing the i-th element in a singly or doubly linked list takes Θ(i) time in the worst case (and Θ(n) on average for a random position) because you must follow pointers node-by-node from the head (or tail) until reaching the desired position.

Accessing the i-th element in an array takes O(1) time because arrays occupy contiguous memory, so the address of element i is computed directly as base_address + i × element_size.  
In a singly or doubly linked list, the nodes are scattered in memory and only linked by pointers, so reaching the i-th node requires sequentially following i pointers from the head (or from the tail in a doubly linked list), resulting in O(i) time.  
Thus, random access is constant-time in arrays but linear in the position index for linked lists.

In a typical array, the i-th element is reached by direct pointer arithmetic, giving Θ(1) (constant-time) access.  
In a singly or doubly linked list, elements are not stored contiguously; to reach the i-th node you must follow i links from the head (or n−i links from the tail in some doubly lists), so worst-case time is Θ(i) and Θ(n) when i≈n.  
Average random access in a linked list is Θ(n) versus Θ(1) in an array.  
Thus, arrays support true constant-time random access, while linked lists incur linear time proportional to the distance traversed.

Accessing element i in an array uses pointer arithmetic (base address + i × element size), so it takes Θ(1) time.  
In a linked list the nodes are not contiguous and you can only start from the head (or head/tail for a doubly list), so you must follow pointers one-by-one until you reach position i.  
That traversal costs Θ(i) time, which in the worst case (i ≈ n) is Θ(n).  
Hence, arrays provide constant-time random access, while linked lists incur linear-time positional access.

Accessing the i-th element of an array is O(1) because pointer arithmetic lets the CPU jump directly to the required memory address.  
Accessing the i-th element of a singly or doubly linked list is O(i) (worst-case O(n)) because the list must be traversed node-by-node from one end until the desired position is reached.

Accessing the i-th element in an array takes Θ(1) time because the address is computed directly by offsetting from the base pointer.  
Accessing the i-th element in a singly or doubly linked list takes Θ(i) time (Θ(n) in the worst case) because the list must be traversed node-by-node from the head (or tail) until the desired position is reached.

Accessing the i-th element of an array is Θ(1) because the element’s address is obtained by simple pointer arithmetic.  
Accessing the i-th element of a singly or doubly linked list is Θ(i) (Θ(n) for arbitrary random access) because you must sequentially follow i pointers from the head (or tail) to reach it.  
Thus, arrays provide constant-time random access, whereas linked lists incur linear time.

No.  
In the comparison model a sorting algorithm corresponds to a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are key comparisons and whose leaves represent the n! possible input permutations.  
A binary tree of height d has at most 2^d leaves, so to distinguish all permutations we need 2^d ≥ n!.  
Thus d ≥ log₂(n!) = Θ(n log n) by Stirling’s approximation.  
Each comparison adds one level, so any comparison-based sorter needs Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case (and in expectation for randomized algorithms).  
Consequently Θ(n log n) is both the lower and upper bound, and no comparison-based algorithm can asymptotically beat it.  
Linear-time methods like counting or radix sort succeed only because they exploit extra information about the keys and therefore are not purely comparison-based.

No.  
In the pure comparison model every algorithm’s execution can be represented as a binary decision tree whose leaves correspond to the n ! possible input permutations.  
A binary tree that distinguishes n ! cases must have height at least log₂(n !) ≈ n log₂n − Θ(n).  
Therefore any comparison-based sorting algorithm needs Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst (and even average) case.  
Since O(n log n) algorithms such as mergesort and heapsort match this lower bound, Θ(n log n) is optimal; no comparison algorithm can be asymptotically faster on arbitrary inputs.

No. In the comparison model, every deterministic or randomized sorting algorithm corresponds to a binary decision tree whose leaves represent the n! possible permutations of the input. A binary tree with L leaves must have height at least log₂L, so any comparison-based sort needs at least log₂(n!) comparisons in the worst case. Stirling’s approximation gives log₂(n!) = Θ(n log n). Hence the worst-case number of comparisons—and therefore the running time—of any comparison-based sorting algorithm is bounded below by Ω(n log n). Algorithms that appear faster (e.g., counting sort, radix sort) escape this bound only because they use information other than comparisons, such as numeric keys and extra memory, thereby operating outside the pure comparison model.

In the pure comparison model every algorithm corresponds to a decision tree whose leaves represent the n ! possible input orderings.  
A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, so we need 2^h ≥ n !, giving h ≥ log₂(n !) = Θ(n log n).  
Thus any comparison-based sorter must perform Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case.  
Algorithms like mergesort, heapsort, and introsort meet this bound, so Θ(n log n) is optimal.  
Faster runtimes (e.g., O(n) radix or counting sort) are achievable only when extra assumptions beyond comparisons—such as bounded integer keys—are allowed.

No.  In the comparison model every deterministic or randomized algorithm can be represented by a decision tree whose internal nodes are element-to-element comparisons and whose leaves correspond to the n! possible permutations of the input.  A binary decision tree with L leaves must have height at least ⌈log₂ L⌉.  Because any correct sorter needs at least one leaf for each permutation, L ≥ n!, so its height (and thus the worst-case number of comparisons) is at least log₂(n!) = Θ(n log n).  Therefore every comparison-based sort requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case, matching the O(n log n) achievable by mergesort and heapsort.  Faster asymptotic running times are only possible when the algorithm can exploit information beyond pairwise comparisons (e.g., counting sort, radix sort), but within the pure comparison model O(n log n) is optimal.

In the comparison model a correct sorter must distinguish all n! possible input permutations, which means its execution traces form a binary decision tree with at least n! leaves.  
A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, so 2^h ≥ n! and therefore h ≥ log₂(n!) = Θ(n log n).  
Thus any comparison-based algorithm needs Ω(n log n) comparisons—and therefore Ω(n log n) time—in the worst case (and in expectation for randomized algorithms).  
Faster bounds are achievable only by using non-comparison information (e.g., counting or radix sort), which lies outside the comparison model.

No. In a pure comparison model each sorting algorithm corresponds to a binary decision tree whose leaves represent the n ! possible permutations of the input. A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, so 2^h ≥ n !. Hence h ≥ log₂(n !) = Ω(n log n) by Stirling’s approximation, and the algorithm needs at least that many comparisons in the worst case. Because time is at least proportional to the number of comparisons, every comparison-based sort has a worst-case running time of Ω(n log n). Thus O(n log n) is asymptotically optimal in this model; beating it would violate the decision-tree lower bound. (Faster sorts like counting or radix sort evade the bound by using operations other than comparisons and therefore fall outside the comparison model.)

Huffman coding is optimal only within the class of fixed-to-variable, instantaneous (prefix-free) codes for independently and identically distributed source symbols with known probabilities.  
Huffman’s optimality in that class follows by induction: merging the two least-probable symbols and assigning them equal longest codewords reduces the problem to a smaller one whose optimal solution can be extended without increasing average length, so the resulting tree has minimum expected codeword length among all prefix codes.  
Outside this restricted class it is not optimal.  
Arithmetic coding, for example, produces an average length that can be made arbitrarily close to the source entropy H, whereas a Huffman code can be as high as H+1 bits.  
Take a binary source with P(0)=0.9 and P(1)=0.1: Huffman must assign the 1-bit codewords “0” and “1”, giving an average length of 1 bit, while arithmetic coding approaches the entropy 0.799 bits per symbol, compressing a further 20%.  
Therefore Huffman coding is provably optimal for prefix codes but not universally optimal among all compression schemes.

Huffman coding is optimal only within the class of instantaneous (prefix-free) variable-length codes.  
The two least-probable symbols must be siblings at maximum depth in any minimum-expected-length prefix tree; merging them into a single “super-symbol” reduces the problem size, and an induction on alphabet size shows that the Huffman procedure, which repeats this merge recursively, yields the unique tree with the minimum weighted path length L, so no other prefix code can achieve a smaller L.  
Thus for a memoryless source with entropy H(X) one has H(X) ≤ L < H(X)+1, and Huffman attains the minimum possible L under the prefix-code constraint.  
However, more general schemes (e.g., arithmetic or range coding, or block codes over long symbol sequences) can make the average length approach H(X) arbitrarily closely and therefore beat Huffman’s +1-bit gap.  
Hence Huffman coding is provably optimal for prefix codes but is not universally optimal among all lossless compression algorithms.

Huffman coding is optimal for the specific problem of assigning fixed-length, binary, prefix (instantaneous) codewords to the symbols of a discrete memory-less source with known probabilities. Any optimal prefix code possesses the sibling property: two least-probable symbols are siblings of equal maximal length. Merging these siblings into a single composite symbol of probability p_c = p_a + p_b produces a smaller, equivalent coding problem; conversely, splitting an optimal (n−1)-symbol solution at p_c by appending 0/1 recreates an optimal n-symbol solution. Huffman’s algorithm performs this merge-and-split recursively, so by induction the code it outputs has minimum expected length L_H among all prefix codes, satisfying ⌈H(P)⌉ ≤ L_H < H(P)+1. Hence no other prefix code can compress the same single symbols to fewer bits on average. Larger gains are possible only by abandoning the constraints (e.g., using block or arithmetic coding), so Huffman is “optimal” solely within its defined class, not among all conceivable compression techniques.

Huffman coding is optimal among all instantaneous (prefix‐free) fixed‐to‐variable length codes for a memoryless source with known symbol probabilities, meaning it yields the minimum achievable expected codeword length L subject to the Kraft–McMillan inequality.  
Proof sketch: 1) If a code is optimal, its two least‐probable symbols must have the two longest codewords and those codewords must differ only in the final bit; otherwise one can shorten them and reduce L, contradicting optimality. 2) Huffman’s algorithm repeatedly merges the two least‐probable symbols, assigns them complementary final bits, and replaces them with a single symbol whose probability is their sum, building the tree bottom-up. 3) By induction on the alphabet size, this merging step preserves optimality because any optimal code for the reduced source can be expanded to an optimal code for the original source, and conversely Huffman’s construction on the reduced source extends to a code with the same L. Therefore Huffman produces a code whose expected length equals the theoretical minimum for any prefix code.  
However, because it encodes symbols one at a time and restricts itself to integer bit lengths, it is not globally optimal compared with schemes like arithmetic coding that allow fractional-bit averages or exploit intersymbol dependencies.

Huffman coding is optimal among all instantaneous (prefix-free) codes that assign one codeword to each input symbol.  
Any optimal prefix tree must place the two least-probable symbols as siblings at the maximum depth; otherwise swapping them downward would decrease the average length, contradicting optimality.  
Huffman’s algorithm repeatedly merges precisely those two symbols into a composite symbol whose probability is their sum, recurses on the reduced set, and finally appends one extra bit to separate the pair—exactly mirroring the structural property just stated.  
Inductively, if the tree built for the reduced alphabet is optimal, the expanded tree for the original alphabet is also optimal, because every optimal tree for the full set corresponds to an optimal tree for the reduced set and their average lengths differ by the unavoidable cost of the two merged symbols.  
Thus Huffman always achieves the minimum possible expected codeword length L = ∑p_i l_i for that symbol set, and no other prefix-free, symbol-by-symbol code can do better.  
Note, however, that if fractional-bit codes (arithmetic coding) or long block codes are allowed, still greater compression can be achieved, so Huffman’s optimality is confined to the stated model.

Huffman coding is optimal only within the class of instantaneous (prefix) codes that assign an integral number of bits to each source symbol whose probabilities are known.  
In any optimal prefix code the two least-probable symbols must appear as siblings at the maximum depth; otherwise exchanging one of them with a deeper symbol strictly lowers the average length, contradicting optimality.  
Huffman’s algorithm builds a tree that satisfies this “sibling property” at every step by repeatedly merging the two least-probable nodes into a composite node whose probability is their sum.  
After one merge the problem reduces to finding an optimal code for a smaller alphabet; if the tree produced for this reduced problem is optimal (induction hypothesis), expanding the merged node back into its two children gives an optimal tree for the original alphabet.  
The base case of two symbols is trivially optimal, so by induction the tree returned by Huffman’s algorithm minimizes the expected code-word length L among all prefix codes.  
Consequently H(X) ≤ L < H(X)+1, where H(X) is the entropy of the source.  
Because Huffman is limited to integral lengths, fractional-bit methods such as arithmetic coding can achieve expected lengths arbitrarily close to H(X); therefore Huffman is not globally optimal among all lossless compression algorithms, only among prefix codes.

Huffman coding minimizes the expected code-word length for any single-symbol, prefix-free code: in every optimal prefix tree the two least-probable symbols can occupy the deepest, equal-length sibling leaves; merging them into one pseudo-symbol reduces the task to a smaller, structurally identical problem, and recursive application of this merge rule is precisely Huffman’s algorithm, proving by induction that no other prefix code yields a shorter average length.  Consequently, for a source with probabilities p₁,…,pₙ its Huffman length L_Huff attains the infimum over all instantaneous codes, satisfying H(X) ≤ L_Huff < H(X)+1.  Yet schemes that relax those constraints—e.g., arithmetic coding or long-block Huffman coding—can drive the average length arbitrarily close to the entropy.  Thus Huffman coding is optimal within the class of fixed, symbol-by-symbol, prefix-free codes, but not universally optimal for all data-compression settings.

In the worst case—such as when the input array is in reverse order—Bubble Sort performs (n-1) + (n-2) + … + 1 ≈ n(n-1)/2 comparisons and about the same number of swaps. Therefore its worst-case running time is Θ(n²), and its auxiliary space remains O(1).

In the worst case (e.g., the input list is in reverse order), Bubble Sort performs (n − 1) passes and makes ∑_{i=1}^{n−1} i = n(n−1)/2 comparisons and the same order of swaps, giving a time complexity of Θ(n²). Auxiliary space remains Θ(1).

In the worst case—when the input list is in reverse order—Bubble Sort performs (n − 1) passes.  
On the i-th pass it compares adjacent pairs through the unsorted portion of length n − i, producing (n − i) comparisons and, typically, the same order of swaps.  
The total number of comparisons is therefore Σ_{i=1}^{n−1} (n − i) = n(n − 1)/2, which grows proportionally to n².  
Similarly, the total number of swaps is Θ(n²) in this scenario.  
Thus, Bubble Sort’s worst-case time complexity is Θ(n²) for both comparisons and swaps, with no additional factors altering the asymptotic bound.

In Bubble Sort’s worst-case scenario—when the input array is in reverse order—every pair of adjacent elements must be compared and swapped on each pass. For an array of n elements, the first pass performs (n − 1) comparisons, the second (n − 2), and so on, giving the sum (n − 1) + (n − 2) + ⋯ + 1 = n(n − 1)/2 comparisons. This summation grows proportionally to n²/2, so the comparisons and swaps are Θ(n²). Therefore, the worst-case time complexity of Bubble Sort is O(n²).

In the worst case (e.g., reverse-sorted input), Bubble Sort makes n−1 passes.  
On pass i (counting from 0), it performs n−1−i comparisons.  
Total comparisons = Σ_{i=0}^{n−2} (n−1−i) = n(n−1)/2 = Θ(n²).  
Whenever a comparison detects an inversion, a swap is executed, so swaps are also Θ(n²).  
Therefore, the worst-case running time of Bubble Sort is Θ(n²) and its auxiliary space usage remains Θ(1).

Bubble Sort makes (n-1) + (n-2) + … + 1 = n(n−1)/2 = Θ(n²) comparisons in the worst case.  
For a completely reversed array, each comparison results in a swap, giving Θ(n²) swaps as well.  
Hence the worst-case running time is Θ(n²) (i.e., O(n²)).  
Its space usage remains O(1) auxiliary memory.

In the worst case (e.g., when the array is in reverse order), Bubble Sort performs (n − 1) passes, and on each pass it compares adjacent elements across the unsorted portion. The total number of comparisons (and swaps) sums to 1 + 2 + … + (n − 1) = n(n − 1)/2. Because this quadratic term dominates, the worst-case time complexity is Θ(n²). The algorithm uses only a constant amount of extra space, so its space complexity is Θ(1).

Chaining stores all keys that hash to the same index in an auxiliary data structure (usually a linked list or dynamic array) attached to that index, so each table slot can hold multiple elements.  
Open addressing keeps all keys inside the hash table array itself; on a collision it probes for another empty slot following a probe sequence (e.g., linear, quadratic, or double hashing).  
With chaining, table size affects the number of buckets but not the maximum number of elements; load factor can exceed 1, and performance depends on the average chain length.  
With open addressing, the table must always have empty slots; load factor must stay below 1 to keep probes short, and performance degrades sharply as the table fills.  
Memory usage differs: chaining needs extra pointers for separate lists but can grow beyond the array, whereas open addressing uses a fixed-size array with no per-element pointer overhead.  
Deletion is simple in chaining (remove from the list) but more complex in open addressing, often requiring special tombstone markers to preserve probe sequences.  
Cache performance usually favors open addressing because data stay in a contiguous array, while chaining may suffer from pointer chasing.  
Chaining tolerates poor hash functions better since collisions only lengthen lists, whereas open addressing relies heavily on uniform hashing to avoid long probe runs.

Chaining stores all keys that hash to the same slot in an auxiliary data structure (usually a linked list) located at that slot, so each table entry holds a collection of items.  
Open addressing keeps exactly one key–value pair per table position; when a collision occurs it probes other slots according to a scheme (e.g., linear, quadratic, or double hashing) until it finds an empty one.  
Chaining’s load factor can exceed 1 because overflow items live in lists, while open addressing requires the table to maintain a load factor below 1 for efficient probing.  
Insertion and deletion in chaining operate primarily on list operations and are unaffected by clustering, whereas in open addressing they can degrade as the table fills and primary/secondary clustering appears.  
Memory for chaining involves pointers for lists but can expand dynamically; open addressing uses a single array with no extra pointers, giving better cache locality but fixed capacity until resized.  
Rehashing in chaining only involves moving items when the table itself grows, but open addressing usually necessitates relocating many existing keys during a resize.  
Consequently, chaining handles highly skewed or unpredictable data distributions more gracefully, while open addressing offers faster average-case lookups on well-sized tables and lower per-key overhead.

Chaining stores all keys that hash to the same index in an auxiliary data structure (commonly a linked list or dynamic array) attached to that slot, so each table entry holds a reference to a collection of items.  
Open addressing keeps every key directly inside the main array: if a collision occurs, the algorithm probes other positions (linear, quadratic, or double-hash) until it finds an empty cell.  
Chaining’s table can stay sparsely filled—load factor can exceed 1—because extra nodes live outside the array, while open addressing usually limits the load factor below 1 to maintain short probe sequences.  
Insertion and deletion with chaining are simple O(1) operations on a list, but open addressing may require multiple probes, and deletions need special “tombstone” markers to preserve probe chains.  
Memory locality favors open addressing (everything in one array) and can yield faster cache performance, whereas chaining incurs pointer overhead and possible cache misses.  
Chaining can gracefully handle varied key distributions and is less sensitive to clustering; open addressing is vulnerable to primary and secondary clustering but uses no extra per-element pointers.  
Resizing a chained table typically reallocates only the bucket array, leaving node lists intact, while open addressing requires rehashing every key into a new array.  
Overall, chaining trades extra memory and pointer traversals for simpler collision handling, whereas open addressing trades stricter load constraints and more complex probing logic for compact, cache-friendly storage.

Chaining keeps each hash-table slot as a pointer to a linked structure (list, tree, etc.); all keys that hash to the same index are stored in that external structure.  
Open addressing stores every key directly inside the table array itself; when a collision occurs it probes other slots (linear, quadratic, or double hashing) until it finds an empty one.  
Because chaining’s overflow nodes live outside the main array, the table can hold more items than its immediate slot count, while open addressing is limited by the array size.  
Chaining’s average lookup time remains O(1 + α) where α = load factor (items/slots), but performance degrades gracefully and rehashing is optional; open addressing requires α < 1 and suffers steep slowdown as α approaches 1.  
In chaining, deletions are simple list removals; in open addressing they need special “deleted” markers or table reorganization to preserve probe sequences.  
Chaining allows heterogeneous record sizes and is cache-unfriendly; open addressing uses contiguous memory, giving better cache locality but demanding a good probe scheme to avoid clustering.  
Memory overhead in chaining comes from pointers in each node; open addressing has no per-element pointer cost but wastes some slots to keep the load factor low.

Chaining resolves collisions by storing each bucket’s multiple keys in an auxiliary structure (usually a linked list or dynamic array), while open addressing keeps every key inside the main table and probes for another empty slot when a collision occurs.  
Chaining’s buckets can grow arbitrarily, so its average-time look-ups stay Θ(1) as long as the load factor α = n/m is moderate, and it continues to work even when α > 1; open addressing requires α < 1 and its probe lengths grow quickly as α approaches 1.  
Chaining needs extra memory for pointers/nodes but allows very simple insertion and true Θ(1) deletion.  
Open addressing uses only the array, giving better cache locality and usually less space overhead, yet deletion must mark “tombstones” or trigger costly reorganization.  
Rehashing a chained table usually just replaces the bucket array and relinks existing lists, whereas open addressing must reinsert every stored key.  
Well-chosen probing schemes (linear, quadratic, double hashing, etc.) are critical to open addressing’s performance; chaining’s performance depends mainly on hash-function quality.  
Therefore, chaining favors simplicity and robustness under high load, while open addressing offers tighter memory use and faster cache-friendly access when the table is kept sparsely filled.

Chaining stores, at each table index, a secondary container (e.g., a linked list) holding every key that hashes to that slot, so a collision simply grows the list.  
Open addressing keeps every key inside the main array; when a collision occurs it probes other positions (linear, quadratic, double hashing, etc.) until it finds an empty slot.  
Chaining uses extra memory for list nodes but can operate with a load factor > 1, whereas open addressing is memory-compact yet needs a load factor < 1 for speed.  
Expected successful search cost is O(1 + λ) for chaining (λ = keys/buckets) and about O(1/(1 − α)) for open addressing (α = keys/slots), so open addressing slows sharply as the table fills.  
Deletion is simple in chaining (remove from the list) but tricky in open addressing, which often requires “tombstone” markers to preserve probe sequences.  
Chaining is more tolerant of poor hash functions because collisions stay localized; open addressing is sensitive to clustering effects.  
Thus, chaining resolves collisions with external per-bucket storage, while open addressing resolves them by systematic probing inside a single contiguous array.

Chaining stores all elements that hash to the same slot in an auxiliary data structure (typically a linked list, tree, or dynamic array) located at that slot, so each table entry holds a pointer to a collection of keys.  
Open addressing keeps all elements inside the table itself; if a collision occurs, a probe sequence (linear, quadratic, double hashing, etc.) is followed to find another empty slot.  
In chaining, the table can accommodate more elements than its initial size because chains can grow arbitrarily, while open addressing is limited by the fixed number of table slots.  
Average-case search time for both methods is O(1) when the load factor is low, but with high load factors chaining degrades to O(α) and open addressing to O(1/(1-α)) probes, where α is load factor.  
Deletion in chaining is straightforward—remove the node from the chain—whereas in open addressing it often requires special markers (“deleted” flags) to preserve probe sequences.  
Chaining tolerates all load factors but uses extra memory for pointers; open addressing uses a single contiguous array, improving cache performance and reducing memory overhead.

Fibonacci heaps support insert, find-min, decrease-key, and meld in O(1) amortized time and extract-min in O(log n), whereas a binary heap needs O(log n) for insert, decrease-key, meld, and extract-min. This superior amortized performance, especially for decrease-key and meld, makes Fibonacci heaps asymptotically faster for graph algorithms that call these operations frequently (e.g., Dijkstra’s or Prim’s), reducing their running time from O(E log V) to O(E + V log V).

Fibonacci heaps offer asymptotically faster amortized operations than binary heaps: insert, decrease-key, and meld take O(1) amortized time (versus O(log n) for binary heaps), while extract-min remains O(log n). This accelerates graph algorithms such as Dijkstra’s shortest-path and Prim’s minimum-spanning tree, whose runtimes drop from O((V+E) log V) with binary heaps to O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heaps because of the cheaper decrease-key.

Fibonacci heaps offer asymptotically faster amortized bounds than binary heaps for key operations: insert, decrease-key, and meld (union) are O(1) amortized rather than O(log n), and delete-min is O(log n) amortized. These lower costs make algorithms that rely heavily on decrease-key and meld—such as Dijkstra’s shortest-path and Prim’s minimum-spanning-tree—run faster in theory when implemented with Fibonacci heaps.

Fibonacci heaps give asymptotically faster amortized operations: insert, find-min, meld, and decrease-key run in O(1) amortized time and delete-min in O(log n), while a binary heap needs O(log n) for insert, decrease-key, and delete-min and Θ(n) for meld. This makes algorithms that rely heavily on decrease-key and meld (e.g., Dijkstra’s shortest-path or Prim’s MST) run faster with Fibonacci heaps, achieving overall O(E + V log V) instead of O(E log V).

Fibonacci heaps give O(1) amortized time for insert, decrease-key, and meld (union) operations, while maintaining O(log n) amortized time for delete-min; binary heaps need Θ(log n) time for insert and decrease-key and offer no efficient meld. This superior performance, especially on decrease-key, makes Fibonacci heaps asymptotically faster for algorithms such as Dijkstra’s and Prim’s that perform many key decreases.

Fibonacci heaps keep the heap‐order property while giving O(1) amortized time for insert, decrease-key, and meld (union) operations, whereas binary heaps take Θ(log n) time for each of those. This faster decrease-key/meld reduces the overall complexity of graph algorithms such as Dijkstra’s or Prim’s from Θ(E log V) with binary heaps to Θ(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heaps, providing a theoretical speed-up when many key decreases or heap merges are needed.

Fibonacci heaps support insert, decrease-key, and meld (union) in O(1) amortized time, whereas a binary heap needs O(log n) for each of those operations; both give O(log n) for extract-min. Consequently, algorithms that perform many decrease-key or meld operations—such as Dijkstra’s single-source shortest path or Prim’s minimum-spanning tree—run faster when implemented with Fibonacci heaps.

Insert the new key as in a binary-search tree and, while retracing the path to the root, update each visited node’s balance factor (bf = height(left) − height(right)).  
The first node z encountered with |bf(z)| = 2 is the only one that can be out of balance, because all nodes above it had |bf| ≤ 1 before the insertion and their heights have increased by at most 1.  
Let y be the child of z on the taller side and x the child of y on the taller side; the triple ⟨z,y,x⟩ is restructured by a single (LL or RR) or a double (LR or RL) rotation chosen according to the signs of bf(z) and bf(y).  
In every case the rotation gives new balance factors bf′(z), bf′(y), bf′(x) ∈ {−1,0,1}.  
The height of the subtree rooted at the new local root (y or x) becomes exactly the height that the subtree rooted at z had before the insertion, so ancestors of z regain the same heights and balance factors they had originally.  
Therefore no node above z becomes unbalanced, and all nodes below z were explicitly fixed by the rotation.  
Consequently, after a single localized rotation (single or double), every node in the tree again satisfies |bf| ≤ 1, so the AVL tree remains balanced after each insertion, with overall height Θ(log n).

Assume the AVL invariant (balance factor −1, 0, 1 at every node) holds before an insertion.  
The new key is first added as a leaf, so only the nodes on the search path from that leaf to the root can have their heights altered.  
Walking back up this path, let z be the first node whose balance factor becomes ±2; nodes above z are still balanced.  
Exactly four structural cases arise for z (LL, RR, LR, RL) depending on whether the insertion occurred in the left/right subtree and its sub-direction.  
A single rotation (LL or RR) or a double rotation (LR or RL) is applied at z, after which z and its child(ren) attain balance factors 0 or ±1 and the height of the subtree rooted at the new top of the rotated section equals the height of z before the insertion.  
Because that height is unchanged, no ancestor of z experiences any further height difference, so all ancestors remain balanced.  
Thus after at most one (single or double) rotation every node in the tree again satisfies the balance-factor bound.  
By induction on the number of insertions, an AVL tree remains balanced after each insertion while preserving O(log n) height.

Insert the key as in an ordinary BST, then walk back toward the root updating each node’s balance factor (height(left)−height(right)).  
Let z be the first node whose balance factor becomes ±2; its two children are y (the higher child) and x (the higher child of y).  
Because z was previously balanced (|balance|≤1), the height of the subtree rooted at z has just increased by 1, so only z can violate the AVL bound.  
Exactly four relative shapes for (z,y,x) exist (LL, LR, RR, RL); performing the corresponding single or double rotation makes x the new root of that subtree.  
For every case the rotation sets the balance factors of x, y, and z to −1, 0, or 1 and restores the subtree’s height to what it was before the insertion.  
Consequently the height of the subtree rooted at x is the same as the old height of z, so no ancestor above z can now become unbalanced.  
Thus a single rotation (or double rotation counted as one restructuring) along the insertion path suffices to restore the AVL property.  
Because each insertion causes at most one such restructuring and the height of an AVL tree is O(log n), the tree remains balanced after every insertion.

1. An empty or single-node AVL tree is balanced.  
2. Inserting a key proceeds as in a BST, so only nodes on the insertion path can change height, and each of those heights can increase by at most 1.  
3. Traversing that path upward, compute each node’s balance factor bf = h(right) − h(left).  
4. If |bf|≤1 the node is still balanced; if |bf|=2, one of four cases (LL, RR, LR, RL) uniquely applies.  
5. The chosen single or double rotation rearranges ≤3 nodes and sets their balance factors to 0 or ±1.  
6. After the rotation the height of the subtree rooted at the adjusted node is at most what it was before insertion, so ancestors higher up cannot become newly unbalanced.  
7. Therefore exactly one rotation on the first node with |bf|=2 suffices to restore balance on the entire path to the root.  
8. All other nodes were unchanged, so every node in the tree now satisfies |bf|≤1.  
9. By induction on the number of insertions, the AVL balance property is preserved after every insertion.

During an insertion, only nodes on the path from the new leaf to the root can change height.  
Let α be the first ancestor on that path whose balance factor becomes ±2.  
Depending on whether the insertion occurred in α’s left-left, left-right, right-right, or right-left grandchild, α matches one of four patterns: LL, LR, RR, or RL.  
An LL or RR pattern is fixed by a single rotation; an LR or RL pattern is fixed by a double rotation.  
After the rotation, α’s balance factor becomes 0 and its children’s factors are each in {−1,0,1}.  
The height of the rotated subtree equals α’s previous height before insertion, so all ancestors above α retain their former balance factors.  
Consequently, no node in the tree has a balance factor whose absolute value exceeds 1.  
Therefore, the AVL balance condition is preserved after every insertion.

Climbing from the new leaf toward the root, we update heights until the first ancestor z whose two children now differ in height by 2 is encountered; all nodes above z were already balanced and their height difference is still ≤1.  
Let y be z’s taller child and x be y’s taller (or newly inserted) child; the four possible configurations (LL, LR, RL, RR) are repaired by a single rotation (LL, RR) or a double rotation (LR, RL).  
After the rotation the two subtrees of z differ in height by at most 1, because the longest path in the higher subtree is shortened by one while the shorter side is unaffected.  
Nodes inside the rotated subtree are reordered but their own left and right subtrees keep the same heights, so they remain balanced, and nodes outside the subtree are untouched.  
If the rotation leaves z’s height unchanged, no higher ancestor can become unbalanced; if it decreases by 1, the same argument can be applied while moving upward, requiring at most one (possibly double) rotation per ancestor.  
Since the height of an AVL tree before insertion is O(log n), at most O(log n) ancestors are examined and rebalanced, so the process terminates quickly.  
Consequently, after every insertion all nodes again satisfy |height(left) − height(right)| ≤ 1, and the AVL balance property is preserved.

Let the AVL property (|balance factor| ≤ 1 for every node) hold before an insertion.  
The insertion path is unique, so only the balance factors along that path can change, each by at most ±1.  
Walking back up the path, let z be the first node whose balance factor becomes ±2; all ancestors above z are still balanced.  
Let y be z’s child on the taller side and x be y’s child on the side where the new key was inserted.  
A single rotation (LL or RR) or a double rotation (LR or RL) on the triple (z, y, x) is then applied.  
After that rotation the heights of the subtrees rooted at z, y, x are exactly those that z, y, x had before the insertion, so each now has balance factor −1, 0 or 1.  
Because the height of the subtree rooted at z is unchanged, the balance factors of all ancestors of z remain within ±1 as they were before.  
Thus, after at most one restructuring involving one or two rotations, the AVL property is fully restored, so the tree is balanced after every insertion.

Let h be the height of the tree and bh(x) the black-height (number of black nodes on any path from x to a leaf).  
Because a red node cannot have a red child, at most one red node can appear between any two black nodes, so every root-to-leaf path has length h ≤ 2·bh(root).  
Inductively, a subtree whose root has black-height k contains at least 2ᵏ − 1 internal nodes: when k = 0 it is empty, and for k > 0 each child has black-height ≥ k − 1 and thus ≥ 2^{k−1} − 1 nodes, yielding (2^{k−1} − 1) + (2^{k−1} − 1) + 1 = 2ᵏ − 1 in total.  
Hence a red–black tree with n nodes satisfies n ≥ 2^{bh(root)} − 1, so bh(root) ≤ ⌊log₂(n + 1)⌋.  
Combining the two inequalities gives h ≤ 2·⌊log₂(n + 1)⌋.  
Therefore the height of a red–black tree is O(log n).

Let n be the number of internal nodes and h the height.  
If B is the black-height of the root, every root-to-leaf path has exactly B black nodes, and because two red nodes cannot be adjacent, at most one red node can lie between consecutive black nodes; hence h ≤ 2B.  
Inductively, a subtree whose root has black-height b contains at least 2ᵇ − 1 internal nodes: when b = 0 the subtree is a leaf (1 node), and otherwise the root has two children each with black-height b or b−1, giving at least (2^{b−1} − 1) + (2^{b−1} − 1) + 1 = 2ᵇ − 1 nodes.  
Applying this to the whole tree yields n ≥ 2ᴮ − 1, so B ≤ ⌊log₂(n+1)⌋.  
Therefore h ≤ 2B ≤ 2 log₂(n+1) = O(log n), proving that every red–black tree has logarithmic height.

Let h be the height of a red–black tree with n internal nodes, and let bh be the black-height (number of black nodes on any root-to-leaf path).  
1. Because no path can contain two consecutive red nodes, at least half of the h nodes on any longest path are black, so bh ≥ ⌈h⁄2⌉.  
2. The smallest tree with black-height bh occurs when every black node has two red children and all red nodes have only black children; this yields the recurrence N(bh) = 1 (root) + 2 N(bh–1), giving N(bh) = 2^{bh} – 1.  
3. Hence n ≥ 2^{bh} – 1.  
4. Substituting bh ≥ h⁄2, we obtain n ≥ 2^{h⁄2} – 1.  
5. Rearranging, h ≤ 2 log₂(n + 1).  
Therefore the height h is O(log n).

Let h be the height of a red–black tree with n internal (key-holding) nodes, and let bh be the black-height, i.e., the number of black nodes on any root-to-leaf path.  
1. Because no red node can have a red parent, at least every second node on any path is black, so h ≤ 2·bh.  
2. We prove by induction that any subtree whose root has black-height bh contains at least 2^{bh} − 1 internal nodes.  
3. Base case: bh = 0 contributes 0 internal nodes (only NIL leaves).  
4. Inductive step: for bh > 0 the root is black and each child has black-height bh − 1; hence each child subtree has at least 2^{bh−1} − 1 internal nodes, giving a total of 1 + 2(2^{bh−1} − 1) = 2^{bh} − 1 nodes.  
5. Therefore n ≥ 2^{bh} − 1, implying bh ≤ ⌊log₂(n + 1)⌋.  
6. Combining this with h ≤ 2·bh yields h ≤ 2·log₂(n + 1) = O(log n).  
Thus the height of every red–black tree is bounded above by a constant multiple of log n, proving h = O(log n).

Let bh(x) be the number of black nodes (including x if black) on every root-to-leaf path in the subtree rooted at x; by property 5 of red–black trees all such paths have the same bh(x).  
Claim 1: A subtree whose root has black-height k contains at least 2ᵏ − 1 internal nodes.  
Proof by induction on k: k = 0 (leaf) gives 0 = 2⁰ − 1 nodes.  For k > 0, each child of the root has black-height ≥ k − 1, so by induction each child has ≥ 2^(k-1) − 1 nodes; thus the subtree has ≥ 1 + 2(2^(k-1) − 1) = 2ᵏ − 1 nodes.  

Therefore, for the whole tree with n internal nodes and black-height B, n ≥ 2ᴮ − 1, hence B ≤ log₂(n + 1).  
Claim 2: The actual height h of any path from the root is at most 2B, because red nodes cannot be adjacent (property 4), so between any two black nodes there can be at most one red node.  

Combining, h ≤ 2B ≤ 2 log₂(n + 1) = O(log n). Thus the height of a red–black tree grows logarithmically with the number of stored keys.

Let h be the height of a red–black tree with n internal (non-NIL) nodes and let bh(v) denote the black-height of a node v.  
1. Because no red node has a red child, at least every other node on any root-to-leaf path is black, hence h ≤ 2·bh(root).  
2. Show by induction that a subtree whose root has black-height k contains at least 2^k − 1 internal nodes:  
   • Base k = 0: only the NIL leaf, giving 0 nodes.  
   • Inductive step: both children of a root with bh = k have black-height ≥ k − 1, so each child’s subtree has ≥ 2^{k−1} − 1 nodes; adding the root gives ≥ (2^{k−1} − 1)·2 + 1 = 2^k − 1 nodes.  
3. Therefore n ≥ 2^{bh(root)} − 1, so bh(root) ≤ log₂(n + 1).  
4. Combining 1 and 3 yields h ≤ 2·log₂(n + 1) = O(log n).

Let bh(v) be the number of black nodes on any path from v to a leaf (well-defined by property 5).  
Because a red node cannot have a red child, a longest root-to-leaf path can alternate red and black nodes, so its length satisfies h(v) ≤ 2 · bh(v).  
Inductively one shows that a subtree whose root has black-height b contains at least 2ᵇ − 1 internal nodes.  
Thus for the whole tree with n internal nodes and black-height B, n ≥ 2ᴮ − 1 and therefore B ≤ log₂(n + 1).  
Combining these inequalities gives h(root) ≤ 2 log₂(n + 1).  
Therefore the height of a red–black tree with n keys is O(log n).

A red–black tree enforces five structural rules:  
1. Every node is colored either red or black.  
2. The root node is always black.  
3. Every external leaf (the NIL or null child used to terminate paths) is considered black.  
4. No red node may have a red parent or red child; red nodes can only have black parents and black children.  
5. For every node, every simple path from that node down to a descendant NIL leaf contains the same number of black nodes, called the node’s black-height.  

Together these invariants bound the tree’s height to at most 2 log₂(n + 1), ensuring all basic operations run in O(log n) time.

A red–black tree upholds five invariants:  
1) Every node is colored either red or black.  
2) The root node is always black.  
3) All leaves (the NIL/sentinel nodes) are black.  
4) No red node may have a red parent or red child (i.e., red nodes cannot be adjacent).  
5) For any node, every simple path to a descendant leaf contains the same number of black nodes, called its black-height.  

These constraints keep the height of the tree O(log n), ensuring efficient search, insertion, and deletion.

A red–black tree enforces five invariants:  
1) Every node is colored either red or black.  
2) The root node is always black.  
3) Every null (NIL/leaf) pointer is treated as a black node.  
4) A red node may not have a red parent or red child, so red nodes are never adjacent on a path.  
5) For any node, every path to a descendant NIL contains exactly the same number of black nodes (its black-height).  
Together, these rules bound the longest root-to-leaf path to at most twice the length of the shortest, giving the tree O(log n) height and guaranteeing logarithmic search, insertion, and deletion times.

3. All leaves (the NIL sentinel nodes) are black.
