Red–black trees maintain five fundamental properties:  
1. Every node is colored either red or black.  
2. The root is always black.  
3. All external (NIL) leaf nodes are considered black.  
4. No red node can have a red parent or red child, preventing two consecutive reds on any path.  
5. For every node, every simple path from that node to any descendant NIL leaf contains the same number of black nodes, called the node’s black-height.  
Together, these invariants bound the tree’s height to at most 2 log₂(n + 1), guaranteeing O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion times.

A red–black tree enforces five invariants:  
1) Every node is colored either red or black.  
2) The root is always black.

Red–black trees enforce five invariants:  
1. Every node is colored either red or black.  
2. The root is always black.  
3. All external NIL leaves are treated as black sentinel nodes.  
4. If a node is red, both of its children must be black, so no two red nodes appear consecutively on any path.  
5. For any node, every path from that node down to any descendant NIL leaf contains the same number of black nodes (the black-height).

AVL trees enforce a height balance factor of –1…1, yielding height ≤ 1.44 log₂(n + 2); red–black trees allow up to twice that height (≤ 2 log₂(n + 1)).  
Because of the tighter height, AVL searches usually require about 25 % fewer comparisons than red–black searches.  
AVL insertions need a retrace to update balance factors and up to 2 rotations; deletions can trigger O(log n) rotations, so updates cost more than in red–black trees.  
Red–black insertions or deletions need at most 2 rotations plus O(1) recolorings, giving smaller, predictable update overhead.  
Both structures guarantee O(log n) worst-case time, but AVL favors read-heavy workloads, while red–black trees suit write-heavy or real-time workloads that value bounded update cost.  
Each AVL node stores a few height/balance bits; each red–black node stores one color bit, so memory footprints are comparable.  
In practice, ordered library containers and OS kernels typically choose red–black trees for their cheap updates, while databases or search-dominated systems may prefer AVL trees for faster lookups.

AVL trees keep each node’s left-right height difference to −1, 0, or +1, so their height is ≤1.44 log₂(n+2); red–black trees only require that no path has more than twice as many nodes as any other, giving height ≤2 log₂(n+1).  
Because of this tighter height bound, AVL trees generally offer faster look-ups and in-order traversals.  
Maintaining that strict balance, however, costs more: an AVL insertion may trigger up to 2 rotations and a deletion can cascade O(log n) rotations, plus frequent height-factor updates.  
Red–black trees need at most 2 rotations on insert and 3 on delete, with only color flips otherwise, so updates are cheaper and more predictable.  
Consequently, workloads dominated by searches favor AVL trees, while update-heavy or mixed workloads favor red–black trees.  
Both structures guarantee O(log n) worst-case operations, use one extra bit of metadata per node (balance factor vs. color), and have comparable space overhead.

Both are height-balanced binary search trees, but AVL trees enforce a stricter balance: every node’s subtrees differ in height by at most 1, giving worst-case height ≈ 1.44 log₂n.  
Red–black trees allow a looser balance (black-height rules), limiting height to ≤ 2 log₂n.  
Because AVL trees are shallower, successful and unsuccessful searches (or in-order scans) are typically a little faster.  
Insertions and deletions disturb AVL balance more often, so they may trigger up to O(log n) rotations, while red–black trees need at most 2 rotations on insert and 3 on delete.  
Thus AVL favors read-heavy workloads with infrequent updates, whereas red–black trees favor update-heavy or mixed workloads.  
Both use one extra field per node: AVL stores a 2-bit balance factor; red–black stores a 1-bit color.  
Under random operations the average heights and costs are close, so the practical difference is usually small.

Both AVL and red–black trees guarantee O(log n) search, insertion and deletion, but they achieve this with different balance strictness.  
1. AVL trees keep the height difference between any node’s sub-trees ≤1, giving a worst-case height of ≈1.44 log₂(n+2); red–black trees allow up to twice the black-height, yielding height ≤2 log₂(n+1).  
2. Because of the tighter height bound, AVL trees usually perform slightly fewer comparisons per search than red-black trees.  
3. Updates are costlier in AVL: an insertion may require up to two rotations and a deletion up to O(log n) rotations, whereas red-black trees need at most two rotations for either operation.  
4. Therefore AVL favors read-heavy workloads where fast lookups matter most, while red-black favors write-heavy or mixed workloads where cheaper rebalancing is beneficial.  
5. Each AVL node stores a 2-bit balance factor; each red-black node stores a 1-bit color flag—comparable memory overhead.  
6. Many system libraries (e.g., Linux rbtree, C++ std::map) choose red-black trees because their predictable, low-rotation updates suit general-purpose dynamic sets.  
7. AVL trees are common in applications like in-memory databases or caches that perform many more reads than writes.

AVL trees keep the height of left and right sub-trees within 1, limiting total height to ≈ 1.44 log₂ n, whereas red–black trees allow paths up to twice as long as the shortest, giving height ≤ 2 log₂ (n + 1).  
The tighter bound makes AVL lookups slightly faster because fewer levels are visited on average and in the worst case.  
That strict balance costs more maintenance: an AVL insertion may trigger up to 2 rotations and a deletion up to Θ(log n) rotations, while a red–black insertion or deletion needs no more than 2 rotations plus a few recolorings.  
Consequently, red–black trees generally outperform AVL trees in update-heavy workloads, while AVL trees excel when searches dominate.  
AVL nodes must store a balance factor or height (typically 2–3 bits), whereas red–black nodes add only one color bit, giving them marginally lower per-node memory overhead.  
Both structures guarantee O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion, differing mainly in constant factors and rotation counts.  
Red–black trees are favored in many libraries and kernels for their predictable, low-rotation updates, whereas AVL trees remain attractive for in-memory databases and caches that are read-heavy.

AVL trees enforce a stricter balance (height difference ≤ 1 between sibling sub-trees), giving a worst-case height of ⌈1.44 log₂ n⌉, whereas red–black trees only guarantee that the longest root-to-leaf path is ≤ 2 log₂ (n + 1), so they can be up to ~30 % taller.  
Consequently, searches in AVL trees perform fewer comparisons than in red–black trees, especially for read-heavy workloads.  
Insertions and deletions trigger rebalancing more often in AVL trees; an insertion may need up to two rotations and a deletion can cascade O(log n) rotations, while red–black trees need at most two rotations for insertion and typically fewer than AVL for deletion.  
This makes red–black trees faster on write-heavy workloads and preferable for systems (e.g., operating-system schedulers, associative containers in C++) where update cost dominates.  
AVL trees store a 2-bit balance factor per node; red–black trees store a 1-bit color, so space overhead is comparable but slightly smaller for red–black trees.  
Because red–black trees rotate less and allow a looser height bound, they tend to exhibit better cache behavior under mixed loads and are simpler to implement in lock-free or concurrent variants.  
AVL trees, however, are well suited for in-memory databases or search-intensive applications that value minimum lookup latency over update speed.

AVL trees keep each node’s subtree heights within 1, giving a worst-case height of ⌈log₂(n)⌉+1, while red–black trees allow looser balance (height ≤ 2·log₂(n)+1).  
Because they are shallower, AVL trees guarantee faster worst-case searches than red–black trees, but the difference is at most a constant factor (~45 % fewer levels).  
The stricter AVL condition causes insertions and, especially, deletions to trigger more rebalancing; they may require up to two rotations per insertion and O(log n) rotations per deletion, whereas red–black trees need at most one rotation per insertion or two per deletion.  
Thus, update operations are typically cheaper and more predictable in red–black trees, making them preferable for workloads with many modifications.  
AVL trees excel when searches dominate and updates are infrequent, offering slightly better lookup latency.  
Both structures use O(log n) time for search, insert, and delete and O(n) space overall.  
Implementation complexity and metadata size are similar (one balance factor or one color bit per node).  
In practice, the choice hinges on the read-vs-write ratio: AVL for read-heavy, red–black for write-heavy or mixed workloads.

QuickSort’s worst-case running time is Θ(n²) because if each pivot ends up being the smallest or largest element (e.g., sorting an already sorted array with the first-element pivot rule), one partition has n–1 elements and the other has 0, giving the recurrence T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n), whose solution is quadratic.  
Its average-case (expected) running time is Θ(n log n); with a random pivot or uniform input order, the partitions are roughly balanced on average, yielding the recurrence T(n)=2·T(n/2)+Θ(n) and a logarithmic recursion depth.  
The gap arises from how balanced the partitions are: balanced splits reduce the depth of recursion to O(log n), keeping total work proportional to n log n, while highly unbalanced splits push the recursion depth to n, adding up to n² comparisons and moves.

Worst-case QuickSort occurs when every partition leaves one side empty (e.g., already sorted data with the first or last item chosen as pivot), causing n-1 recursive levels and Θ(n²) comparisons and swaps.  
Average-case complexity is Θ(n log n) because, over all input permutations or with a random pivot, each level of recursion is expected to split the list proportionally (constant-fraction splits), yielding about log n levels with Θ(n) work per level.  
The key difference is pivot quality: consistently poor pivots create highly unbalanced partitions and quadratic work; randomly distributed or median-like pivots keep partitions balanced and total work near n log n.  
Space usage mirrors this: worst case needs Θ(n) stack frames, while the average case needs Θ(log n).

QuickSort’s worst-case running time is Θ(n²) because repeatedly choosing a pivot that leaves one subarray of size n−1 and the other of size 0 produces the recurrence T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n), whose solution is quadratic.  
Its average-case running time is Θ(n log n); with random data (or a randomized pivot) the expected sizes of the two recursive subproblems are roughly proportional to n/2, giving the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n), which solves to n log n.  
The difference arises from how balanced the partitions created by the pivot are: highly unbalanced partitions lead to many more comparisons and deeper recursion, while balanced partitions limit the depth of recursion to about log n and keep the total number of element comparisons linear at each level.

QuickSort’s worst-case running time is Θ(n²), reached when every pivot produces one empty and one (n–1)-element subarray, creating n levels of nearly full scans.  
Its average (expected) running time is Θ(n log n) because, over all pivot positions or with random pivot choice, partitions are usually reasonably balanced, giving about log n levels of recursion with n work per level.  
The disparity arises from partition balance: highly unbalanced splits force repeated passes over almost the entire remaining array, while roughly balanced splits cut the problem size exponentially each step.  
Randomized pivot selection or heuristics like median-of-three make the balanced scenario overwhelmingly likely, so QuickSort runs in Θ(n log n) time on average but still carries a Θ(n²) worst-case risk.

QuickSort’s worst-case running time is Θ(n²).  
This happens when each partition produces one empty subarray and one of size n-1, e.g., choosing the first element as pivot on an already sorted array.  
The recursion depth then reaches n and comparisons sum to 1+2+…+(n-1)=Θ(n²).  
By contrast, its expected (average-case) running time is Θ(n log n).  
With a random or input-independent pivot, each element has equal chance to appear in any position, so partitions are balanced on average.  
The recursion tree therefore has height Θ(log n), and processing n elements on each level yields the Θ(n log n) bound.  
Both cases include only constant-factor work per comparison or swap.  
Consequently, QuickSort is generally fast but can degrade without good pivot selection; randomization or median-of-three rules are used to keep the Θ(n²) outcome rare while retaining the Θ(n log n) expectation.

QuickSort’s running time depends on how the chosen pivot splits the array.  
If each pivot ends up being either the smallest or largest element, the partitions have sizes n−1 and 0, giving the recurrence T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n) and a worst-case time complexity of Θ(n²).  
With a randomly chosen pivot (or with random input), every element is equally likely to be the pivot, so the expected sizes of the two subarrays are roughly n/2; the average-case recurrence E[T(n)]=2E[T(n/2)]+Θ(n) solves to Θ(n log n).  
Thus, the worst‐case cost arises from extremely unbalanced splits, while the average cost reflects the high probability of balanced splits.  
In practice, randomized pivot selection or median-of-three heuristics make the Θ(n log n) behavior dominant.  
Both cases use only Θ(log n) auxiliary stack space with good pivot choices but can require Θ(n) stack space in the worst case.

QuickSort’s worst-case running time is Θ(n²).  
It occurs when each pivot chosen ends up being the smallest or largest element, leaving partitions of sizes n−1 and 0 at every recursive step.  
This gives the recurrence T(n)=T(n−1)+Θ(n), whose solution sums to Θ(n²).  
The average-case running time is Θ(n log n).  
With random inputs or random pivot selection, the expected split of each pivot is roughly proportional, keeping partitions reasonably balanced.  
The corresponding average-case recurrence solves to Θ(n log n).  
Thus, consistently unbalanced splits make quadratic work, while typically balanced splits reduce total comparisons to proportional n log n.  
Recursion depth is O(n) in the worst case versus O(log n) on average, so auxiliary stack space follows the same pattern.

Let T(n) be the worst-case time to sort n elements with MergeSort.  
The algorithm splits the array into two halves (cost Θ(1)), recursively sorts each half (each of size n/2), and then merges the two sorted halves in linear time Θ(n).  
This yields the recurrence T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + Θ(n).  
Applying the Master Theorem (a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = Θ(n)), we have n^{log_b a} = n^{log_2 2} = n.  
Because f(n) = Θ(n) matches n^{log_b a}, the theorem’s second case gives T(n) = Θ(n log n).  
Thus, MergeSort performs a total of Θ(n log n) comparisons/moves, leading to an overall running time of O(n log n).

MergeSort repeatedly divides the input of size n in half, producing a recursion tree whose height is ⌈log₂ n⌉.  
At each level of that tree, all sub-arrays created at that depth together contain exactly the original n elements.  
Merging the pairs of sub-arrays on any single level therefore costs Θ(n) comparisons and moves.  
Since there are Θ(log n) levels and each level costs Θ(n), the total work sums to Θ(n log n).  
Constant-time base cases and overhead do not change this asymptotic bound, so MergeSort’s running time is O(n log n).

Let T(n) be the worst-case time to sort n elements with MergeSort.  
The algorithm splits the array in half, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves.  
Splitting is O(1); each merge scans every element once, so merging costs O(n).  
Thus T(n) satisfies the recurrence T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + cn for some constant c.  
By the Master Theorem (case 2, where a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = Θ(n)), the solution is T(n) = Θ(n log n).  
Intuitively, the merge at every level touches all n elements, but the number of recursion levels is log₂n, giving n work per level × log₂n levels = Θ(n log n) total time.

Let T(n) be the running time on n items.  
Mergesort splits the array into two halves, sorts each half recursively, then performs a linear Θ(n) merge.  
This gives the recurrence T(n)=2 T(n⁄2)+Θ(n).  
In the Master Theorem, a=2 and b=2, so n^{log_b a}=n.  
Because the extra work f(n)=Θ(n) matches n^{log_b a}, the theorem’s second case applies.  
That case yields T(n)=Θ(n log n).  
Intuitively, there are log₂n levels of splitting, and each level does Θ(n) combining work, so the total is Θ(n log n).

MergeSort splits an array in half until subarrays of one element remain, producing a recursion tree whose height is log₂n because the size halves each level. At every level of that tree, all elements participate in a merge operation that scans and copies each element exactly once, costing Θ(n) work for that level. Thus the total work equals the sum of Θ(n) over log₂n levels, yielding Θ(n log n). Formally, the time satisfies the recurrence T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + Θ(n); applying the Master Theorem to a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = Θ(n) gives T(n) = Θ(n log n). Both best-case and worst-case running times follow the same recurrence, so no input arrangement can improve or worsen this bound. Therefore, MergeSort runs in O(n log n) time.

At size n, MergeSort divides the array into two halves, sorts each half recursively, and then merges the two sorted halves.  
The merge step alone scans every element once, costing Θ(n).  
Because the array is halved each time, a recursion tree has height log₂n.  
At every level of that tree, the total work of all merges is still Θ(n) (since every element participates in exactly one merge per level).  
Multiplying the Θ(n) work per level by the log₂n levels yields Θ(n log n).

Each recursive call splits the input array in half, so the recursion tree has a height of log₂n.  
At every level of this tree, the algorithm performs a merge operation that touches every element exactly once, costing Θ(n) time for that level.  
Because there are log₂n levels and each level costs Θ(n), the total work is Θ(n · log₂n).  
Formally, the running time satisfies the recurrence T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + Θ(n), whose Master-Theorem solution is Θ(n log n).

Proof by strong induction on array length n.  
Base case (n ≤ 1): MergeSort returns the array unchanged, which is already sorted and is a permutation of the input.  
Inductive hypothesis: assume every array of length < n is correctly sorted and permuted by MergeSort.  
Inductive step: for an input of length n ≥ 2, the algorithm divides it into subarrays L and R with lengths < n, calls MergeSort recursively on each, and by the hypothesis obtains sorted arrays L′ and R′ that are permutations of L and R.  
The merge routine repeatedly appends the smaller front element of L′ or R′, guaranteeing that the resulting array is in non-decreasing order while containing every element of L′ and R′ exactly once.  
Because L′ ∪ R′ equals the original array’s elements, the merged output is a sorted permutation of the input.  
Thus, by induction, MergeSort is correct for all input sizes.

Let mergeSort(A) return A if |A| ≤ 1; otherwise split A into halves A₁,A₂, recursively call mergeSort on each, then return Merge(A₁′,A₂′).  
Lemma (Merge): Given two non-decreasing sequences B and C, Merge outputs sequence D that is (i) non-decreasing and (ii) a permutation of B∪C. Proof: loop invariant—at each step, D contains exactly the |D| smallest elements of B∪C not yet copied—holds at initialization, is preserved by copying the smaller head element, and at termination leaves B and C empty, so (i)–(ii) follow.  
Base case: If |A| ≤ 1, mergeSort returns A, which is trivially sorted and a permutation of itself.  
Inductive hypothesis: Assume mergeSort correctly sorts every array of size < n.  
Inductive step: For |A| = n > 1, recursive calls return sorted permutations B and C of A₁ and A₂ by the hypothesis; applying the Lemma, Merge(B,C) yields a sorted permutation D of A₁∪A₂ = A, so mergeSort(A) is correct.  
By mathematical induction on |A|, mergeSort returns a sorted sequence containing exactly the elements of the input for every array size.

Proof (strong induction on input size n).  
1. Base case: If n ≤ 1, MergeSort immediately returns the array; a single-element (or empty) array is trivially sorted, so the algorithm is correct.  
2. Inductive hypothesis: Assume MergeSort correctly returns a sorted permutation for every array of size < n.  
3. Consider an array A of size n > 1. MergeSort divides A into two subarrays L and R with sizes < n, recursively sorts them, and then calls MERGE(L,R).  
4. By the inductive hypothesis, the recursive calls return sorted arrays L′ and R′ that are permutations of L and R, respectively.  
5. The MERGE procedure repeatedly selects the smaller front element of L′ or R′, appending it to the output; thus every element from L′∪R′ appears exactly once and in non-decreasing order, so MERGE produces a sorted permutation of L′∪R′.  
6. Because L′∪R′ equals the original set of elements of A, the merged output is a sorted permutation of A.  
7. Therefore, by induction, MergeSort outputs a sorted permutation for every n, establishing total correctness (partial correctness plus guaranteed termination from strictly decreasing subproblem sizes).

Let P(n) be “MergeSort correctly returns the multiset of its n input keys in non-decreasing order.”  
Base: For n ≤ 1 the algorithm immediately returns the input, so P(0) and P(1) hold.  
Inductive hypothesis: Assume P(k) is true for every k<n.  
Inductive step: Given an array A of length n>1, MergeSort splits it into two subarrays of sizes ⌊n/2⌋ and ⌈n/2⌉, each strictly smaller than n; by the hypothesis the recursive calls return these two subarrays sorted and containing exactly the elements that were in them.  
The Merge procedure then scans the two sorted lists once, appending the smaller current key to a new output list, thereby producing a single list that is (i) sorted and (ii) the multiset union of the two inputs; thus it contains exactly the keys of A, all in order.  
Therefore the whole call on size n returns a sorted permutation of its original elements, so P(n) holds.  
By mathematical induction, P(n) is true for all n≥0, establishing partial correctness.  
Because each recursive call acts on strictly smaller arrays and the merge step is finite, the recursion depth is O(log n) and every call finishes, so the algorithm terminates.  
Hence MergeSort is correct.

Prove correctness by strong induction on the input length n.  
Base case: if n ≤ 1, MergeSort returns the array unchanged, which is trivially sorted.  
Inductive hypothesis: assume MergeSort correctly sorts every array of length < n.  
For an input of length n > 1, the algorithm splits it into subarrays L and R whose lengths are < n, so by the hypothesis each recursive call returns a sorted permutation L′ and R′.  
The merge step repeatedly outputs the smaller front element of L′ or R′, ensuring every prefix of the output is the smallest possible and thus the whole result is sorted.  
Because merge outputs every element of L′ and R′ exactly once, the final array contains all and only the original elements.  
Hence the merged result is both sorted and a permutation of the input.  
By induction, MergeSort is correct for all n.

1. Proof by strong induction on the input length n.  
2. Base case: for n ≤ 1 MergeSort returns the array unchanged, which is trivially sorted and a permutation of itself.  
3. Inductive hypothesis: assume MergeSort correctly sorts every array of length < n.  
4. For an array A of length n > 1, MergeSort splits A into subarrays L and R with lengths < n.  
5. By the hypothesis, the recursive calls return L′ and R′ that are individually sorted and are permutations of L and R respectively.  
6. The merge step repeatedly appends the smaller front element of L′ or R′, ensuring each output element is ≤ every later output element.  
7. Therefore the merged sequence S is sorted.  
8. Because each element of L′ and R′ is moved exactly once to S, S is a permutation of A.  
9. Hence MergeSort returns a sorted permutation for length n inputs.  
10. By induction, MergeSort is correct for all finite array lengths.

We prove correctness by strong induction on n, the number of elements in the array.  
Base case: for n ≤ 1, MergeSort returns the array unchanged, which is already sorted and a permutation of itself.  
Induction hypothesis: assume MergeSort works for all arrays of size < n.  
Inductive step: given an array of length n > 1, MergeSort splits it into two subarrays of lengths n₁ and n₂ with n₁,n₂ < n.  
By the induction hypothesis, the recursive calls return each subarray sorted and as permutations of their original elements.  
The merge procedure takes two sorted arrays and outputs their union in non-decreasing order while preserving all elements, thus producing a sorted permutation of the two inputs.  
Therefore, the merged array of length n is sorted and is a permutation of the original array.  
By induction, MergeSort always outputs a sorted permutation of its input, establishing partial correctness.  
Because each recursive call strictly reduces the subarray length, the algorithm eventually reaches the base case, so it terminates.  
Hence, MergeSort is correct.

Standard recursive MergeSort allocates a temporary array of size Θ(n) for merging the two sorted halves, which dominates its space usage. The recursion stack adds only Θ(log n) extra space. Combining both, the overall auxiliary space complexity is Θ(n). Bottom-up (iterative) MergeSort still needs Θ(n) auxiliary space for merging. Specialized in-place variants can reduce auxiliary space to Θ(1) but sacrifice simplicity and often performance, so they are not the typical implementation.

Standard (top-down) MergeSort requires Θ(n) auxiliary space to hold the temporary merged array(s) created at each combine step, because every element must be copied into a buffer once per level before being written back. The recursive calls additionally consume Θ(log n) stack space, as the depth of the recursion tree equals the number of times the array can be halved. Combining these, the overall extra memory used is Θ(n + log n) = Θ(n). An in-place variant can cut the auxiliary array to O(log n) or even O(1), but at the cost of higher constant factors and implementation complexity. Therefore, for the conventional version taught in most textbooks, the space complexity is Θ(n).

For the classic top-down MergeSort on an array, an auxiliary array of size n is allocated to hold elements during each merge, giving O(n) extra storage. The recursion adds a call stack of depth ⌈log₂n⌉, which costs only O(log n) additional space. Since O(n) dominates O(log n), the total auxiliary space complexity is O(n). (If implemented on linked lists, only the O(log n) stack space is needed, so the complexity can drop to O(log n).)

Standard (top-down) MergeSort allocates a temporary array the same size as the input when it performs each merge, giving it Θ(n) extra memory beyond the original array. The recursion uses at most Θ(log n) stack frames, each of constant size, so this portion is minor relative to Θ(n). Therefore the overall auxiliary space complexity of the usual MergeSort implementation is Θ(n). Variants that perform the merge in place can reduce the auxiliary array to O(1), leaving only the Θ(log n) recursion stack (or even O(1) if implemented iteratively), but these are not the default textbook algorithm.

The standard recursive MergeSort on an array allocates a temporary array (or two sub-arrays) whose combined size is Θ(n).  
The recursion adds one stack frame per level, giving Θ(log n) additional space.  
Since Θ(n) dominates Θ(log n), the overall auxiliary space complexity is Θ(n).  
If an iterative (bottom-up) version is used that still copies into a temporary array, the space stays Θ(n) because the auxiliary buffer is still needed even without recursion.  
For linked lists, merging can be done by pointer re-wiring without extra buffers, so only the recursion stack of Θ(log n) nodes is required, giving Θ(log n) space.  
In-place array MergeSort variants that try to avoid the extra buffer exist, but they incur higher constant factors or more complex algorithms and still need at least Θ(log n) stack space.  
Therefore, the usual array MergeSort is Θ(n) space, while optimized linked-list or in-place variants can reduce it to Θ(log n) at best.

Standard array-based MergeSort allocates an auxiliary array to hold the merged output at each level; this buffer can be reused, but its size is Θ(n).  
The recursion adds only Θ(log n) stack frames, which is asymptotically dominated by the buffer size.  
Therefore, the overall extra space required by the conventional, stable MergeSort is Θ(n).  
(Variants that perform in-place merging can reduce auxiliary space to O(1) but at the cost of increased constant factors and implementation complexity.)

Standard (array-based) MergeSort allocates a temporary array large enough to hold the elements being merged, and at the top level that auxiliary array is Θ(n).  
Because sub-calls reuse this same buffer rather than allocate new ones simultaneously, the total auxiliary data stored at any moment never exceeds Θ(n).  
The recursive decomposition adds a call-stack depth of Θ(log n), which contributes only Θ(log n) additional memory words.  
Combining these, the overall extra space required is Θ(n + log n) = Θ(n).  
Therefore, MergeSort is not an in-place algorithm: its space complexity is linear in the size of the input.

Building the binary heap from the n input keys costs O(n) time.  
The heap guarantees that its maximum (or minimum) element is at the root, which can be removed in O(1) time.  
After each removal, a “sift-down’’ (heapify) operation restores the heap property, and in a binary heap this takes O(log n) time because the heap’s height is ⌊log₂ n⌋.  
We remove the root and perform heapify exactly n times.  
Thus, the n removals contribute n · O(log n) = O(n log n).  
Adding the initial O(n) heap-construction cost gives O(n) + O(n log n).  
Since O(n log n) dominates O(n) for large n, the overall running time is O(n log n) in the best, average, and worst cases.

A binary heap built from n keys can be constructed in Θ(n) time.  
Once the heap is built, HeapSort repeatedly removes the maximum (or minimum) element n times.  
Each removal swaps the root with the last element and then performs “heap-down” to restore the heap property.  
Because a binary heap has height ⌊log₂ n⌋, a single heap-down operation touches at most O(log n) nodes.  
Thus the n removals together cost n · O(log n) = O(n log n).  
Adding the initial Θ(n) build cost yields O(n) + O(n log n) = O(n log n).  
No step in the algorithm exceeds this bound, so the overall running time of HeapSort is Θ(n log n), independent of the initial order of the input.

Heapsort first transforms the array into a binary heap in O(n) time using the “heap-build” procedure that performs a constant‐amortized number of sift-downs per element.  
A binary heap of n elements has height ⌊log₂n⌋, so removing the maximum element (or inserting one) requires at most that many comparisons and swaps.  
Heapsort then performs n successive extract-max operations; each costs O(log n), giving O(n log n) for this phase.  
The total running time is the sum of the build phase, O(n), and the extraction phase, O(n log n).  
Because O(n log n) dominates O(n) for large n, the overall complexity is Θ(n log n).

Building the binary heap from the n input keys with the bottom-up heapify algorithm costs Θ(n).  
The algorithm then performs n extract-max (or extract-min) operations to move the heap’s root to its final sorted position.  
Each extraction swaps the root with the last element and “sifts down” along a path whose length is bounded by the heap’s height, which is ⌊log₂ n⌋, so one extraction costs Θ(log n).  
Repeating this Θ(log n) operation n times contributes Θ(n log n).  
Adding the linear build cost yields Θ(n) + Θ(n log n) = Θ(n log n), and the n log n term dominates for large n.  
Thus HeapSort runs in Θ(n log n) time in the best, average, and worst cases.

Building a binary heap from n items costs Θ(n) because each node is sifted down at most a distance proportional to its depth.  
The algorithm then performs n iterations in which it removes the root (the largest element) and restores the heap property with a heapify (sift-down) operation.  
A heap of at most k elements has height ⌊log₂k⌋, so each heapify costs Θ(log n) in the worst case.  
Doing this heapify once per removal gives n × Θ(log n) = Θ(n log n).  
The Θ(n) build step is asymptotically dominated by the Θ(n log n) removal phase.  
Consequently, the overall running time of HeapSort is Θ(n log n).

A binary heap of n keys can be built in Θ(n) time using the bottom-up heap-construction procedure. After the heap is built, HeapSort repeatedly removes the maximum (or minimum) key and restores the heap property by “sifting down” a single path from the root, whose length is bounded by the height of the heap. A binary heap storing n elements has height ⌊log₂ n⌋, so each sift-down costs Θ(log n) time in the worst case. The algorithm performs exactly n such removals. Therefore the total cost of the removal phase is Θ(n log n). Adding the Θ(n) build phase yields Θ(n log n + n), which simplifies to Θ(n log n). Because these bounds hold for every input, HeapSort’s worst-case, average-case, and best-case running time are all Θ(n log n).

HeapSort first converts the input array into a binary heap in O(n) time using the bottom-up "heapify" procedure.  
A binary heap guarantees that inserting or deleting its root takes O(log n) time because the heap has height ⌈log₂ n⌉ and any re-heapification touches at most one path from root to leaf.  
After the initial heap is built, HeapSort performs exactly n delete-max (or delete-min) operations, each followed by a single O(log n) “sift-down” to restore the heap property.  
Thus the n deletions cost n · O(log n) = O(n log n).  
Adding the linear-time heap construction gives a total running time of O(n) + O(n log n) = O(n log n), and no individual step dominates this bound.

Label the heap’s height h = ⌊log₂n⌋.  
Build–Heap calls Sift-Down on every internal node, starting at index ⌊n/2⌋ and moving upward.  
A node at depth d (root at depth 0) can be sifted down at most h−d levels, so its cost is O(h−d).  
At depth d there are at most 2ᵈ nodes, so the total work is  

  Σ_{d=0}^{h-1} 2ᵈ · O(h−d)  ≤  O( Σ_{d=0}^{h-1} 2ᵈ·(h−d) ).  

Substitute k = h−d to sum by heights:  

  = O( Σ_{k=1}^{h} 2^{h-k} · k )  
  = O( 2^{h} · Σ_{k=1}^{h} k/2^{k} )  
  ≤ O( n · 2 ) = O(n) because 2^{h} ≤ n and Σ_{k≥1} k/2^{k} = 2.  

Hence the bottom-up Build–Heap runs in linear time.

Let h = ⌊log₂n⌋ be the height of the complete binary tree containing the n array keys.  
Bottom-up HEAP-BUILD calls “sift-down” on every internal node; a node at depth d (root = 0) can move down at most h – d levels, so one call costs O(h – d) comparisons.  
There are exactly 2ᵈ nodes at depth d, hence the total work is  

 Σ_{d=0}^{h} 2ᵈ · (h – d) = n · Σ_{k=0}^{h} k / 2ᵏ.  

Because Σ_{k=0}^{∞} k / 2ᵏ = 2, the finite sum is < 2, giving  

 Total work ≤ 2n = O(n).  

Thus the bottom-up BUILD-HEAP procedure transforms an arbitrary array into a binary heap in linear time.

In the bottom-up “heapify” procedure we begin with the last internal node ⌊n/2⌋ and repeatedly sift it downward, moving leftward to the root.  
A node that lies h edges above the lowest level can be moved downward at most h steps, so its individual cost is O(h).  
Exactly ⌈n/2^{h+1}⌉ nodes have height h (because half the remaining nodes disappear each level up).  
The total work is therefore  

  Σ_{h=0}^{⌊log n⌋} ⌈n/2^{h+1}⌉ · O(h)  ≤  n · Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h}.

The infinite series Σ_{h≥0} h/2^{h} converges to 2, hence the sum is bounded above by 2n, i.e. Θ(n).  
Thus the entire heap-construction requires only linear time, O(n), even though individual sifts may take logarithmic time.

Build-Heap starts at the last internal node ⌊n/2⌋ and calls Sift-Down for every internal node i.  
Let height(i) be the number of edges from node i to its deepest descendant; Sift-Down(i) moves an element downward at most height(i) steps, so its cost is O(height(i)).  
In a complete binary tree with n nodes, exactly ⌈n/2^{h+1}⌉ nodes have height h (h = 0 for leaves, 1 for their parents, etc.).  
Thus the total work  
 W ≤ Σ_{h=0}^{⌊log n⌋} ⌈n/2^{h+1}⌉ · O(h) = O(n) Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h}.  
The infinite series Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h} converges to 2, so W ≤ 2n = Θ(n).  
Hence the entire Build-Heap procedure runs in linear time, O(n).

Heap-Build scans the array from ⌊n/2⌋ down to 1, performing “sift-down” on every node.  
Sifting a node at height h (distance to a leaf) costs at most h comparisons/swaps.  
At most ⌈n/2^{h+1}⌉ nodes have height h, because each level downward at least doubles the number of nodes.  
Total work  T(n) ≤ Σ_{h=0}^{⌊log n⌋} ⌈n/2^{h+1}⌉ · h  ≤ n · Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h+1}.  
The geometric/arithmetical series Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h} sums to 2.  
Hence T(n) ≤ n · 2 = 2n, which is Θ(n).  
Therefore, building a binary heap from an arbitrary array runs in O(n) time.

BUILD-HEAP calls HEAPIFY on every internal node i = ⌊n/2⌋ … 1.  
Running time of HEAPIFY on node i is proportional to its height h(i) = ⌊log n⌋ – ⌊log i⌋.  
For each height h ≥ 0 there are at most n/2^{h+1} nodes of that height.  
Total work = Σ_{h=0}^{⌊log n⌋} (n/2^{h+1})·h.  
Factor n out: n Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h+1}.  
The infinite series Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h+1} sums to 1.  
Thus total time ≤ n·1 = Θ(n).  
Therefore a heap can be built from an arbitrary array in linear time.

In the bottom-up heap–build algorithm we start at the last non-leaf index ⌊n/2⌋ and “sift-down’’ each element toward the leaves.  
A node of height h (leaves have h = 0) can be moved downward at most h positions, so its individual cost is O(h).  
At height h there are at most n/2^{h+1} nodes.  
Total work ≤ Σ_{h=0}^{⌊log n⌋} (n/2^{h+1})·O(h) = O(n)·Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h}.  
The geometric/arithmetical series Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h} converges to 2.  
Hence the whole sum is bounded by 2n, i.e. Θ(n).  
Therefore the standard bottom-up procedure builds a binary heap from any array in linear time.

The heap property says that in a min-heap every node’s key is ≤ its children’s keys (≥ in a max-heap) while the tree remains complete. Heaps are stored in arrays, letting the children of index i be at 2i and 2i+1 (1-based) or 2i+1 and 2i+2 (0-based). To insert, append the new key at the next open leaf, then repeatedly swap it with its parent (“sift-up”) until the property holds. To delete the root, save its value, move the last array element to the root, shrink the array, and then repeatedly swap this moved key with the smaller (or larger) child that violates the property (“sift-down”). Each sift-up or sift-down moves the key one level, so at most log n swaps occur. Thus both insertion and deletion preserve completeness and restore the heap order in O(log n) time.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree in which every parent node satisfies either the min-heap order (key ≤ each child) or the max-heap order (key ≥ each child).  
Insertion: place the new key in the next open leaf to preserve completeness, then “bubble-up” (heapify-up) by repeatedly swapping it with its parent while the heap order is violated, stopping when the property is restored or the key reaches the root.  
Because each swap moves the key one level closer to the root, insertion takes at most ⌈log₂n⌉ swaps.  
Deletion (usually of the root): move the key in the last leaf to the root position, delete that leaf to keep the tree complete, then “bubble-down” (heapify-down) by repeatedly swapping the moved key with the smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) of its children until the heap order holds or a leaf is reached.  
This downward adjustment also costs at most ⌈log₂n⌉ swaps.  
Both operations therefore maintain the heap property while preserving the complete-tree shape in O(log n) time.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree where every node obeys the heap property: in a max-heap each parent key is ≥ its children (≤ for a min-heap).  
Insertion: place the new element in the next open leaf position to keep the tree complete, then repeatedly compare it with its parent and swap upward (“sift up”) until the property is satisfied or the root is reached.  
Because the tree height is ⌈log₂ n⌉, insertion needs at most O(log n) swaps.  
Deletion (of the root): move the last leaf’s key to the root, delete that leaf, and then repeatedly compare the moved key with its larger (or smaller) child, swapping downward (“sift down”) until the heap property holds or a leaf is reached.  
Sifting down also takes O(log n) time.  
At every swap only local comparisons are made, so once the process ends each subtree—and therefore the entire structure—again satisfies the heap property while completeness remains intact.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array where every parent key is ≤ (min-heap) or ≥ (max-heap) the keys of its children; this ordering rule is the heap property.  
To insert, place the new element in the next open array slot (the last leaf), then “percolate up”: repeatedly swap it with its parent until the parent order is correct or the root is reached, restoring the heap in O(log n).  
To delete the root, replace it with the last array element, shrink the heap size, and “percolate down”: repeatedly swap this element with the smaller child (min-heap) or larger child (max-heap) while it violates the heap property.  
Percolate-up fixes the path from the new leaf to the root; percolate-down fixes the path from the root to a leaf, so all other subtrees remain valid heaps throughout.  
Parent and child positions are computed in O(1) time with indices ⌊i/2⌋, 2i, 2i+1, making both insertion and root deletion Θ(log n) operations that preserve the heap property.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array in which every node obeys the heap property: in a max-heap each parent’s key is ≥ its children’s keys (≤ for a min-heap).  
Insertion appends the new key to the end of the array (the next open leaf), then repeatedly swaps it with its parent while the heap property is violated (“sift-up”), stopping when the parent is no longer smaller/larger or the root is reached.  
Because each swap moves one level upward in a tree of height ⌈log₂n⌉, insertion costs O(log n).  
Deletion typically removes the root (the maximum or minimum key).  
The last array element is moved to the root position, the array size is reduced by one, and the moved key is repeatedly swapped with the larger (or smaller) of its children while the heap property is violated (“sift-down” or “heapify”).  
Each swap moves one level downward, so deletion also costs O(log n) and leaves the structure both complete and heap-ordered.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree where every parent key is ordered with respect to its children: in a max-heap each parent ≥ its children, and in a min-heap each parent ≤ its children.  
Insertion keeps this property by first appending the new element at the leftmost open position of the bottom level (to preserve completeness) and then repeatedly “percolating up” (swapping with its parent) until the ordering condition is satisfied.  
Deletion is usually performed on the root (the maximum or minimum).  The last element on the bottom level is moved to the root to maintain completeness, then the heap property is restored by “percolating down”: the element is swapped with its larger (max-heap) or smaller (min-heap) child until it is correctly ordered or reaches a leaf.  
Both sift-up and sift-down take O(log n) time because the heap’s height is logarithmic in the number of elements.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree where every parent key is ≤ (min-heap) or ≥ (max-heap) its children, guaranteeing the root is the minimum or maximum element, respectively.  
Insertion: place the new key in the leftmost open position on the bottom level to preserve completeness, then repeatedly swap it with its parent (percolate/heapify-up) until the heap property holds.  
Because each swap moves the key up one level in a tree of height log n, insertion runs in O(log n) time.  
Deletion of the root (extract-min or extract-max): replace the root with the last key in the bottom level, remove that last node, and then repeatedly swap the moved key with the smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) of its children (percolate/heapify-down) until order is restored.  
Each downward swap reduces the node’s height by one, so deletion also runs in O(log n).  
Both operations rely solely on comparisons and swaps, keeping the structure complete and the heap property intact.

With a doubling (and optional halving) resize strategy:  
1. Random access and overwriting are Θ(1).  
2. Pushing to the end is Θ(1) except on a full array; the k-element copy during resizing makes that single push Θ(k), but across m pushes the total extra cost forms a geometric series ≤2m, so amortized cost is Θ(1) per push.  
3. Popping from the end is Θ(1); if capacity is halved when size ≤¼ capacity, the occasional Θ(k) copy again yields Θ(1) amortized per pop.  
4. Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position requires shifting Θ(n) elements, so even amortized complexity is Θ(n).  
5. Therefore: access/update Θ(1), push_back Θ(1) amortized, pop_back Θ(1) amortized, and interior insert/delete Θ(n) amortized.

In a dynamic array that doubles its capacity when full and (optionally) halves when it becomes ≤ 1/4 full, direct indexing always costs Θ(1).  
Appending an element normally costs Θ(1), and the rare Θ(n) copy during a resize is offset over the n inexpensive appends that preceded it, giving an amortized cost of Θ(1) per append.  
Similarly, if shrinking is triggered only when the array is ≤ 1/4 full, the occasional Θ(n) copy during contraction is paid for by the Θ(n) prior deletions, so each delete-at-end is also Θ(1) amortized.  
Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position still requires shifting elements, so these operations remain Θ(n) even in the amortized sense.  
Thus: indexing = Θ(1), append / pop-back = Θ(1) amortized, arbitrary insert / delete = Θ(n) amortized, and the overall space used is at most 2n.

For a dynamic array that doubles its capacity when full (and halves it when one-quarter full):

1. Random access is always Θ(1).
2. Appending an element costs Θ(1) amortized, though a single append that triggers a resize costs Θ(n) in the worst case.
3. Deleting the last element costs Θ(1) amortized; a shrink resize, when performed, costs Θ(n) in the worst case.
4. The total cost of a sequence of n appends (starting from an empty array) is Θ(n), so the average cost per append is Θ(1).
5. Similarly, a sequence of n pops incurs Θ(n) total movement, giving Θ(1) amortized per pop.
6. Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position requires shifting elements, yielding Θ(n) worst-case and Θ(n) amortized per such operation.
7. Thus, common stack-like operations (push/pop) are Θ(1) amortized, whereas general inserts/deletes inside the array remain Θ(n).

For a dynamic array that starts with size 1 and doubles its capacity whenever full (and optionally halves when one-quarter full), element access is always O(1) worst-case. An append (push_back) costs O(1) amortized, because the occasional O(n) resize occurs only once every n constant-time inserts, giving total cost 2n over n operations. Pop_back is likewise O(1) amortized if the array is halved only when usage drops to ≤¼ capacity. Insertion or deletion at an arbitrary index costs O(n) amortized, dominated by the need to shift up to n/2 elements; the rare resize does not change the asymptotic bound. Iteration over all elements remains O(n). Thus the amortized complexities are: access O(1), append O(1), pop O(1), arbitrary insert/delete O(n), with overall storage proportional to Θ(n).

For a dynamic array that doubles its capacity when full and (optionally) halves it when ≤ ¼ full, the amortized cost of appending (push_back) is O(1): m successive appends take O(m) total, because each expensive resize to size k copies k elements and occurs only after roughly k inexpensive inserts. Access by index is O(1) worst-case and amortized. pop_back is also O(1) amortized, since a shrink-resize copies at most n/2 elements after Θ(n) constant pops. Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position requires shifting Θ(n) elements, so both have Θ(n) worst-case and amortized cost. Thus the overall amortized complexities are: append O(1), pop_back O(1), random access O(1), interior insert/delete Θ(n), with occasional resizes not changing these bounds.

Accessing or overwriting an element by index costs O(1) in both worst-case and amortized time.  
Appending (push_back) costs O(1) amortized because a sequence of m appends triggers at most ⌊log₂m⌋ resizings, giving total cost O(m); the single operation that causes a resize is O(n) worst-case.  
Removing the last element (pop_back) is O(1) amortized—even if the implementation halves capacity when the load factor drops below ½—because total shrink costs over m removals are O(m).  
Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position requires shifting Θ(n) elements, so its worst-case and amortized cost are both Θ(n).  
Doubling (and optionally halving) capacity is sufficient to keep the aggregate cost of any intermixed sequence of k appends and pops within Θ(k).

For a dynamic array that doubles its capacity when full and halves it when one-quarter full, random access and updating by index take O(1) worst-case time. Appending or popping at the end costs O(1) amortized: each individual reallocation of Θ(n) happens only after Θ(n) constant-time pushes/pops, so the total cost of n end-operations is Θ(n). Consequently, the average cost per push_back or pop_back remains O(1). Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position still requires shifting elements, so these operations are Θ(n) both in the worst case and on average. Resizing uses geometric growth/shrinkage, ensuring that the total cost of all reallocations over any sequence of m operations is O(m). Therefore, the amortized space overhead is at most a constant factor of the number of stored elements, and no operation causes unbounded quadratic work over time.

Amortized analysis finds an upper bound on the average cost per operation over any long sequence, even if individual operations can be expensive.  
In the accounting (a.k.a. bank-account) method, each operation is charged an “amortized cost” that may exceed or fall below its real cost; the surplus is stored as prepaid credit on data-structure objects, and deficits are paid from previously saved credit.  
The rule is: at no time may the total stored credit become negative; this ensures that the total real cost of any sequence ≤ total amortized cost charged.  

The potential method formalizes the same idea with a numeric potential function Φ(state) ≥ 0.  
Define amortized_cost(i) = real_cost(i) + Φ(after i) − Φ(before i).  
Because ∑[Φ(after i) − Φ(before i)] telescopes to Φ(final) − Φ(initial) and Φ(initial) ≥ 0, the sum of real costs over any sequence ≤ sum of amortized costs.  
Thus, by choosing charges or a potential that keeps individual amortized_cost(i) small, we prove a small average cost per operation regardless of the worst-case pattern of requests.

Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over any worst-case sequence.  
In the accounting (banker’s) method, each operation is assigned an “amortized” charge: expensive operations may be under-charged, cheap ones over-charged.  
Surplus from over-charged operations is stored as credits on data-structure objects, forming a conceptual bank account.  
Credits can be spent only by future operations that touch the credited objects; if the account never goes negative, all costs are covered.  
Thus the sum of amortized charges always upper-bounds the true total cost, proving that n operations, each with O(1) amortized cost, run in O(n) worst-case time.  
The potential method restates this: choose a non-negative potential Φ(state) and define amortized cost = actual cost + (Φ_after – Φ_before).  
Because Φ never drops below zero, the telescoping sum of potential changes shows total amortized cost ≥ total actual cost.  
Both methods are equivalent; the accounting view is intuitive, while the potential view is algebraic.  
They guarantee low average performance even when individual operations occasionally incur high cost.

Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over any sequence, ensuring worst-case guarantees for the whole sequence rather than for individual steps. In the accounting (or potential) method, each operation is charged an amortized cost that may exceed its actual cost, and the excess is stored as “credit.” These credits accumulate as a potential Φ associated with the data structure’s state. Choose Φ(state) ≥ 0 with Φ(initial) = 0. For the i-th operation, define amortized_cost = real_cost + Φ_after − Φ_before. Summing k operations telescopes to Σ amortized = Σ real + Φ_k − Φ_0 = Σ real + Φ_k. Because Φ_k ≥ 0, the total amortized cost upper-bounds the total real cost, proving the desired complexity. Designing Φ (e.g., proportional to unused table capacity in dynamic arrays) lets us show tight bounds such as O(1) amortized insertions despite occasional costly resizes.

Amortized analysis guarantees a bound on the average cost of each operation over any sequence, even if some individual operations are expensive.  
In the accounting method, each operation is assigned an artificial “charge” that may exceed its actual cost; the surplus is stored as prepaid credit that later pays for more expensive operations, ensuring the total charged cost upper-bounds the real total cost.  
You choose charges so that the running credit balance never becomes negative, then the amortized cost per operation is simply the chosen charge.  
The potential method formalizes this idea with a potential function Φ(state) ≥ 0 that measures stored energy (credit) in the data structure.  
Define amortized cost â_i = real cost c_i + Φ_i − Φ_{i−1}; summing telescopes to Σâ_i = Σc_i + Φ_n − Φ_0 ≥ Σc_i, so the average amortized cost bounds the average real cost.  
By designing Φ so that its change absorbs occasional spikes in c_i, you can prove tight per-operation bounds such as O(1) for stack push/pop with periodic resizing or O(α(n)) for union-find.  
Accounting is an intuitive “credit” view, while the potential method gives an algebraic framework; both yield identical amortized guarantees.

Amortized analysis finds the average cost per operation over any worst-case sequence.  
Accounting (banker’s) method assigns each operation an “amortized” charge; cheap operations are overcharged and the surplus is stored as credits that pay for later expensive ones, ensuring the credit balance never drops below zero.  
Because the sum of amortized charges equals the sum of real costs plus non-negative leftover credit, the total real cost of n operations is bounded by the total amortized cost.  
The potential method encodes those credits as a non-negative potential function Φ(state).  
For operation i, define amortized cost âᵢ = real costᵢ + Φ_after – Φ_before; choosing Φ₀ = 0 and keeping Φ ≥ 0 guarantees ∑ real costᵢ ≤ ∑ âᵢ.  
Thus both methods prove that even if some individual operations are costly, the average cost per operation over any sequence stays within the chosen amortized bound (e.g., O(1) for stack push/pop or dynamic-array insertions).

Amortized analysis estimates the average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence, guaranteeing that the total paid never exceeds the total actual work.  
In the accounting (banker’s) method, we assign an artificial “charge” to each operation: some operations are deliberately overcharged, creating stored “credits,” while others are undercharged, spending those credits.  
We prove correctness by showing (1) every operation always has enough saved credits to pay its true cost and (2) the sum of the assigned charges bounds the real total cost; the amortized cost is simply the fixed charge we chose per operation.  

The potential (physicist’s) method encodes these credits in a global numeric potential Φ(state) ≥ 0.  
Define amortized_cost(i) = actual_cost(i) + Φ(after i) − Φ(before i).  
If Φ initially is 0 and never negative, summing over n operations telescopes: Σ amortized_cost = Σ actual_cost + Φ(final) − Φ(initial) ≥ Σ actual_cost.  
Thus the average amortized_cost bounds the average real cost, yet can be easier to compute.  
Choosing Φ so that expensive operations sharply lower potential (spending stored work) and cheap ones raise it (saving work) yields tight amortized bounds, e.g., O(1) for stack operations with periodic resizing or for table doubling.

Amortized analysis bounds the average cost of each operation over any worst-case sequence.  
In the accounting (potential) method we assign an artificial charge to every operation and store surplus as a “bank balance” or potential Φ.  
The amortized cost ĉₖ of operation k is defined as its real cost cₖ plus the change in potential, ĉₖ = cₖ + (Φ_after − Φ_before).  
Φ starts at 0, is always non-negative, and represents prepaid work that expensive operations may spend later.  
Because Σĉₖ = Σcₖ + (Φ_final − Φ_initial) ≥ Σcₖ, the amortized costs upper-bound the true total cost, so their average gives a worst-case per-operation guarantee.  
To apply the method choose Φ so that cheap operations deposit extra potential while rare expensive ones withdraw it.  
Example: in a dynamic array that doubles on overflow, let Φ = 2n − m (n = items, m = capacity); each insertion is charged 3 units, covering the occasional O(n) resize and ensuring O(1) amortized time.  
Thus the technique converts irregular high costs into a stable per-operation bound without randomness or averaging across different input sequences.

In a Fibonacci heap, a decrease-key operation simply lowers the key of a node and, if the heap order is violated, cuts that node (and possibly some marked ancestors) from its parent and moves each cut node to the root list. These cuts, pointer changes, and potential mark/unmark steps take O(1) actual time each. The heap postpones any tree-degree rebalancing until the next extract-min, so no consolidation work is done during decrease-key. The amortized analysis uses the potential Φ = (#trees in the root list) + 2·(#marked non-root nodes). Cutting a child increases #trees by 1 but decreases the parent’s degree, so Φ rises by at most 1; unmarking a node lowers Φ by 2, offsetting the constant cost of the cut. A cascading cut stops as soon as it reaches an unmarked parent, and every node can become marked, cut, and unmarked at most once between consecutive decreases of its key, bounding total future cuts. Hence the net potential change per decrease-key is O(1), yielding O(1) amortized time.

A decrease-key simply lowers a node’s key, and if the heap order is violated the node is cut from its parent and moved to the root list, an O(1) actual action.  
Each node is allowed to lose only one child without being cut; the second loss triggers its own cut (marking and cascading cuts).  
Thus a single decrease-key may trigger a short cascade, but every cascade cuts a node that had previously been marked, so a node is cut at most once per child it loses.  
Define the potential Φ = (# of trees in the root list) + 2·(# of marked nodes).  
Cutting a node increases the number of trees by 1 (ΔΦ = +1) but removes a mark (ΔΦ = −2), yielding a net potential change of at most −1.  
Therefore the amortized cost of each cut is 1 (actual) + (−1) (potential) = 0, and the whole decrease-key has amortized cost O(1).  
Because the costly consolidations are postponed to later delete-min operations, and the potential function captures the work “saved up,” every sequence of n decrease-key operations contributes only O(n) total time.  
Hence, Fibonacci heaps achieve O(1) amortized time per decrease-key.

A decrease-key sets a node’s key and, if the heap order is violated, cuts that node from its parent, placing it in the root list.  
This cut—together with the constant-time pointer updates for the new key—takes O(1) actual time.  
If the parent was previously unmarked it is merely marked; otherwise a “cascading cut” recursively detaches the parent and continues upward, each step also costing O(1).  
To bound the total work, use the potential Φ = (#roots) + 2·(#marked nodes).  
A single cut increases #roots by 1 (ΔΦ ≤ 1) and, when it marks the parent, raises Φ by at most 2 more, so ΔΦ ≤ 3.  
A cascading cut both removes a mark (–2 potential) and adds a root (+1), giving ΔΦ ≤ –1; thus each extra cut actually lowers potential.  
Therefore the amortized cost of the first cut is O(1) actual + O(ΔΦ) = O(1), and every additional cascading cut contributes negative amortized cost.  
Summing these effects, the entire decrease-key operation has O(1) amortized time in a Fibonacci heap.

A decrease-key operation only changes one key and, if the heap order is violated, repeatedly “cuts’’ the offending node from its parent and moves it to the root list.  
Each cut, including the possible cascading cuts, requires only constant pointer changes.  
The amortized analysis uses the potential Φ = (number of trees in the root list) + 2 × (number of marked, i.e., once-cut, nodes).  
A single cut increases the root-list size by 1 (raising Φ by 1) and may unmark its parent (lowering Φ by 2), so its net potential change is ≤ 0 after the first cut.  
Subsequent cascading cuts each lower the number of marked nodes, reducing Φ by at least 1 per cut.  
Therefore, although an operation that triggers k cuts performs O(k) actual work, its potential drops by at least k − 1, giving amortized cost O(k) + ΔΦ = O(1).  
Because every decrease-key is analyzed this way, the amortized time per decrease-key in a Fibonacci heap is Θ(1).

A decrease-key only (1) updates the key, (2) cuts the node from its parent if the heap-order is violated, and (3) repeatedly “cascading-cuts” marked ancestors until either an unmarked ancestor is reached or the root is encountered.  
Each individual cut and mark/unmark touches a constant number of pointers, so its real cost is O(1).  
The potential function Φ = (number of trees in the root list) + 2·(number of marked nodes) rises by at most 1 for the first cut (adds one tree, removes a mark) and drops by at least 1 for every additional cascading cut (because a marked node becomes unmarked when cut).  
Hence the net potential change for the whole operation is ≤ +1.  
Because the actual work per cut is O(1) and the total potential increase is bounded by a constant, the amortized cost of decrease-key is O(1).

In a Fibonacci heap, decrease-key cuts the node whose key was reduced from its parent (if the heap-order property is violated) and places it in the root list; if the parent had already lost a child it is also cut, causing a cascading cut up the tree.  
Each individual cut requires only a constant number of pointer changes.  
No re-balancing of degrees is done now; that work is deferred to the next extract-min, so the immediate cost per cut is O(1).  
For amortized analysis use Φ = (#roots) + 2·(#marked nodes).  
A single cut raises the root count by ≤1 and may create at most one new mark, so Φ can increase by at most 3.  
Every further cascading cut removes a previously marked node, decreasing Φ by 2, which “pays” for the O(1) real work of the cut.  
Thus the total amortized cost of all cuts triggered by one decrease-key is bounded by a constant.  
Consequently, decrease-key in a Fibonacci heap runs in O(1) amortized time.

In a Fibonacci heap, a decrease-key first updates the key and, if the heap order is violated, performs a single cut that moves the node (and its subtree) to the root list.  
If the node’s parent was already marked, cascading cuts continue up the tree; otherwise the parent is simply marked.  
The potential function Φ = #roots + 2·#marked captures stored work.  
Creating a new root increases Φ by at most 1, and marking a parent increases Φ by 2.  
Each cascading cut removes one mark (decreasing Φ by 2) while adding at most one new root (increasing Φ by 1), so Φ falls by at least 1 per extra cut.  
Because a node is marked only after its first child is lost and is cut (and unmarked) after its second child is lost, any node can participate in at most O(1) cuts per key decrease over its lifetime.  
Thus the actual cost of k extra cuts is paid by an equal or greater drop in potential, yielding an amortized cost of O(1) for decrease-key.

Let n = |V| and m = |E|.  
Using a binary heap, each Extract-Min and Decrease-Key is O(log n), so n Extract-Min + m Decrease-Key gives Θ((n + m) log n) ≈ Θ(m log n) for connected graphs.  
With a Fibonacci heap, Extract-Min is O(log n) amortized and Decrease-Key is O(1) amortized, yielding Θ(n log n + m).  
Hence both versions are Θ(n log n) on sparse graphs (m = O(n)), but on dense graphs the Fibonacci heap improves the m term from Θ(m log n) to Θ(m).  
Summarized: Binary heap ⇒ Θ(m log n); Fibonacci heap ⇒ Θ(n log n + m).  
Theoretical gain comes at the cost of higher constants and complexity, so binary heaps often win in practical implementations despite the asymptotic advantage of Fibonacci heaps.

With |V| vertices and |E| edges (non-negative weights), Dijkstra implemented with  
1. a binary heap performs |V| Extract-Min operations (each O(log |V|)) and up to |E| Decrease-Key operations (each O(log |V|)), yielding Θ((|V|+|E|) log |V|) = Θ(|E| log |V|).  
2. a Fibonacci heap performs the same |V| Extract-Mins at O(log |V|) amortized each and |E| Decrease-Keys at O(1) amortized each, giving Θ(|V| log |V| + |E|).  

Hence, on sparse graphs where |E| = Θ(|V|), both run in Θ(|V| log |V|); on dense graphs where |E| = Θ(|V|²), the Fibonacci-heap variant improves from Θ(|V|² log |V|) to Θ(|V|²). In practice, binary heaps often outperform Fibonacci heaps despite the worse asymptotic bound because of simpler structure and lower constant factors.

With a binary heap, Dijkstra performs V Extract-Min and up to E Decrease-Key operations, each costing O(log V), so the total running time is O((V + E) log V) ≈ O(E log V).  
Using a Fibonacci heap, Extract-Min costs O(log V) amortized and Decrease-Key costs O(1) amortized, giving a total of O(V log V + E).  
For sparse graphs (E ≈ V) the two bounds are both Θ(V log V) up to constant factors.  
For dense graphs (E ≈ V²) the Fibonacci heap version runs in Θ(V²) while the binary-heap version needs Θ(V² log V), a logarithmic factor slower.  
Hence, Fibonacci heaps improve Dijkstra’s asymptotic complexity by eliminating the log V factor from the E Decrease-Key operations, but only yield significant gains on graphs whose edge set is super-linear in the number of vertices.

Using a binary (binary-heap) priority queue, Dijkstra’s algorithm performs V EXTRACT-MIN operations at O(log V) each and up to E DECREASE-KEY operations also at O(log V) each, giving total time Θ((V + E) log V) ≈ Θ(E log V) for connected graphs.  
Replacing the queue with a Fibonacci heap keeps EXTRACT-MIN at O(log V) amortized but reduces each DECREASE-KEY to O(1) amortized, so the overall time becomes Θ(V log V + E).  
Therefore, the Fibonacci-heap version is asymptotically faster when the graph is sparse enough that E is much larger than V (e.g., E ≫ V log V), because the costly DECREASE-KEY operations dominate in a binary heap.  
When E is Θ(V log V) or smaller, both heaps give similar performance, and for dense graphs (E ≈ V²) the asymptotic advantage of Fibonacci heaps disappears.  
In practice, because of high constants and cache effects, binary heaps are often preferred unless the graph is extremely large and sparse.

With a binary (binary) heap implementation, Dijkstra’s algorithm performs V Extract-Min operations and up to E Decrease-Key operations, each costing Θ(log V), giving a total running time of Θ((V + E) log V).  
With a Fibonacci heap, Extract-Min is Θ(log V) amortized but Decrease-Key is Θ(1) amortized, so the total running time becomes Θ(V log V + E) = Θ(E + V log V).  
Thus the Fibonacci heap removes the log V factor from the E Decrease-Key calls, making it asymptotically faster whenever E is ω(V).  
For sparse graphs where E = Θ(V), both variants run in Θ(V log V).  
For denser graphs (e.g., E ≈ V²) the Fibonacci version is Θ(V²) while the binary-heap version is Θ(V² log V), saving a factor of log V.

Let n be the number of vertices and m the number of edges.  
Binary-heap implementation: n extract-min operations cost Θ(n log n) and up to m decrease-key operations cost Θ(m log n), giving total Θ((n + m) log n) ≈ Θ(m log n) for connected graphs.  
Fibonacci-heap implementation: n extract-mins cost Θ(n log n) while m decrease-keys cost Θ(m) amortized, yielding Θ(n log n + m).  
Therefore a Fibonacci heap improves Dijkstra’s running time from Θ(m log n) to Θ(n log n + m), a clear asymptotic gain when the graph is sparse (m » n).  
For dense graphs where m ≈ n², both approaches are Θ(n² log n), and in practice the simpler binary heap often outperforms due to lower constants.

With a binary heap, each of V extract-min operations costs O(log V) and up to E decrease-key operations each cost O(log V), giving Dijkstra’s total time O(V log V + E log V) = O((V+E) log V).  
With a Fibonacci heap, extract-min is O(log V) amortized but decrease-key is O(1) amortized, so the total becomes O(V log V + E).  
Hence the Fibonacci heap saves a factor of log V on every decrease-key, making it asymptotically faster on sparse graphs where E≫V.  
For dense graphs where E = Θ(V²), both implementations run in Θ(V²) time, so the practical advantage of Fibonacci heaps diminishes.

Dijkstra’s algorithm always selects the currently “closest” unsettled vertex and permanently fixes its distance, assuming no later path will be shorter. This assumption only holds when every edge weight is non-negative, because adding more edges can only increase or leave unchanged an already minimal cost. If a graph contains a negative edge, a path that reaches a settled vertex later could subtract cost and create a total distance smaller than the one Dijkstra has already locked in. Since the algorithm never revisits or relaxes a settled vertex, it cannot correct this error. Consequently, the final distances and any paths derived from them may be incorrect.

Dijkstra’s algorithm relies on the “greedy” property that once the vertex with the smallest tentative distance is removed from the priority queue, no later relaxation can reduce that distance. This holds only when every edge weight is non-negative: any path that reaches the vertex later must be at least as long as the distance already recorded, because all additional edges add zero or positive cost. If a negative edge exists, a vertex can be finalized early and then be reached through a different route whose total cost is smaller due to the negative weight, contradicting the algorithm’s assumption. Since Dijkstra never revisits a vertex after it is extracted, it misses this new shorter path and returns an incorrect shortest-path tree. Thus the presence of even a single negative edge invalidates the core invariant that guarantees Dijkstra’s correctness.

Dijkstra’s algorithm permanently “locks in” the distance of a vertex when it is first removed from the priority queue, on the assumption that any alternative path reaching that vertex later must be at least as long because every remaining edge weight is non-negative. If a graph contains a negative edge, a vertex that has already been finalized can still be reached through a yet-unexplored path whose total cost is lower due to that negative edge. Since the algorithm never revisits or relaxes finalized vertices, it cannot update their distances, so subsequent computations are based on incorrect values. This premature commitment propagates errors through the relaxation steps, causing some shortest-path estimates to stay too large and producing wrong overall results. Therefore, the core relaxation rule remains sound, but the correctness proof that depends on the non-negativity of edge weights breaks, making Dijkstra unreliable whenever negative edges exist.

Dijkstra’s algorithm assumes that once the currently-known shortest distance to a vertex v is the smallest among all unsettled vertices, no future path can make v shorter, so v can be “fixed” forever. This assumption is justified only when all edge weights are non-negative, because any alternative path to v would first have to traverse additional non-negative edges and therefore could not reduce its distance. If a negative edge exists, a vertex that appears to have the minimum tentative distance can later be reached through another unsettled vertex plus a negative edge, yielding a lower total cost. Dijkstra’s algorithm never re-examines vertices it has already fixed, so it will miss this improvement and output suboptimal distances. Therefore, negative weights violate the algorithm’s fundamental “greedy choice” guarantee, causing incorrect results.

Dijkstra’s algorithm assumes path costs never decrease as additional edges are appended. With all non-negative weights, the first time a vertex is extracted from the priority queue its tentative distance is guaranteed to be minimal, so the algorithm can safely “finalize” it and never look back. A negative edge breaks that property: a vertex may initially seem farther away, be finalized, and later be reached through an unprocessed vertex plus a negative edge for a lower total cost. Because Dijkstra never revisits finalized vertices, it fails to update this shorter distance and returns an incorrect result. Thus the core greedy step that underpins the algorithm’s correctness is invalid whenever negative edge weights exist.

Dijkstra’s algorithm assumes that every time it permanently selects the current minimum-distance vertex, no future relaxation can produce a shorter path to that vertex. This assumption holds only when all edge weights are non-negative, because adding any further edge can only increase or leave unchanged the already minimal distance. With a negative edge, a vertex that was earlier settled may later be reached through another path whose total cost is smaller due to that negative weight, contradicting the algorithm’s “settled-means-optimal” property. Since Dijkstra never revisits settled vertices, it will not update their distances, causing incorrect final shortest-path values. Hence, the algorithm’s greedy choice is invalidated whenever edges with negative weight exist.

Dijkstra repeatedly chooses the unsettled vertex with the smallest current distance and then “locks in” that distance, assuming no future relaxation can lower it. This assumption is valid only when every edge weight is non-negative, because any extension of a path can only increase or leave unchanged its length. If a graph contains a negative edge, a vertex whose distance has been locked may later be reached through a different path whose total length is smaller due to that negative edge. Since Dijkstra never revisits or relaxes a settled vertex, it will keep the incorrect larger distance and propagate it to subsequent computations, producing wrong shortest-path results. A one-edge negative cycle is not required; a single negative edge arriving after the vertex is settled is enough to break the guarantee.

Maintain the loop invariant: whenever a vertex v is removed from the priority queue and placed in the settled set S, dist[v] equals the true shortest-path distance from the source s to v, and every unsettled vertex w has dist[w] equal to the length of the shortest s-w path whose interior vertices lie entirely in S. 2) The invariant holds initially because S is empty, dist[s]=0, and all other distances are ∞. 3) Assume it holds before an iteration and let u (with minimum dist) be extracted. 4) If a shorter s-u path existed, let x be the first vertex on that path not in S and y its predecessor in S; then length(s-u) ≥ dist[y]+w(y,x). 5) When y was settled, relaxation ensured dist[x] ≤ dist[y]+w(y,x), so dist[u] ≤ dist[x] ≤ length(s-u), contradicting the supposed shorter path. 6) Thus dist[u] is optimal, preserving the invariant. 7) Subsequent relaxations may decrease dist for unsettled vertices but never alter settled ones, so the invariant continues to hold. 8) By induction the invariant is true at every removal, and when the queue is empty every vertex is settled. 9) Consequently each recorded distance equals the weight of the shortest path from s to that vertex. 10) Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm is correct.

Let S be the set of vertices whose distance has been “settled.”  
Invariant: after every iteration (1) for every v ∈ S, d[v] = δ(s,v) (the true shortest distance), and (2) for every v ∉ S, d[v] equals the length of the shortest s–v path whose internal vertices all lie in S\{v}.  
The invariant is initially true: S = ∅, d[s] = 0, and all other d[v] = ∞.  
Choose u ∉ S with minimum d; consider a shortest path P from s to u and let y be the first vertex of P outside S (with predecessor z ∈ S).  
By the invariant, d[z] = δ(s,z); thus δ(s,y) = δ(s,z) + w(z,y) = d[z] + w(z,y).  
Relaxation keeps d[y] ≤ d[z] + w(z,y), so d[y] ≤ δ(s,y) ≤ δ(s,u).  
Because u minimizes d outside S, d[u] ≤ d[y] ≤ δ(s,u); but relaxation always gives δ(s,u) ≤ d[u], hence d[u] = δ(s,u).  
Adding u to S and relaxing its outgoing edges maintains the invariant.  
By induction the invariant holds when S includes every vertex, so each final d[v] equals δ(s,v).  
Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm correctly computes all single-source shortest-path distances for graphs with non-negative edge weights.

Dijkstra maintains a set S of vertices whose shortest‐path lengths from the source s have been permanently fixed.  
Loop invariant: (i) for every u∈S, dist[u]=δ(s,u); (ii) for every v∉S, dist[v] equals the length of the shortest s–v path whose internal vertices lie in S, so dist[v]≤δ(s,v).  
The invariant holds initially because S=∅, dist[s]=0, and dist[v]=∞ for v≠s.  
When the algorithm selects the vertex x with minimal dist[] in V∖S, suppose a shorter path P to x exists whose first vertex not in S is y (possibly x).  
Because edge weights are non-negative, the s–y prefix of P has length ≥dist[y], and the choice of x implies dist[x]≤dist[y], yielding dist[x]<dist[x], a contradiction.  
Therefore dist[x]=δ(s,x), preserving (i) when x is added to S.  
Relaxing edges from x sets each remaining vertex v’s label to the length of the shortest s–v path whose internal vertices are in S∪{x}, maintaining (ii).  
By induction the invariant holds after every iteration; when the queue empties we have S=V and dist[v]=δ(s,v) for all v.  
Hence Dijkstra’s algorithm always outputs correct single-source shortest-path distances for graphs with non-negative edge weights.

Let δ(s,v) be the true shortest-path length from source s to v.
