Invariant: after any iteration (a) for every settled vertex u, d[u]=δ(s,u); (b) for every unsettled vertex v, d[v] ≥ δ(s,v).  
Initialization satisfies the invariant because only s is settled with d[s]=0=δ(s,s) and all other labels are ∞.  
Assume the invariant holds and let u be the unsettled vertex chosen next (minimum d).  
Any s→u path that first reaches an unsettled vertex must be at least d[u] long, because that first unsettled vertex has label ≥ d[u]; thus no path to u is shorter than d[u], so d[u]=δ(s,u) and u may be safely settled, preserving part (a).  
For every edge (u,v) relaxed, the new label d′[v]=min(d[v],d[u]+w(u,v)) is at least δ(s,v) since d[u]=δ(s,u) and w(u,v)≥0, so part (b) is preserved.  
By induction, the invariant holds throughout; when the algorithm ends all vertices are settled, giving d[v]=δ(s,v) for every v.  
Predecessor pointers recorded during relaxation therefore trace actual shortest paths, proving Dijkstra’s algorithm correct for graphs with non-negative edge weights.

Invariant: after k iterations, every vertex in the settled set S has dist [v] equal to the length of the shortest s-v path, and every unsettled vertex has its dist [] equal to the length of the shortest s-v path that uses only vertices in S as internal nodes.  
Base case k = 0 holds because S = { } and dist [s] = 0 is the true shortest s-s path.  
Inductive step: assume the invariant holds after k iterations; let u be the vertex with minimum dist extracted next.  
For contradiction, suppose there exists a shorter s-u path P going through some first unsettled vertex w.  
All edges on P before w lie in S, so the prefix length equals dist [w] when w was first reached, hence dist [w] ≤ length(P).  
Because u was chosen with minimum tentative distance, dist [u] ≤ dist [w] ≤ length(P), contradicting that P is shorter.  
Thus dist [u] is optimal; adding u to S preserves the invariant.  
By induction the invariant holds when S contains all vertices, so every dist [v] returned by the algorithm is the true shortest-path length.

Maintain the invariant: (i) every vertex u already “settled’’ in the set S has dist[u] equal to the true shortest-path length from the source s, and (ii) for every vertex v∉S, dist[v] equals the length of the shortest s–v path whose interior vertices lie in S.  
The invariant holds initially because S = {s} with dist[s]=0 and no other vertex has a path consisting solely of vertices in S.  
Assume it holds before an iteration and let x be the outside vertex of minimum dist chosen next.  
Any s–x path must leave S at some vertex y; by (i) the prefix s→y is at least dist[y], and the non-negative suffix y→x makes the whole path length ≥ dist[y]+w(y,x) ≥ dist[x].  
Thus no path is shorter than dist[x]; dist[x] is optimal and adding x to S preserves (i).  
Relaxing edges out of x can only shorten labels for vertices still outside S, so (ii) is restored.  
By induction the invariant is true after every iteration, and when the algorithm stops S contains all vertices with correct shortest distances.  
Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm always returns correct shortest-path lengths and the associated predecessor pointers form a shortest-path tree.

Maintain set S of vertices whose distances are “settled.”  
Invariant: (1) for every v ∈ S, d[v]=δ(s,v); (2) for every v ∉ S, d[v] equals the length of the shortest s–v path whose interior vertices lie in S.  
The invariant is true initially because S = {s} with d[s]=0.  
Assume it holds at the start of an iteration and let u be the vertex with minimum d extracted and added to S.  
Suppose, toward a contradiction, that some path P gives a length L<d[u]; let y be the last vertex of P in S and x=u the first outside S.  
Because the prefix s…y is settled, its length is d[y]=δ(s,y), so L ≥ d[y]+w(y,x).  
Relaxation on edge (y,x) already set d[x] ≤ d[y]+w(y,x) < d[u], contradicting the choice of u as the minimum tentative distance.  
Thus no shorter path to u exists, proving d[u]=δ(s,u) and preserving the invariant.  
By induction, when the queue empties every vertex is settled with d[v]=δ(s,v).  
Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm correctly returns shortest-path distances for graphs with non-negative edge weights.

With an adjacency-list representation, both BFS and DFS examine every vertex and edge exactly once, giving a time complexity of O(V + E). With an adjacency-matrix representation, scanning all neighbors costs Θ(V) per vertex, resulting in O(V²). Thus their asymptotic running times are identical for the same representation; only traversal order differs. Memory use: BFS needs O(V) for its queue, while DFS uses O(V) for its explicit or recursive stack.

With an adjacency-list representation, BFS enqueues each vertex once and examines each edge once, yielding Θ(|V| + |E|) time; DFS, using recursion or an explicit stack, likewise visits every vertex and traverses every edge once, so its time complexity is also Θ(|V| + |E|). If the graph is stored as an adjacency matrix, checking all possible neighbors inside the loops costs Θ(|V|²), making both BFS and DFS Θ(|V|²) regardless of the actual number of edges. The queue used by BFS and the stack used by DFS add only Θ(|V|) extra operations, which do not affect these asymptotic bounds.

Using an adjacency-list representation, both Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) visit every vertex and inspect every edge at most once, giving a time complexity of O(V + E), where V is the number of vertices and E the number of edges. With an adjacency-matrix representation, scanning all incident edges of each vertex costs Θ(V²), so both algorithms become Θ(V²) in that case regardless of E. BFS has an additional O(V) queue-operation overhead, and DFS has an O(V) recursion (or stack) overhead, but these do not change the asymptotic bounds.

With an adjacency-list representation, both Breadth-First Search and Depth-First Search examine every vertex and every edge at most once, giving Θ(V + E) time.  
With an adjacency-matrix representation, scanning all possible neighbors of every vertex costs Θ(V²) time for either algorithm.

For a graph with V vertices and E edges stored in an adjacency-list, both BFS and DFS visit every vertex once and scan every edge once, giving Θ(V + E) time.  
If the graph is stored in an adjacency-matrix, each algorithm still visits all vertices but now inspects every possible edge, yielding Θ(V²) time.

Let V be the number of vertices and E the number of edges.  
1. With adjacency-list storage, Breadth-First Search enqueues each vertex once and examines every incident edge once, yielding O(V + E) time.  
2. With adjacency-matrix storage, BFS must scan an entire row for each vertex, giving O(V²) time.  
3. Depth-First Search, whether implemented recursively or with an explicit stack, also touches every vertex and edge exactly once in a list representation, so its running time is O(V + E).  
4. Using a matrix, DFS likewise incurs O(V²) because exploring a vertex requires scanning its whole row.  
5. In both algorithms the dominant factor is the cost of inspecting adjacency information; the search order (queue vs. stack) does not change the asymptotic bound.

With an adjacency-list representation both BFS and DFS examine every vertex and edge at most once, giving Θ(V + E) time.  
With an adjacency-matrix representation each algorithm scans every row of the matrix, so the time rises to Θ(V²).  
In undirected graphs each edge may be processed twice (once from each endpoint) but the Θ(V + E) bound holds.  
Hence BFS and DFS share identical asymptotic time complexity; they differ only in the order of exploration, not in big-O cost.

Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores a graph level by level, visiting all vertices at distance k from the start before moving to distance k + 1 and is usually implemented with a queue.  
Depth-First Search (DFS) follows one branch as far as possible before backtracking, using recursion or an explicit stack.  
BFS guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph and is complete for finite graphs.  
Its main cost is memory: the frontier can grow to O(|V|) in the worst case.  
Typical BFS uses include shortest-path finding in unweighted networks, computing vertex levels, discovering connected components, and optimal-move puzzle solving.  
DFS requires only O(h) memory, where h is maximum depth, but it can pursue long dead-end paths and does not ensure minimal solutions without modification.  
DFS excels at tasks like topological sorting, cycle detection, maze or puzzle generation, enumerating all solutions, and existence checks for deep paths.  
In short, pick BFS when the shallowest solution matters and memory is available; pick DFS when memory is tight or depth-oriented analysis is needed.

Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores a graph level by level using a queue, guaranteeing that the first time you reach a vertex you have found the shortest (fewest-edge) path from the start.  
Depth-First Search (DFS) dives as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using recursion or an explicit stack, so the first found path is not necessarily the shortest but exploration needs far less bookkeeping.  
BFS typically needs O(V) memory for its queue of frontier vertices, while DFS can run in O(h) memory, where h is the maximum depth reached.  
Because of its level-ordered nature, BFS is preferred for shortest-path discovery in unweighted graphs, minimum hops in network routing, social-network friend suggestions, and AI problems where the shallowest solution is desired.  
DFS is favored for topological sorting of a DAG, cycle detection, strong-/connected-component identification, solving puzzles by backtracking (e.g., Sudoku, N-Queens, maze generation), and situations where memory is tight or solutions are likely to be deep.  
BFS naturally lends itself to parallel expansion of layers, while DFS is inherently sequential but can be adapted for iterative deepening.  
In summary, choose BFS when shortest paths or layer-wise processing matter and memory is ample; choose DFS when depth exploration, low memory usage, or ordering tasks like topological sort are the priorities.

BFS (Breadth-First Search) visits vertices level by level from a start node using a queue, ensuring the first time you reach a vertex you have taken the minimum number of edges.  
DFS (Depth-First Search) follows one branch as deep as possible using recursion or an explicit stack, then backtracks to explore alternative paths.  
BFS typically consumes O(V) memory because it stores every node on the current frontier, while DFS needs only O(depth) memory corresponding to the longest path explored.  
Choose BFS when you need shortest paths in an unweighted graph, to test connectivity, to compute distances, or to find the nearest resource or goal.  
Choose DFS for tasks requiring exhaustive exploration such as topological sorting, cycle detection, strong or connected component discovery, puzzle solving with backtracking, and maze or map generation.  
BFS is advantageous when a solution is likely to be close to the start node; DFS is often better when solutions lie deep or when memory is constrained.  
Both run in O(V + E) time, but their differing space demands and traversal orders dictate their typical use cases.

Breadth-First Search (BFS) expands all vertices at the current depth before moving deeper, using a queue, so it naturally discovers the shortest (fewest-edge) paths in an unweighted graph and visits vertices level-by-level.  
Depth-First Search (DFS) follows one branch as far as possible before backtracking, using recursion or an explicit stack, producing a LIFO exploration order.  
BFS’s level ordering makes it ideal for finding the shortest path or minimum number of moves, testing graph connectivity, generating level-order (breadth-wise) tree traversals, routing in peer-to-peer networks, and solving problems like “six degrees of separation.”  
DFS’s deep exploration is well-suited for topological sorting of DAGs, detecting cycles, finding articulation points/bridges, generating maze paths, solving puzzles that require backtracking (e.g., Sudoku, N-Queens), and enumerating all possible paths.  
Both run in O(V + E) time, but BFS usually needs O(V) space for its queue, whereas DFS often needs only O(h) space (h = maximum recursion depth) if implemented recursively.  
BFS guarantees the first time a vertex is dequeued it has the shortest path from the source; DFS does not guarantee minimal paths.  
DFS naturally produces preorder, inorder, or postorder traversals on trees, while BFS produces level-order traversal.  
Thus, choose BFS when shortest paths or uniform exploration are required and DFS when deep exploration, ordering, or backtracking structure is needed.

Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores a graph level by level using a queue, guaranteeing the first time you reach a node you have found the shortest (fewest-edge) path to it in an unweighted graph.  
Depth-First Search (DFS) dives down a path as far as possible using a stack or recursion, then backtracks, so it does not guarantee shortest paths but can run with very little extra memory if the graph is sparse.  
Because BFS discovers nodes in increasing distance order, it is preferred for shortest-path finding in unweighted graphs, peer-to-peer or social-network “degree of separation” queries, broadcast/multicast routing, and level-order traversal of trees.  
DFS is preferred when you need to exhaustively explore or enumerate structures, such as topological sorting of DAGs, detecting cycles, finding connected components, solving puzzles or mazes via backtracking, and generating all possible paths or configurations.  
BFS’s queue can grow to hold all nodes on a frontier, which may be large, while DFS’s recursion/stack usually stores only the current path, making it memory-lighter on very wide graphs but potentially deeper in recursion.  
Both run in O(V+E) time, but their systematic ordering—BFS by depth, DFS by path—makes each better suited to the above distinct classes of problems.

BFS (Breadth-First Search) scans a graph level by level from a start node, using a queue to visit all neighbors before moving deeper.  
DFS (Depth-First Search) follows one branch as far as it can before backtracking, using recursion or an explicit stack.  
Because BFS expands uniformly, the first time it reaches a vertex it has found the shortest path (fewest edges) in an unweighted graph, while DFS offers no such guarantee.  
BFS may need memory proportional to the width of the graph (entire frontier), whereas DFS needs memory proportional only to the current path depth.  
BFS naturally yields level numbers and parent pointers useful for shortest-path trees, reachability within k steps, and broadcast/simulation tasks.  
DFS naturally yields discovery/finish times that enable topological sorting, cycle detection, articulation-point and strongly-connected-component algorithms.  
Typical BFS applications: finding the minimum number of moves in a maze, routing in unweighted networks, “friend-of-a-friend” suggestions, and AI state-space searches where minimal cost equals minimal steps.  
Typical DFS applications: dependency resolution (compile order), puzzle solvers with backtracking (Sudoku, N-Queens), maze generation, detecting cycles in graphs, and analyzing graph connectivity.  
Both run in O(V + E) time; choose BFS when shortest paths or level information matter, and DFS when deep exploration, backtracking, or lower memory footprint are more important.

Breadth-first search (BFS) explores all neighbors of a vertex before moving to the next level using a queue, while depth-first search (DFS) follows one path as far as possible using a stack or recursion and backtracks when necessary.  
Both run in O(V + E) time, but BFS typically stores O(V) vertices in its frontier, whereas DFS needs only O(h) memory, where h is the current recursion depth.  
BFS is complete and guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph; DFS may reach a goal faster on some instances but can wander indefinitely in deep or infinite graphs.  
Typical BFS uses: single-source shortest paths, level-order traversal of trees, minimum-move puzzle solving, network broadcast simulation, and bipartite checking.  
Typical DFS uses: topological sorting, cycle detection, maze generation/solving, finding articulation points or bridges, and computing strongly connected components.  
BFS naturally outputs each vertex’s distance from the source, whereas DFS provides discovery and finishing times that underpin many structural graph analyses.  
In tree contexts, BFS yields breadth-wise layers; DFS yields preorder, inorder, or postorder sequences.  
Choose BFS when shortest distance or breadth coverage is critical and memory is acceptable; choose DFS when memory is tight or when algorithms rely on entry/exit order along deep paths.

BFS starts by putting the source s in a FIFO queue with dist[s]=0; whenever a vertex u is dequeued, every undiscovered neighbor v is given dist[v]=dist[u]+1 and is enqueued. Because the queue is first-in-first-out, all vertices at distance k are dequeued (and expand their neighbors) before any vertex at distance k+1 is dequeued. Hence, by induction on k, when a vertex v is first removed from the queue its label dist[v]=k equals the length of some path found by BFS from s to v. Suppose a shorter path of length k′<k existed; let w be the predecessor of v on that path, so dist(s,w)=k′−1. Vertex w would have been dequeued before any vertex at distance ≥k′, therefore before v. When w was processed it would have discovered v and set dist[v]≤k′, contradicting dist[v]=k. Thus no shorter path exists, and dist[v] is the true shortest-path length. The predecessor pointers recorded on each discovery edge therefore reconstruct actual shortest paths, so BFS always finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.

In an unweighted graph each edge costs 1.  
BFS explores vertices layer by layer: all vertices at distance 1 are dequeued before any at distance 2, those at distance 2 before any at distance 3, and so on.  
The FIFO queue guarantees this ordering because vertices are enqueued only from currently dequeued vertices’ neighbors.  
A vertex’s distance and predecessor are fixed the first time it is enqueued.  
Assume for contradiction that BFS assigns a vertex w distance d while a shorter path of length k < d exists.  
Let x be the predecessor of w on that shorter path, so x is at distance k − 1 < d − 1.  
Vertex x would have been dequeued before the vertex that set w’s distance, causing w to be enqueued (and thus labeled) with distance k, contradicting d > k.  
Therefore BFS always assigns each vertex its true minimum distance from the source, and the stored predecessors reconstruct a shortest path.

BFS maintains a FIFO queue, so vertices are explored in non-decreasing order of the number of edges from the source.  
When a vertex u is removed from the queue every path to any still-undiscovered neighbor v is exactly dist(u)+1 edges long, where dist(u) is already minimal.  
Because all edges have equal cost 1, any other path to v must contain at least dist(u)+1 edges, so the first time v is discovered its distance is the smallest possible.  
Once v is given a distance label it is never decreased, since later discoveries can only offer paths of equal or greater length.  
Thus, upon termination, the stored distance for every vertex equals the length of a shortest path from the source.  
The predecessor pointers saved at discovery trace back those minimum-length paths.

Let the source be s (distance 0).  
BFS dequeues vertices in non-decreasing order of their recorded distance d, and when a vertex u at distance d is dequeued it assigns each still-undiscovered neighbor w the distance d + 1 and enqueues w.  
Hence every vertex enters the queue exactly once, and all items already in the queue when w is enqueued have distance ≤ d + 1.  
Assume, toward contradiction, that the first time BFS discovers some vertex v it records distance k but a shorter path of length k′<k exists.  
That shorter path would reach v through a predecessor x with distance k′−1<k, so x would have been dequeued before v was discovered, causing v to be enqueued with distance k′—contradicting “first time.”  
Therefore the distance assigned to each vertex when it is first discovered equals the true shortest-path length from s.  
Tracing parent pointers recorded at discovery yields an actual shortest path.

Let s be the start vertex and let d(v) be the length of a shortest s–v path in the unweighted graph.  
BFS enqueues s with label dist(s)=0, then repeatedly dequeues the front vertex u and enqueues every undiscovered neighbor w with label dist(w)=dist(u)+1.  
Induction on the queue order proves that when a vertex u is dequeued, dist(u)=d(u): the base case (u=s) is trivial; for the inductive step, any s–w path must first reach u or another vertex already dequeued, so its length ≥ dist(u)+1=dist(w).  
Thus the first time a vertex is discovered (and hence when it is enqueued) its recorded distance equals the true shortest-path length, and this value never changes.  
Because each vertex is examined exactly once, BFS visits vertices in non-decreasing order of true distance from s.  
Consequently, when the destination t is first dequeued (or discovered), the sequence of parent pointers recorded by BFS yields an s–t path of length dist(t)=d(t), i.e., a shortest path.

Breadth-First Search (BFS) visits vertices in non-decreasing order of their distance (number of edges) from the start vertex s, because it explores all neighbors of every vertex before moving on to vertices at the next “layer.”  
Formally, let d(v) be the true shortest-path distance from s to any vertex v, and let dist(v) be the distance recorded by BFS when v is first dequeued.  
Induction on k = d(v):  
• Base k = 0: s is enqueued with dist(s)=0, so dist(s)=d(s).  
• Inductive step: assume for all vertices with d ≤ k BFS dequeues them with correct distances; take any vertex w with d(w)=k+1. Its predecessor u on a shortest path has d(u)=k, so by the inductive hypothesis u is dequeued before any vertex at layer k+1 is enqueued. When u is processed, BFS enqueues w with dist(w)=dist(u)+1=k+1, and no later relaxation can assign a smaller value because any other path to w would have to pass through a vertex at layer ≥k, yielding length ≥k+1.  
Hence dist(w)=d(w) when w is first removed from the queue, and BFS never later changes distances.  
Therefore every vertex is reached with its exact shortest-path length, and the predecessor pointers recorded by BFS reconstruct a shortest path from s to any reachable vertex.

Let d(v) denote the length of the shortest s-v path in an unweighted graph G, and let dist[v] be the value BFS stores when it first discovers v from the source s.  
Invariant: whenever a vertex u is dequeued, dist[u] = d(u).  
Basis: s is dequeued first with dist[s] = 0 = d(s).  
Inductive step: assume the invariant holds for every vertex dequeued before u; when its predecessor p was dequeued we had dist[p] = d(p).  
BFS discovers u from p along one edge, so dist[u] = dist[p]+1 = d(p)+1.  
Any s-u path must reach the layer containing p before reaching u, hence is at least d(p)+1 edges long; thus dist[u] ≤ d(u) and, combined with the previous equality, dist[u] = d(u).  
Because BFS enqueues vertices in order of discovery and edges all have equal weight 1, every vertex is dequeued once with its minimum possible distance.  
Therefore, when the target vertex t is dequeued, dist[t] equals the length of a shortest s-t path, so BFS always returns a shortest path in an unweighted graph.

Topological sorting orders the vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) so that for every directed edge (u, v) vertex u appears before v in the list.  
A common algorithm uses depth-first search (DFS): perform DFS from every unvisited vertex, and when a vertex finishes, push it onto a stack; popping the stack yields the topological order.  
During DFS each vertex and each outgoing edge is examined exactly once, giving Θ(V + E) time, where V is the number of vertices and E the number of edges.  
Another algorithm employs Kahn’s approach with in-degree counting: repeatedly output any vertex of in-degree 0 and delete its outgoing edges, updating in-degrees.  
Using a queue to manage zero-in-degree vertices, Kahn’s algorithm likewise processes each vertex and edge once, so it also runs in Θ(V + E) time.  
Both algorithms require Θ(V) auxiliary space (for the recursion stack or explicit queue/stack and in-degree array).  
A topological order exists only if the graph is acyclic; detection of a remaining edge or a cycle during these procedures signals failure.

A topological sort of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) is a linear ordering of its vertices in which every directed edge (u, v) places u before v.  
Kahn’s algorithm achieves this by repeatedly enqueueing vertices with in-degree 0, deleting them, and reducing the in-degree of their neighbors; summing the constant-time work for each vertex and edge yields Θ(V + E) time and Θ(V + E) space.  
The depth-first-search method pushes a vertex onto a stack after exploring all outgoing edges; because each vertex and edge is visited once, it also runs in Θ(V + E) time and uses Θ(V + E) space (Θ(V) if edges are stored externally).  
Both algorithms report failure if, after processing, unvisited vertices remain, indicating a cycle, and this detection does not change the asymptotic cost.  
Since any correct procedure must at least inspect every vertex and edge to produce or refute an ordering, the Θ(V + E) bound is optimal for adjacency-list representations.

Topological sorting produces a linear ordering of all vertices in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) such that every directed edge (u → v) places u earlier than v in the order.  
Two standard algorithms achieve this:  

1. Kahn’s algorithm repeatedly removes vertices with in-degree 0, appending them to the output and decrementing the in-degree of their neighbors.  
2. A depth-first search (DFS) marks each vertex and, upon finishing a vertex’s DFS call, pushes it onto a stack; popping the stack yields the topological order.

For both methods every vertex is processed once and every edge is examined once, giving a time complexity of Θ(V + E) and a space requirement of Θ(V). Topological sorting is only possible if the graph has no cycles; detection of an empty queue in Kahn’s algorithm or encountering a back edge in DFS signals a cycle and failure to produce an ordering.

A topological sort of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) lists its vertices in a linear order where every edge u → v places u before v.  
Kahn’s algorithm achieves this by repeatedly removing vertices with indegree 0 and updating indegrees of their neighbors.  
A depth-first search (DFS) alternative pushes each vertex onto a stack after exploring all outgoing edges, then pops the stack to obtain the order.  
Both methods visit each vertex once and inspect every edge once.  
Therefore, their running time is Θ(V + E), where V is the number of vertices and E the number of edges.  
They need Θ(V) extra space for the queue (Kahn) or recursion/stack (DFS).  
If the process finishes before all vertices are output, a cycle exists and no topological ordering is possible.

A topological sort orders the vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) so that every edge u → v places u before v in the sequence.  
Kahn’s algorithm repeatedly deletes vertices with in-degree 0, appends them to the output list, and decreases the in-degree of their neighbors; if no vertex with in-degree 0 remains before all vertices are output, the graph contains a cycle.  
The DFS method performs depth-first search, adds a vertex to a stack once all of its descendants are finished, and finally pops the stack to obtain the order; encountering a “gray” (currently visiting) vertex signals a cycle.  
Both algorithms examine each vertex once and each edge once when the graph is stored in adjacency-list form.  
Therefore their running time is Θ(V + E), where V is the number of vertices and E the number of edges.  
Each method also needs O(V) auxiliary space for queues, stacks, or recursion.

A topological sort of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) is a linear ordering of its vertices such that for every directed edge u → v, vertex u appears before v in the ordering.  
It exists only for DAGs; the presence of a cycle makes such an ordering impossible.  
Two standard algorithms are used: (1) Depth-First Search, which appends each vertex to a list when its DFS finishes, and (2) Kahn’s algorithm, which repeatedly removes vertices of indegree 0.  
In either algorithm every vertex is processed once and every edge is examined once.  
Therefore the running time is Θ(V + E), where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.  
Both approaches require Θ(V) additional space for bookkeeping (recursion stack or queue plus degree counts).

Topological sorting orders the vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) so every edge u → v places u before v. Common methods are Kahn’s BFS-like algorithm and a DFS post-order algorithm. Kahn’s approach repeatedly removes vertices of in-degree 0, appends them to the result, and decrements in-degrees of adjacent vertices. The DFS variant pushes a vertex onto a stack after exploring its descendants, then pops the stack to form the ordering. Both algorithms flag a cycle if no vertex with in-degree 0 exists (Kahn) or a back-edge is detected (DFS). For a graph with V vertices and E edges, each vertex and each edge is processed exactly once, yielding O(V + E) time complexity. Queue or stack operations are O(1) and do not affect this bound. Space complexity is also O(V + E) to hold the graph and auxiliary data.

Let G=(V,E) be a directed graph and let L be the list produced by Kahn’s algorithm, which repeatedly removes a vertex whose current in-degree is 0, appends it to L, and deletes its outgoing edges. Termination is certain because each iteration deletes at least one vertex and |V| is finite. We show by induction that L is a topological order whenever G is acyclic. Base: the first vertex chosen has in-degree 0, so no predecessor precedes it. Induction: assume the first k vertices in L precede all of their out-neighbors; when the (k+1)-st vertex v is selected its in-degree is 0, meaning every predecessor of v has already been deleted and thus appears among the first k vertices, so every edge (u,v) has u before v in L. Hence every edge of G is oriented from an earlier to a later vertex in L, making L a valid topological sort. Conversely, if G contains a directed cycle, every vertex on that cycle always has in-degree ≥1, so no iteration can ever remove any of them and the algorithm stops with |L|<|V|, correctly signalling the absence of a topological order. Therefore the algorithm outputs a complete list exactly when the input graph is a DAG, and that list satisfies the topological-ordering property. The DFS-based algorithm is correct for the same reason: reversing vertex finishing times places each vertex before every out-neighbor discovered later, yielding an identical order. Thus topological sorting algorithms are correct.

In Kahn’s algorithm we maintain the loop invariant: the list L already built is a topological order of the vertices removed so far.  
At the start this is true because L is empty.  
If the current graph has no vertex of in-degree 0 yet is non-empty, every vertex lies on a directed cycle, so the algorithm rightly reports “no topological order.”  
Otherwise the algorithm selects a vertex v of in-degree 0, appends v to L, and deletes v together with its outgoing edges.  
All incoming edges of v have already been eliminated in earlier iterations, so every original edge (u,v) satisfies u ≺ v in L.  
Removing v cannot introduce new edges, hence the reduced graph is still acyclic and must again contain a vertex of in-degree 0, preserving the invariant.  
By induction the invariant holds after each deletion, and when the graph becomes empty L contains every vertex exactly once.  
Consequently L respects every edge of the original DAG and constitutes a valid topological ordering.  
Therefore Kahn’s algorithm outputs a topological sort iff the input graph is acyclic, establishing its correctness.

Let G be a DAG and let L be the list produced by Kahn’s algorithm: repeatedly pick a vertex of in-degree 0, append it to L, and delete it with its outgoing edges.  
Because G is acyclic, at least one vertex has in-degree 0; otherwise every vertex would have an ancestor, and tracing predecessors would create a directed cycle.  
Thus the algorithm can always choose a vertex until the graph is empty, so it outputs |V| vertices and terminates.  
When an edge (u, v) exists, v is still present when u is deleted, so u is placed in L before v.  
Hence every edge points forward in L, giving the required partial-order extension.  
Inductively, this forward-edge property holds after each deletion, so the final list L is a valid topological ordering.  
If at any step no in-degree-0 vertex exists, the remaining graph has none, which is possible only if it contains a directed cycle, so the algorithm correctly reports “not a DAG.”  
Therefore Kahn’s algorithm (and any equivalent zero-in-degree deletion scheme) produces a correct topological sort exactly when the input graph is acyclic.

Let the algorithm repeatedly output any vertex whose current indegree is 0, then delete the vertex and all of its outgoing edges (Kahn’s algorithm).  
Induction on the number of vertices proves correctness.  

Base: Before the first step every edge (u → v) has u’s indegree ≥0 and v’s indegree ≥1, so some vertex x with indegree 0 exists and no edge enters x; thus placing x first cannot violate the desired order.  

Inductive step: Assume the first k vertices printed form a valid topological prefix; remove them and their outgoing edges from the graph, leaving another acyclic graph G′.  
Because G′ is still a DAG, it again contains a vertex y with indegree 0, and by construction no remaining edge points to y, so appending y after the first k vertices keeps the ordering property.  

Thus, by induction, every printed vertex precedes all of its original out-neighbors, i.e., for every original edge (u → v) the algorithm outputs u before v.  

Therefore the produced sequence is a correct topological sorting of the DAG.

Let G be a directed acyclic graph.  
At each iteration Kahn’s algorithm removes a vertex u with in-degree 0, appends it to list L, and deletes all edges (u, v).  
Invariant: every vertex already in L precedes every edge still present in the residual graph.  
The invariant is initially true (L is empty); if it holds before a step, deleting u cannot violate it because u had no incoming edges and its outgoing edges vanish with u, so the invariant is preserved.  
Since G is acyclic, some zero-in-degree vertex always exists, guaranteeing that the loop deletes all |V| vertices and terminates.  
When the algorithm stops, the residual graph is empty, and by the invariant each original edge (u, v) has u earlier than v in L, so L is a topological ordering.  
If the algorithm ever finds remaining vertices but no zero-in-degree vertex, the residual graph contains a directed cycle, proving that G is not acyclic and that no topological order exists.  
Therefore Kahn’s algorithm outputs a valid topological sort exactly when one exists, establishing its correctness.

Let G be a directed acyclic graph.  
Kahn’s algorithm repeatedly chooses a vertex with indegree 0, appends it to list L, deletes that vertex and its outgoing edges, and continues until the graph is empty.  
Because one vertex is removed per iteration, the algorithm halts after ≤ |V| iterations.  
At every iteration a vertex of indegree 0 exists; otherwise every remaining vertex would lie on a directed path that eventually revisits a vertex, forming a cycle and contradicting acyclicity.  
Thus all |V| vertices are output.  
For any original edge (u,v), u is deleted (and placed in L) before that edge is removed, so v is still present and must be deleted later; therefore u precedes v in L.  
Hence L is a linear ordering in which every edge points forward, i.e., a topological order.  
The depth-first-search variant is correct for the same reason: an edge (u,v) forces v to finish before u, so reversing finish times also yields a forward-edge ordering.  
If the input contains a cycle, no vertex with indegree 0 ever appears (or DFS encounters a back edge), so the algorithms detect the impossibility of an ordering.  
Therefore these algorithms produce a topological sort exactly when the input graph is acyclic, proving their correctness.

Maintain a set S of vertices with current in-degree 0; repeatedly remove any v ∈ S, append it to list L, delete its outgoing arcs, and insert into S every neighbor whose in-degree just fell to 0.  
Invariant: S equals the set of vertices whose in-degree is 0 in the graph that remains.  
The invariant is initially true and is preserved because deleting a vertex cannot increase any in-degree and every vertex that reaches in-degree 0 is added to S.  
When v is placed in L no arc of the original graph enters v from a vertex still present, so every original arc (v, w) is oriented from an earlier to a later vertex in L.  
Thus L is a topological order for the vertices it contains.  
If the graph is acyclic, some vertex always has in-degree 0, so the loop removes all |V| vertices and outputs a complete topological sort.  
If the graph contains a directed cycle, every vertex on that cycle always has in-degree ≥1, S becomes empty before all vertices are output, and the algorithm can correctly report a cycle.  
Therefore the algorithm outputs a topological ordering exactly when the input is a DAG, establishing its correctness.

The union–find (disjoint-set) structure maintains a forest of trees where each tree represents a set, and the root of a tree is the representative of its set.  
Each element stores a parent pointer; a root’s parent pointer points to itself.  
Find(x) returns the root of x’s tree; with path compression, every node visited on the way to the root is reattached directly to that root, fl attening the tree and making subsequent operations faster.  
Union(x, y) merges the sets containing x and y by linking one root under the other.  
Union by rank (or size) attaches the root of the shallower (or smaller) tree beneath the deeper (or larger) one, preventing tall trees from forming.  
Combined, path compression and union by rank guarantee near-constant (inverse Ackermann) amortized time per operation.  
The structure supports three core operations: MakeSet(x) to create a singleton set, Find(x) to identify a set representative, and Union(x, y) to merge sets.  
These capabilities make union–find ideal for connectivity problems such as Kruskal’s minimum spanning tree algorithm, dynamic connectivity, and clustering.

Union–find (disjoint-set) maintains a forest of rooted trees where each tree represents one set; every node stores a parent pointer and an optional rank (approximate tree height).  
find(x) returns the representative (root) of x’s set; during this operation path compression makes every node on the search path point directly to the root, flattening the tree for future queries.  
union(x, y) merges the two sets containing x and y: first find their roots rx and ry; if they differ, the root with lower rank is made a child of the root with higher rank (union by rank).  
If ranks are equal, one root becomes child of the other and the new root’s rank increments by one.  
Path compression guarantees that subsequent finds are almost constant time, while union by rank prevents trees from becoming tall in the first place.  
With both heuristics, any sequence of m operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) time, where α is the inverse Ackermann function, practically ≤ 4.  
Memory usage is O(n) since each element stores only a parent index and a small rank.  
This structure supports dynamic connectivity queries, making it essential in algorithms like Kruskal’s minimum spanning tree and percolation simulations.

The union–find (disjoint-set) structure maintains a collection of non-overlapping sets and supports two operations: find(x) returns the representative (root) of the set containing element x, and union(x, y) merges the sets containing x and y.  
Each set is stored as a rooted tree whose nodes point to their parent; the root acts as the set representative.  
Path compression optimizes find: while climbing from x to the root, it rewires every visited node to point directly to that root, flattening the tree and making future finds almost constant-time.  
Union by rank (or size) optimizes union: each root keeps a rank (an upper bound on tree height) or size; when merging two roots, the smaller-rank (or smaller-size) tree is attached under the larger-rank root, preventing tall trees.  
With both heuristics combined, any sequence of m operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) time, where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function and is <5 for all practical input sizes, giving near-constant amortized cost.

A union–find (disjoint-set) structure stores each set as a rooted tree whose root points to itself.  
MakeSet(x) creates a singleton by setting parent[x] = x and rank[x] = 0.  
Find(x) climbs parent pointers to the root and, with path compression, rewires every visited node to point directly to that root, flattening the tree.  
Union(x, y) finds roots rx and ry; if they differ, it merges the two trees.  
Union by rank attaches the root with smaller rank under the root with larger rank; when ranks tie, one root becomes child and the other’s rank increases by one.  
Path compression reduces future Find costs, while rank control prevents trees from becoming tall.  
Together they make the amortized cost of any operation O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function and is ≤ 4 for all practical input sizes.  
The structure therefore supports near-constant-time MakeSet, Find, and Union using only O(n) memory.

A union–find (disjoint-set) structure maintains a collection of disjoint sets under two operations:  
1) find(x) returns the representative (root) of the set containing element x,  
2) union(x, y) merges the two sets containing x and y.

It is stored as a forest of rooted trees where each node points to its parent; the root is the set representative.  
Path compression optimizes find: while climbing from x to the root, it makes every visited node point directly to that root, flattening the tree and making subsequent finds near-constant time.  
Union by rank (or size) optimizes union: each root keeps a rank (an upper bound on tree height); when merging two roots, the root with smaller rank becomes the child of the root with larger rank, and if ranks are equal the new root’s rank increases by one.

Combined, these heuristics guarantee amortized time per operation of O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant for all practical input sizes, while using only O(n) memory.

The union–find (disjoint-set) structure stores each set as a rooted tree whose root is that set’s representative.  
find(x) follows parent pointers from x to its root; union(x, y) merges the two trees containing x and y by making one root point to the other.  
Path compression is applied during find: every vertex on the search path is re-linked directly to the root, flattening the tree and vastly shortening future searches.  
Union by rank (or size) chooses the root with the smaller rank/size to become the child, so tree height grows only when two equally ranked roots are merged (then the new root’s rank increases by 1).  
Together, these heuristics keep tree heights extremely small: a sequence of m operations on n elements costs O(m α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function and is < 5 for any practical input, giving effectively constant-time performance.  
The structure therefore supports near-constant-time connectivity checks and merges, making it a key component of Kruskal’s minimum-spanning-tree algorithm, incremental connectivity, clustering, and many other applications.

Union–find (disjoint-set) stores a collection of disjoint sets and supports make-set, find, and union operations.  
Each set is represented by a rooted tree whose root stores a parent pointer to itself.  
find(x) follows parent pointers from x to the root and returns that root as the set’s representative.  
With path compression, while climbing to the root we make every visited node point directly to the root, flattening the tree.  
union(x, y) first finds the two roots r₁ and r₂; if they differ, it merges the sets.  
Union by rank (or size) attaches the root with smaller rank/size under the root with larger rank, keeping trees shallow.  
The rank field approximates the tree height and is updated only when two equal-rank trees are merged.  
Combined, path compression and union by rank give an amortized time per operation of O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant in practice.  
The structure uses O(n) space and is widely employed in Kruskal’s MST algorithm, image segmentation, and dynamic connectivity problems.

Make-Set runs in O(1).  
If no heuristics are used, a sequence of m Finds and Unions on n elements can take Θ(m n) time, i.e., Θ(n) per operation in the worst case.  
Using union-by-size or union-by-rank alone limits tree height to ⌈log₂n⌉, giving Find and Union worst-case and amortized cost O(log n).  
Using path compression alone yields an amortized cost of O(log* n) per operation.  
Combining union-by-rank (or size) with path compression gives the best bound: a sequence of m operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) time, so each operation costs O(α(n)) amortized.  
Here α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function, which grows more slowly than log* n and is ≤ 4 for any n that can occur in practice, making the operations effectively constant-time.

With both union-by-rank (or size) and path-compression heuristics, a sequence of m Union–Find operations (MakeSet, Union, Find) on n elements takes O(m α(n)) time, where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function. Because α(n) ≤ 4 for any input size feasible in practice, each operation runs in effectively constant amortized time. Specifically, credit-based or potential-method analyses show that a Find costs O(α(n)) amortized, while Union costs O(α(n)) amortized (dominated by the Find calls it performs). MakeSet is O(1). Without path compression but with union-by-rank, each operation is Θ(log n) worst-case; without union-by-rank but with path compression, the amortized cost rises to Θ(log n) as well. Thus, the combination of both heuristics yields the optimal known bound for disjoint-set forests: near-constant O(α(n)) per operation and O(n + m α(n)) for any sequence.

Using both union-by-rank (or union-by-size) and path-compression heuristics, a sequence of m ≥ n union–find operations (Make-Set, Find, Union) performed on n initial singleton sets costs O(m α(n)) in the worst case, where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function that grows slower than any practical constant. Specifically, each Make-Set is O(1), each Union is O(α(n)) (because it contains two Find operations), and each Find is O(α(n)) amortized. Since α(n) ≤ 4 for all realistic input sizes, the structure is effectively constant-time per operation in practice. This O(m α(n)) bound is tight: Tarjan proved that no algorithm using only linking and pointer moves can beat it by more than a constant factor.

With both path-compression and union-by-rank (or size) heuristics, a sequence of m ≥ n make/union/find operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) time, where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function. Hence the amortized time per individual union or find is O(α(n)), which is < 5 for any input size attainable in practice and therefore effectively constant. If only path-compression is used (no ranking), the amortized bound degrades to O(log* n) per operation. If only ranking is used (no path-compression), each operation is O(log n) worst-case. Without either heuristic, a worst-case find can take Θ(n) time and a mixed sequence costs Θ(mn) in total.

With union–find that uses neither path compression nor union by rank, each UNION and FIND can cost Θ(log n) and in worst cases Θ(n).  
Adding union by rank (or size) alone guarantees every UNION is Θ(1) and every FIND is Θ(log n).  
Adding path compression alone makes a single FIND worst-case Θ(n) but drives a whole sequence of m operations to O(m log n).  
Combining both union by rank (or size) and path compression yields the classic Tarjan bound: any sequence of m ≥ n UNION and FIND operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) time, where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function.  
Because α(n) ≤ 4 for any conceivable input size, this is regarded as “almost constant” amortized time per operation.

For a disjoint-set (union–find) data structure that uses both union-by-rank (or size) and path compression, any intermixed sequence of m ≥ n make-set, union, and find operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) total time. 2) Here α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function, which grows so slowly that α(n) ≤ 5 for any input size arising in practice. 3) Consequently the amortized (average) time per make-set, union, or find operation is O(α(n)), often treated as “effectively constant.” 4) If only union-by-rank OR only path compression is used, the worst-case per-operation cost becomes O(log n), yielding O(m log n) total. 5) Without either heuristic, a sequence of unions can create chains, giving find operations O(n) worst-case, so m operations take Θ(m n) time.

A disjoint-set (union–find) structure supports MAKE-SET, UNION, and FIND. Without heuristics, UNION costs O(1) and FIND costs O(n) in the worst case. Adding union-by-rank (or size) alone bounds the tree height to O(log n), giving O(log n) worst-case (and amortized) FIND and UNION. Adding only path compression alone gives O(log n) amortized cost per operation. Combining union-by-rank (or size) with path compression yields Tarjan’s bound. For a sequence of m ≥ n operations on n elements, the total running time is O(m α(n)), where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function. Thus the amortized time per MAKE-SET, FIND, or UNION is O(α(n)). Because α(n) ≤ 4 for any realistic n, each operation is effectively constant-time in practice.

With union by rank, each root keeps a non-decreasing integer rank and the root of smaller rank is made child of the larger, breaking ties by raising the latter’s rank, so a node’s rank can rise at most ⌊log₂n⌋ times and only n/2^r nodes can ever have rank ≥ r.  
Group ranks into level sets L₀,L₁,… where level i contains ranks in (A_{i-1},A_i] with A₀=1 and A_i=2^{A_{i-1}}; there are at most α(m,n)+1 such levels, where α is the inverse Ackermann function.  
In a find(x) walk, every arc (v,parent(v)) is either type-B (v lies in a lower level than parent(v)) or type-A (both endpoints in the same level).  
A node can participate in at most α(m,n) type-B traversals over the entire operation sequence because each such step strictly raises its level, so all type-B costs sum to O(n α(m,n)).  
Define potential Φ=Σ_v c^{rank(v)} for a suitable constant c<2; Φ is initially O(n), never negative, and every type-A traversal reduces Φ by at least one, thereby “paying” for its own actual cost.  
Hence the total cost of m ≥ n operations is O(m + n α(m,n)) = O(m α(m,n)).  
The amortized time per Make-Set, Union, or Find is therefore O(α(m,n)), which is at most 4 for any practical input size, so union–find with path compression runs in nearly constant time.

Maintain ranks and always attach the shallower root to the deeper one, increasing the rank only on ties.  
A node’s rank can rise at most ⌊log₂ n⌋ times, so there are O(n log n) total rank increases.  
During Find, label an edge heavy if it joins equal-rank nodes and light otherwise; a walk from a node to the root contains at most log* n light edges because each light step strictly raises the rank through the logarithmic hierarchy.  
Heavy edges form chains whose length is bounded by the inverse Ackermann function α(n), for a longer chain would force Ackermann(rank, length) ≥ n.  
Path compression destroys every edge it traverses, so no edge is paid for twice.  
Consequently a sequence of m ≥ n operations performs O((n + m) α(n)) pointer traversals.  
The amortized cost per operation is therefore Θ(α(n)), which is less than 5 for every realistic n.  
Thus union–find with union by rank (or size) and path compression runs in nearly constant amortized time.

Assign every node a non-decreasing rank (initially 0) and always make the root of lower rank point to the root of higher rank during UNION; ranks never exceed ⌊log₂n⌋.  
During FIND, perform path compression by making every vertex on the search path point directly to the root it discovers.  
Tarjan’s analysis introduces a potential Φ = Σ α(rank(v)) over all vertices, where α is the inverse Ackermann function.  
A UNION changes at most one rank, so it increases Φ by at most α(log₂n)=O(1) and touches only O(1) pointers, giving O(1) amortized time.  
In a FIND, each pointer followed either moves to a node of strictly higher rank or is immediately erased by path compression; any pointer can be followed at most α(n,m) times before its endpoints’ ranks become equal.  
Thus the actual work of all FIND operations is bounded by the initial potential plus O(m α(n,m)).  
Combining the bounds, the total time for any sequence of m UNION/FIND operations on n elements is O((n+m) α(n,m)).  
Because α(n,m) is ≤ 5 for any input feasible in the universe, union–find with path compression runs in practically constant amortized time per operation.

Store with every set–representative a rank r; when Unite(a,b) attach the root of smaller rank to the one of larger rank, raising the rank only when r(a)=r(b).  
Since a rank rises only after merging two trees of that rank, a root of rank r must represent at least 2^r elements, so r ≤ ⌊log₂ n⌋.  
Group the ranks into classes R₀,…,R_{α(n)} where R_i = {r | A(i,1) ≤ r < A(i+1,1)} and α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function.  
Give every node the level ℓ(x) such that r(x)∈R_{ℓ(x)}; thus ℓ(x)≤α(n).  
Set the potential Φ = Σ_x(α(n)−ℓ(x)); initially Φ ≤ n α(n) and Φ ≥ 0 always.  
Make-Set and Union that do not change any parent pointer cost O(1) and leave Φ unchanged, so their amortized cost is O(1).  
During Find(x), each change of a parent pointer costs O(1) and, except for at most α(n) such changes for that node over the entire execution, lowers Φ by at least 1, giving amortized cost O(α(n)).  
Thus any sequence of m operations takes O((n+m) α(n)) time.  
The amortized time per operation is therefore O(α(n)), which is “nearly constant” because α(n)≤4 for all practical values of n.

Let n make-set operations and m−n ≥ 0 intermixed find-set/union operations be performed using union-by-rank and path compression.  
Give every node v a non-negative integer rank r(v); ranks are monotone along parent pointers and r(v) increases only when two trees of equal rank are united, so each element’s rank can rise at most ⌊log₂n⌋ times.  
Partition the ranks into blocks B₀,B₁,… where B₀={0}, B₁={1}, B₂={2,3}, B₃={4–7}, …, i.e. Bᵢ contains the 2ᶦ ranks from 2ᶦ to 2^{i+1}−1.  
Define level(v)=i if r(v)∈Bᵢ; then level(v) can rise only when rank does, hence at most log*n<5 for all practical n.  
During a find-set, whenever path compression makes v’s parent jump from level i to a strictly smaller level j, charge that step to v and say v has paid for level i.  
Because a node pays for each level at most once and there are ⌈α(m,n)⌉ levels (α is the inverse Ackermann function), the total number of charged steps over the entire sequence is O(n α(m,n)).  
Uncharged steps (those that do not cross a level boundary) are at most one per find-set edge traversal, hence O(m).  
Thus the whole sequence costs O(m α(m,n)) time, giving an amortized cost of O(α(m,n)) per operation, which is “nearly constant’’ because α(m,n) ≤ 4 for every conceivable input size.

Let the structure use union-by-rank and path compression.  
Because a node’s parent always has strictly larger rank and ranks increase only when two equal-rank trees are united, each element’s rank can rise at most ⌊log₂ n⌋ times, so ranks lie in {0,…,⌊log n⌋}.  
Partition these ranks into α(n)+1 intervals I₀,I₁,…,I_{α(n)} where α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function; interval i contains ranks in (A(i−1),A(i)] with A the Ackermann function.  
Give every node one credit for each interval it will ever visit (≤α(n)+1 credits in total) when the node is created.  
During FIND, whenever the pointer of a node x is redirected to skip over a node y whose rank lies in a higher interval, pay for that pointer change with one of x’s unused credits; a node changes intervals at most once per credit, so it is charged at most α(n)+1 times over the entire sequence.  
All remaining steps of FIND (tests on while-loop conditions, reading fields, etc.) are bounded by a constant per pointer change, hence are also paid by the same credits.  
A UNION touches only the two roots and a constant number of fields, so it costs O(1) actual time and O(1) amortized time.  
Since each of the n nodes ever created receives O(α(n)) credits and no operation ever uses more credits than it is given, a sequence of m ≥ n operations executes in O(m α(n)) time.  
Thus the amortized cost per operation is O(α(n)), which is “nearly constant” because α(n) ≤ 4 for any input size arising in practice.

Give each set-node a non-decreasing integer rank; when two roots are united, make the root with smaller rank point to the other and increase the latter’s rank only if the two ranks were equal.  
A node’s rank can rise at most ⌊log₂ n⌋ times, because a rank-k root has size ≥2ᵏ, so there are at most n/2ᵏ such nodes.  
Classify every pointer on a find-path as “type-k” if it goes from a rank-k node to a node of larger rank; there are at most n/2ᵏ such pointers.  
During path compression every traversed pointer is either deleted (it is redirected to the root) or becomes a higher-type pointer, so a type-k pointer is charged to its deletion or to the single later step when its type increases.  
Hence a pointer of type-k is charged at most α(n) times, where α is the inverse Ackermann function that counts how many rank jumps can occur.  
Summing over all k, the total charge for m operations on n elements is O(n + m α(m,n)).  
Amortized cost per Make-Set is O(1), and per Union or Find is O(α(m,n)), which is ≤4 for any input feasible in practice.  
Thus union–find with union by rank and path compression runs in nearly constant amortized time.

In the comparison model a sorting algorithm is allowed to look at its input only by comparing two keys and branching on the result.  
The whole computation can be pictured as a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves represent the possible final permutations of the input.  
To be correct the algorithm must be able to reach a distinct leaf for every one of the n! possible orderings of n distinct elements, so the tree needs at least n! leaves.  
A binary tree with L leaves has height at least log₂ L, therefore the worst-case number of comparisons is ≥ log₂(n!).  
Using Stirling’s approximation, log₂(n!) = Θ(n log₂ n).  
Hence every comparison-based sorting algorithm needs Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case, giving the familiar lower bound.

In the comparison model an algorithm is allowed to examine its input only by asking binary comparison questions of the form “is key i ≤ key j?”.  
Such an algorithm can be represented as a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves give the final permutation returned.  
Because the n input keys could be arranged in any of n! orders, the tree must have at least n! distinct leaves.  
A binary tree with L leaves must have height at least ⌈log₂ L⌉, so the decision tree’s height—and therefore the algorithm’s worst-case number of comparisons—is at least ⌈log₂ n!⌉.  
Stirling’s formula gives log₂ n! = Θ(n log₂ n).  
Consequently every comparison-based sorting algorithm must perform c·n log n comparisons for some constant c in the worst case, establishing the lower bound Ω(n log n).

In the comparison model a sorting algorithm’s only operation that inspects the input is a binary comparison “is aᵢ < aⱼ ?”.  
The algorithm’s entire control flow can therefore be drawn as a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves output a particular permutation of the input.  
To sort n distinct keys the algorithm must be able to output any of the n! possible orderings, so the tree needs at least n! leaves.  
A binary tree of height m (i.e., using m comparisons in the worst case) has at most 2ᵐ leaves.  
Hence 2ᵐ ≥ n! and m ≥ log₂(n!).  
Using Stirling’s approximation, log₂(n!) = Θ(n log₂ n).  
Therefore any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires at least Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case, establishing the lower bound.

In the comparison model a sorting algorithm’s only operation that inspects the data is “compare A[i] and A[j],” whose outcome is one of two possibilities (< or ≥).  
The entire execution can therefore be represented as a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves correspond to the possible permutations of the input.  
To be correct the algorithm must be able to reach a distinct leaf for every one of the n! possible input orders, so the tree must have at least n! leaves.  
A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, hence 2^h ≥ n! and h ≥ log₂(n!).  
Using Stirling’s approximation, log₂(n!) = Θ(n log n).  
Therefore any comparison-based sorting algorithm needs at least Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case, establishing the lower bound.

In the comparison model a sorting algorithm is only allowed to obtain information about the keys by asking binary “compare-two” questions such as “is aᵢ < aⱼ?”.  
Every run of the algorithm is therefore a path in a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves correspond to possible output permutations.  
To be correct, the tree must have at least n! different leaves, one for each possible ordering of n distinct keys.  
A binary tree with height h has at most 2ʰ leaves, so we need 2ʰ ≥ n!, i.e. h ≥ log₂(n!).  
Using Stirling’s approximation, log₂(n!) = Θ(n log₂n).  
Hence any comparison-based sorting algorithm needs at least Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case, establishing the lower bound.

The comparison model allows an algorithm to obtain information only through pairwise key comparisons, each returning one of two possible outcomes.  
Such an algorithm can be represented as a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves correspond to the n! possible permutations of the n distinct input items.  
Correct sorting requires that every permutation lead to a unique leaf, so the tree must have at least n! leaves.  
A binary tree of height h contains at most 2^h leaves, giving 2^h ≥ n!.  
Taking logarithms, h ≥ log₂(n!) = Θ(n log n) by Stirling’s approximation.  
Therefore any comparison-based sorting algorithm needs at least Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case, establishing the lower bound for sorting in this model.

The comparison model assumes an algorithm may only inspect the input by comparing two keys and branching on the result.  
A deterministic comparison‐based sorter can therefore be represented as a binary decision tree whose internal nodes are comparisons and whose leaves correspond to the n ! possible output permutations.  
A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, so to accommodate all n ! permutations we require 2^h ≥ n !.  
Taking logarithms gives h ≥ log₂(n !).  
Stirling’s approximation yields log₂(n !) = Θ(n log₂ n).  
Thus the height, which equals the worst-case number of comparisons, is Ω(n log n).  
Consequently any sorting algorithm that relies solely on key comparisons cannot have a better worst-case time complexity than Ω(n log n), although non-comparison methods (e.g., counting or radix sort) can bypass this bound when their assumptions hold.

Counting sort determines an element’s position by using its key value as an index into a counting array, incrementing counts and then computing prefix sums to place each element directly in its final slot. Because elements are never compared to decide ordering, the algorithm’s operations depend on arithmetic on keys, not pairwise comparisons. As a result, its time complexity is O(n + k) (n = number of items, k = range of keys) instead of being constrained by the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to all comparison-based sorts. This key-indexed counting approach classifies counting sort as a non-comparison sorting algorithm.

Counting sort organizes elements by using their keys to index into an auxiliary counting array, increments counts, then reconstructs the sorted output by iterating over that array. Because it determines order solely from these key‐based counts rather than repeatedly comparing pairs of elements to decide which is larger, it bypasses the fundamental operation that defines comparison-based algorithms. As a result, its running time depends on the key range (O(n + k)) instead of the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to algorithms whose primary operation is element-to-element comparison.

Counting sort places each input element into its correct output position by using the element’s key as an index in a counting array and tallying how many elements share each key. It never asks “is A < B?” or “is A > B?” between pairs of elements; instead, it relies solely on arithmetic operations (array indexing and addition) based on the keys’ numeric values. Because no pairwise comparisons occur, the algorithm’s running time is O(n + k) rather than being constrained by the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to all comparison-based sorts. Hence, counting sort is classified as a non-comparison sorting algorithm.

A comparison-based sorting algorithm decides the relative order of two elements only by applying a comparison operator (e.g., “≤”) to pairs of elements, and its control flow depends solely on the outcomes of those comparisons. Counting sort never performs such pairwise element comparisons. Instead, it relies on the assumption that every key is an integer drawn from a small, known range 0…k. It creates an array of size k+1 and, in a single pass, increments a counter corresponding to each key value. The later reconstruction of the output list is driven by these frequency counts, not by testing one element against another. Because no operation of the algorithm evaluates whether one element is “greater than,” “less than,” or “equal to” another element, counting sort falls outside the comparison-based model. Consequently, it bypasses the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to all comparison-based sorts and runs in O(n + k) time instead.

Counting sort determines the final order without ever asking “is A < B?” between data items.  
Instead, it treats each key as an array index, tallies how many times every key occurs, converts those counts into starting positions, and then places each item directly into its correct slot.  
Because the algorithm’s decisions come from arithmetic on counts and indices, not from pairwise key comparisons, it bypasses the decision-tree model that defines comparison-based sorting.  
This is why its time complexity is O(n + k) and not subject to the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to all algorithms whose only operation for ordering is comparison.

Counting sort uses the value of each element as an array index, tallies how many of each value occur, computes prefix sums, and then places each element directly into its final position. At no point does it evaluate whether one element is “less than” or “greater than” another element; elements are never compared to each other. Because its ordering decision is based on arithmetic indexing rather than pair-wise comparisons, it falls outside the model of comparison-based sorting algorithms. Consequently, counting sort is not subject to the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to comparison sorts; it runs in O(n + k) time, where k is the range of possible keys.

Counting sort determines an element’s position by tallying how many times each key value occurs, storing these counts in an auxiliary array indexed by the keys. It never asks questions such as “Is A[i] ≤ A[j]?” or performs any pair-wise comparisons between input elements. Because ordering is inferred solely from frequency counts and cumulative totals, its time complexity is O(n + k) instead of the Ω(n log n) lower bound that applies to all comparison-based sorts. Therefore, counting sort is classified as a non-comparison sorting algorithm.

Counting sort scans the input, builds a frequency array of size k, forms prefix sums, and writes the sorted output, so its running time is Θ(n + k) in the best, average, and worst cases, where n is the number of elements and k is the range of possible key values. The algorithm is linear when k = O(n) and degrades to super-linear if k ≫ n. Space usage is Θ(n + k): Θ(k) for the count array plus Θ(n) for the auxiliary output array required to keep the algorithm stable. Aside from these arrays it needs only O(1) extra variables.

Let n be the number of elements and k the range of possible key values (e.g., 0‒k).  
Counting sort scans the input once to build the count array and once more to write the output, so its running time is Θ(n + k) in the best, average, and worst cases.  
If k = O(n) the algorithm is linear; if k ≫ n the time cost becomes dominated by k.  
It allocates an auxiliary count array of size k + 1 and (usually) an output array of size n, yielding Θ(k + n) additional space.  
Thus, time complexity: Θ(n + k).  
Space complexity: Θ(n + k).

Counting sort runs in Θ(n + k) time: Θ(n) to tally frequencies, Θ(k) to build cumulative counts, and another Θ(n) to write the sorted output, where n is the number of items and k is the range of distinct key values (max – min + 1). Its space usage is Θ(n + k): Θ(k) for the count array and Θ(n) for the output array (or Θ(k) auxiliary if the sort is done in place by rewriting the original array afterward). Counting sort is stable, and its efficiency is optimal when k is O(n); when k ≫ n, both time and space become prohibitive.

Counting sort scans the input array of n elements, builds a frequency array of size k (the range of key values), converts frequencies to prefix sums, and then produces the sorted output.  
Time complexity:  
• Building the frequency array: O(n).  
• Transforming frequencies to cumulative counts: O(k).  
• Placing each element into the output array: O(n).  
Total time = O(n + k).  

Space complexity:  
• Frequency (count) array: k cells.  
• Output array: n cells (often required for stability).  
Total auxiliary space = O(n + k).

Counting sort initializes a frequency array of size k (the range of input keys), scans the n input elements to fill it, computes prefix sums over the k counts, and finally traverses the input again to place each element in its sorted position. Each of those four phases costs O(k), O(n), O(k), and O(n) time, respectively, yielding an overall time complexity of O(n + k) in best, average, and worst cases. The algorithm allocates a count array of size k and (in the usual stable version) an output array of size n, so its auxiliary space complexity is O(n + k) or O(k) if the output can overwrite the input.

Counting sort scans the input once to tally occurrences, does a prefix-sum pass over the k-length count array, and then places each of the n items into an output array. Each of these three stages is linear in either n or k, so the overall time complexity is Θ(n + k) in the best, average, and worst cases. The algorithm needs a count array of size k and, in its standard form, a separate output array of size n. Therefore the extra space required is Θ(n + k).

Let n be the number of input elements and k be the range of key values (e.g., max key + 1).  
Counting sort scans the input, accumulates counts, computes prefix sums, and writes the output, all of which together cost Θ(n + k) time in every case (best, average, and worst).  
If the algorithm uses both a count array of size k and a temporary output array of size n (the common stable version), its auxiliary space is Θ(n + k).  
If you overwrite the original array and only keep the count array, the extra space drops to Θ(k), but the basic implementation most often cited uses Θ(n + k).  
Thus, counting sort is linear with respect to the sum of input size and key range but can become inefficient in either dimension if k ≫ n or vice versa.

A stable sorting algorithm preserves the relative order of elements that compare as equal, so duplicates appear in the output exactly as they occurred in the input. An unstable sorting algorithm provides no such guarantee, allowing equal elements to be reordered arbitrarily. Stability matters when records carry additional fields or when multiple passes of sorting are performed (e.g., sort by last name, then by first name), because it keeps earlier sort criteria intact. Examples of stable algorithms include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort, and Timsort; common unstable ones are Quick Sort, Heap Sort, and Selection Sort. Otherwise, both stable and unstable algorithms can produce the same overall key order—the distinction applies only to how they handle ties.

A stable sorting algorithm keeps the original relative order of elements that compare equal (e.g., two “John” records stay in the same sequence they were before the sort).  
An unstable sorting algorithm may reorder equal elements arbitrarily, so their initial sequence can be lost.  
Stability matters when the data has multiple keys and you plan to perform consecutive sorts—e.g., sort by last name, then by first name—because stability preserves earlier key order.  
Examples of stable algorithms include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, and Timsort (used in Python).  
Examples of unstable algorithms include Quick Sort (in its common in-place form), Heap Sort, and standard C++ std::sort.

A stable sorting algorithm preserves the original relative order of records that have equal keys (values); if two items compare as “equal,” they will appear in the output in the same order they appeared in the input. An unstable sorting algorithm does not guarantee this property—equal keys might be reordered during the sort. Stability matters when the data already carries secondary information encoded by its initial order or when multiple passes of sorting by different keys are combined. Examples of stable sorts include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, and Bubble Sort, while Quick Sort, Heap Sort, and Selection Sort are typically unstable (though stable variants can be implemented at extra cost).

A stable sorting algorithm keeps the relative order of records with equal keys unchanged (if item A equals item B and A appears before B in the input, A will still precede B in the output). An unstable sorting algorithm may reorder equal keys arbitrarily, so that ties in the input need not remain in the same order after sorting. Stability matters when multiple sorts are chained (e.g., sort by last name then by first name) or when records carry secondary information tied to their original order. Examples of stable algorithms include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort, and TimSort; examples of unstable ones include Quick Sort (classic in-place version), Heap Sort, and Selection Sort.

A stable sorting algorithm preserves the original relative order of elements that compare as equal (e.g., if two records with the same key appear in the input as A before B, they will still appear as A before B in the output). An unstable sorting algorithm may change that order, so equal-key elements can emerge in any sequence. Stability matters when each element carries secondary information you want to keep grouped consistently—such as sorting a list of people first by surname (primary key) and later by given name without losing the original given-name ordering. Typical stable algorithms include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, and Timsort, while classic implementations of Quick Sort, Heap Sort, and Selection Sort are unstable unless extra steps are taken. Stability has no impact on time-complexity but can simplify multi-key sorting and make behavior more predictable.

A stable sorting algorithm preserves the relative order of records that have equal keys, while an unstable sorting algorithm may reorder those equal-keyed records arbitrarily. Stability matters when the data carry secondary information (e.g., first sorted by date, later by name) because maintaining prior ordering keeps earlier criteria intact. Examples of stable algorithms include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort, and Timsort. Examples of unstable algorithms include Quick Sort (naïve implementation), Heap Sort, and Selection Sort. If all keys are distinct, stability is irrelevant; instability only shows when equal keys exist. Some unstable algorithms can be made stable with extra bookkeeping or memory. Stability often trades off with space or speed, but many modern libraries provide stable variants to accommodate multi-key sorting needs.

A stable sorting algorithm guarantees that items with equal keys preserve their original relative order in the output, whereas an unstable algorithm offers no such guarantee. This property is essential when records have secondary information or when multiple passes of sorting are performed (e.g., sort by last name, then by first name). Stable sorts include Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort, and Timsort. Commonly unstable sorts include Quick Sort, Heap Sort, and Selection Sort. Stability does not dictate time complexity, but many stable implementations use extra memory to track positions, while some unstable ones achieve in-place operation more readily. An unstable algorithm can be made stable through additional bookkeeping at the cost of extra time or space. Choose a stable sort when preserving earlier orderings of equal keys matters; otherwise, either type is acceptable.

The load factor (α) of a hash table is the ratio n/m, where n is the number of stored keys and m is the number of buckets or slots.  
A low α means many empty buckets, so look-ups, insertions, and deletions are usually O(1) but memory is under-utilized.  
As α grows, collisions become more frequent: in separate chaining the average chain length is α, making operations O(1 + α); in open addressing the expected probes rise to ≈1/(1 – α) for a successful search.  
When α approaches 1 in open addressing, primary clustering causes a sharp jump toward O(n) time per operation.  
To keep performance near constant time, implementations set a threshold α (commonly 0.5–0.75) at which they allocate a larger table and rehash all keys.  
Thus, controlling the load factor trades space for time: lower α favors speed with extra memory, higher α saves memory but risks slower and more variable operation costs.

The load factor (α) is n ÷ m, where n is the number of stored entries and m is the number of buckets or slots.  
Higher α means the table is fuller, raising collision probability.  
More collisions lengthen probe sequences (open addressing) or chains (separate chaining), so average O(1) operations degrade toward O(n).
