Very low α minimizes collisions but wastes memory.  
Implementations set a maximum α (≈0.7 for open addressing, ≈1–2 for chaining); when that threshold is exceeded, they allocate a larger array and rehash all entries.  
Maintaining an appropriate load factor balances memory use against predictable, near-constant-time performance.

The load factor (α) of a hash table is the ratio of stored elements (n) to table slots (m): α = n / m. As α approaches 1, more keys compete for each bucket, increasing the probability of collisions. With more collisions, average-case search, insert, and delete times drift from O(1) toward O(α) for chaining or toward O(1 / (1–α)) probes for open addressing, degrading performance. A low α, however, means many empty slots, so operations stay close to constant time but memory is under-utilized. Implementations therefore pick a threshold (commonly 0.5–0.75); when α exceeds it, the table is resized and rehashed to restore low collision rates. Resizing temporarily costs O(n) but amortizes to O(1) per operation over a sequence of inserts. Choosing a good load factor balances time and space: lower α gives faster operations at higher memory cost, while higher α saves space but slows access.

The load factor (α) of a hash table is the number of stored elements (n) divided by the number of available buckets or slots (m), i.e., α = n / m.  
As α approaches 0, buckets are sparsely populated, collisions are rare, and successful and unsuccessful searches, insertions, and deletions run in expected O(1) time, but memory is under-utilized.  
As α grows toward 1 (or higher for chaining schemes), collisions become more frequent; average probe chain length (chaining) or probe sequence (open addressing) increases, pushing operations toward Θ(α) time and potentially degrading to Θ(n) in the worst case.  
To keep α below a threshold (commonly 0.5–0.75 for open addressing and 1–2 for chaining), most implementations resize and rehash the table when that threshold is exceeded.  
Resizing costs O(n) time but is amortized over many future operations, preserving average O(1) performance.  
Thus the load factor embodies a space-time trade-off: lower α uses more memory for faster accesses, while higher α conserves memory but risks slower lookups and inserts.

The load factor (α) of a hash table is the ratio of stored elements (n) to the number of available buckets or slots (m), α = n/m.  
When α is small, most buckets are empty, so average lookup, insertion, and deletion stay O(1) but memory is wasted.  
As α approaches 1 (or grows beyond 1 in separate-chaining tables), collisions rise, causing longer probe sequences or longer linked lists, which degrades operations toward O(α) ≈ O(n) in the worst case.  
Higher collision rates increase cache misses and can dramatically slow down both successful and unsuccessful searches.  
Most implementations therefore trigger a resize/rehash once α passes a chosen threshold (commonly 0.5–0.9 for open addressing and 1–2 for chaining) to restore near-constant performance.  
Doubling the bucket array size cuts α roughly in half, amortizing the rehash cost over many future operations so average time per operation remains O(1).  
Conversely, some dynamic tables shrink when α falls below a lower bound to recover memory.  
Thus, maintaining a moderate load factor balances space efficiency against the constant-time performance guarantees expected from hash tables.

The load factor (α) of a hash table is the ratio of stored entries (n) to available buckets (k), α = n/k.  
A small α means most buckets are empty, minimizing collisions and keeping average search, insert, and delete times near O(1), but it wastes memory.  
A large α packs entries densely, using memory efficiently, but raises collision frequency, lengthening probe chains or linked lists and increasing expected operation cost toward O(1 + α); in the extreme, it can degrade to O(n).  
Typical libraries aim to keep α between about 0.5 and 0.8 as a compromise.  
When α surpasses a chosen threshold, the table is resized (rehashing all keys into a larger bucket array) to restore low collision rates.  
Thus, load factor directly governs the balance between time efficiency and space efficiency in hash table performance.

The load factor (α) of a hash table is the ratio of stored entries (n) to the number of available buckets or slots (m), i.e., α = n ⁄ m.  
As α rises, more keys map to the same bucket, so the average chain length (in chaining schemes) or probe sequence length (in open addressing) grows.  
When collisions become frequent, the expected O(1) time for search, insertion, and deletion degrades toward O(α) for chaining and toward O(1 / (1 – α)) for open addressing.  
High load factors also increase the likelihood of long “worst-case” operations, causing latency spikes.  
Conversely, very low load factors waste memory because many buckets remain empty.  
To balance speed and space, implementations pick a maximum α (commonly around 0.7–0.9 for open addressing and 1.0–2.0 for chaining); when this threshold is exceeded the table is resized and entries are rehashed, restoring low α and O(1) performance.

Under the simple-uniform-hashing assumption every key lands in each of the m table slots with probability 1/m, independently of the other keys.  
With separate chaining the expected chain length is the load factor α = n/m, so insertions are Θ(1), and both successful and unsuccessful searches (and deletions) take Θ(1 + α) time; choosing m = Θ(n) makes α = Θ(1), giving expected Θ(1) time.  
With open addressing the expected number of probes is 1/(1 – α) for an unsuccessful search and at most (1/α) ln(1/(1 – α)) (≈ (1/(1 – α))⁄2) for a successful search; insertions need the same number of probes, and all operations are Θ(1) when α is kept below a fixed constant (<1).  
Thus, in a well-sized table all fundamental hash-table operations run in expected constant time.

Under the Simple Uniform Hashing Assumption (each key is equally likely to hash to any of m slots, independently of other keys), let n be the number of stored keys and α = n/m be the load factor.  
1. Expected insertion with chaining scans only the first slot’s list, whose expected length is α, giving Θ(1 + α) time; if α = Θ(1) (table size proportional to n), this is Θ(1).  
2. Expected successful search also traverses one list of expected length α, so it is Θ(1 + α) → Θ(1) when α is constant.  
3. Expected unsuccessful search similarly examines α elements, so it is Θ(1 + α) → Θ(1) when α is constant.  
4. Deletion in chaining first performs a search and then removes from a constant-time linked-list operation, so it is Θ(1 + α) → Θ(1) for constant α.  
5. With open addressing (linear or quadratic probing, double hashing), expected probe counts are ≤ 1/(1 − α) for successful search and ≤ 1/(1 − α)² for unsuccessful search; keeping α bounded below 1 yields expected Θ(1) time.  
Thus, under simple uniform hashing and a load factor that remains a fixed constant, all basic hash-table operations—insert, successful/unsuccessful search, and delete—run in expected Θ(1) time.

With simple uniform hashing every key is equally likely to hash into any of m table slots, so the expected number of keys per slot is the load factor α = n ⁄ m.  
Using separate chaining, the expected length of any chain is α, hence expected time for search, insertion, or deletion is Θ(1 + α); if α is kept O(1) (by resizing), these operations run in expected Θ(1) time.  
With open addressing the expected number of probes is 1⁄(1 − α) for a successful search and ≤1⁄(1 − α)² for an unsuccessful search; again, with α bounded away from 1 these are constant.  
Therefore, under simple uniform hashing and a constant load factor, hash‐table insert, find, and delete all have expected O(1) time, while their worst-case time remains O(n).

Under the simple-uniform-hashing assumption (each key is equally likely to hash to any of m slots, independent of other keys), n keys give load factor α = n⁄m.  
With chaining, expected time to search, insert, or delete a key equals the time to compute the hash (Θ(1)) plus the expected list length in its slot (Θ(α)), so each operation is Θ(1 + α).  
If α is kept O(1) by resizing, the expected cost per operation is Θ(1).  
For a successful search the expected list length examined is 1 + α⁄2, still Θ(1 + α).  
With open addressing, an unsuccessful search or insertion probes an expected 1⁄(1 − α) slots, and a successful search probes about (1⁄α) ln(1⁄(1 − α)), both Θ(1) when α < 1 is constant.  
Thus, under simple uniform hashing and constant load factor, hash table operations run in expected constant time, while in the worst case (e.g., all keys collide) they degenerate to Θ(n).

Under the Simple Uniform Hashing Assumption, each key is equally likely to hash to any of the m slots independently of other keys.  
Let n be the number of stored keys and α = n/m the load factor.  
1. Chaining: expected insertion takes Θ(1), expected successful or unsuccessful search (hence deletion) costs Θ(1 + α); when m = Θ(n) so α = Θ(1), this is Θ(1) time.  
2. Open addressing (linear probing, quadratic, double hashing): expected insertion, successful search, and deletion each take Θ(1 / (1 – α)), and an unsuccessful search costs Θ(1 / (1 – α)²); with α bounded below 1 by a constant, these are Θ(1).  
3. In both schemes the worst-case time, caused by all keys hashing to one slot or probe sequence, is Θ(n).

Under the simple-uniform-hashing assumption, each key is equally likely to hash to any of the m table slots, independently of other keys.  
Let n be the number of stored keys and α = n/m the load factor.  
Separate chaining: the expected list length in any slot is α, so  
• Successful search, deletion, or duplicate-checking insertion costs Θ(1 + α) time;  
• Unsuccessful search costs the same Θ(1 + α);  
• Pure insertion that merely adds the key at the chain head is Θ(1).  
If the table is kept so that m = Θ(n) (i.e., α = Θ(1)), all these operations run in expected Θ(1) time.  
Open addressing: with α < 1, the expected number of probes is 1/(1-α) for an unsuccessful search/insertion and about (1/α) ln(1/(1-α)) for a successful search or deletion, which is also Θ(1) when α is a fixed constant below 1.

Under the simple-uniform-hashing assumption—each key is equally likely to map to any slot independently—expected performance depends only on the load factor α = n/m (n keys, m table slots). With chaining, expected cost for an unsuccessful search is Θ(1 + α) and for a successful search is Θ(1 + α/2); when α = Θ(1), both become Θ(1). Insertions run in Θ(1) expected time because the item is added to the front of its chain. Deletions first perform a search of the same expected cost, then remove in O(1), so overall Θ(1). With open addressing, expected probe count for an unsuccessful search is ≤ 1/(1 − α) and for a successful search is ≤ (1/α) ln(1/(1 − α)); keeping α bounded below 1 ensures expected Θ(1) time. Thus, under simple uniform hashing and a constant load factor, search, insert, and delete operations all run in expected O(1) time, while worst-case time remains O(n) if hashing degenerates.

A good hash function is deterministic, yet so thoroughly mixes its bits that even a one-bit change in the input flips roughly half the output bits (strong avalanche effect). It produces values that are uniformly distributed across the output space, minimizing clustering and collision probability. Collisions are still mathematically inevitable, but a good design makes finding any collision—or a second preimage given one input—computationally infeasible. The function must be fast to compute, using only simple, well-understood operations so it scales to large data sets. Its output length should be long enough to keep the birthday-paradox collision chance negligibly small for the intended workload. No shortcuts, patterns, or related-key weaknesses should be known that let an attacker predict, invert, or bias the output. Independent public analysis and a lack of practical attacks are the final indicators that the hash function remains “good.”

A good hash function is deterministic, always producing the same output for the same input, and it distributes outputs uniformly across the entire hash space so collisions are statistically minimized. It exhibits the avalanche effect: a single-bit change in input alters roughly half the output bits, preventing patterns. Computation is fast and uses minimal memory so that hashing does not become a performance bottleneck. The function is easy to implement correctly and should rely only on data available in the key, avoiding expensive external look-ups. For cryptographic uses, it must also be one-way (pre-image resistant), collision-resistant, and resistant to chosen-input attacks. Output length must be large enough to make brute-force collision searches infeasible. A hash function should avoid bias and correlations that could cluster keys and degrade hash table performance. In short, a good hash function is fast, uniform, unpredictable, and hard to reverse or collide.

A good hash function is deterministic yet produces outputs that appear random and evenly distributed across the entire hash space. It minimizes collisions, making the probability that two different inputs share the same hash negligibly small. Small changes in input trigger large, unpredictable changes in output (the avalanche effect), preventing attackers from inferring relationships between inputs. It is fast and resource-efficient to compute on all common platforms. For cryptographic uses, it must also provide pre-image resistance, second-pre-image resistance, and strong collision resistance so that finding any of these in feasible time is computationally impractical. The function’s output length should be sufficient to make brute-force attacks unrealistic. It should avoid structural weaknesses that enable length-extension, side-channel, or hash-flooding attacks. Clear, well-vetted mathematical design and extensive peer review further signal that a hash function is “good.”

A good hash function (for data structures or cryptography) is deterministic, always producing the same fixed-size output for a given input. It distributes outputs uniformly across the hash space, minimizing clustering so that inputs map to buckets evenly. It exhibits the avalanche effect: a one-bit change in input changes roughly half the output bits, preventing patterns. It is quick to compute with minimal memory overhead. Collision probability is extremely low, and finding two inputs that collide should be computationally infeasible. Outputs should reveal no useful information about the inputs, making reversal or partial input recovery impractical. It must handle inputs of arbitrary length securely and efficiently. Finally, its design should resist known cryptanalytic attacks and be publicly scrutinized to confirm robustness.

A good hash function is deterministic and rapidly computable for any input size. It distributes outputs uniformly across the hash space so every bucket is equally likely, minimizing collisions. A single-bit change in the input should avalanche into a seemingly unpredictable and unrelated hash value. It should be one-way: computationally infeasible to reconstruct the original input from the hash or to find any input mapping to a chosen hash (preimage resistance). It must also resist finding two distinct inputs that produce the same hash (collision resistance) or a second input that collides with a given one (second-preimage resistance). Outputs should show no discernible patterns, ensuring that small similarities in inputs do not translate to correlations in hashes. The function should handle streaming data efficiently and produce a fixed-size output regardless of input length. Finally, its design should be publicly scrutinized, free of hidden weaknesses, and implementable without excessive memory or hardware requirements.

A good hash function is deterministic, always producing the same output for the same input. It spreads inputs uniformly across the output space, minimizing collisions. It is fast to compute, even for large data sets. Small changes in input cause large, unpredictable changes in output (avalanche effect). The output range is large enough to keep collision probability negligibly low. For cryptographic use, it must be computationally infeasible to reverse the hash (pre-image resistance) or to find two distinct inputs with the same hash (collision and second-preimage resistance).

A good hash function deterministically maps any-length input to a fixed-size output while distributing values uniformly across the output space. It keeps the probability of two different inputs colliding effectively negligible (≈1/2ⁿ for an n-bit hash). A one-bit input change should unpredictably flip about half the output bits, exhibiting the avalanche effect. The function must be quick to compute yet practically impossible to invert (pre-image resistance) or to craft a second input with the same digest (second-preimage resistance). Strong collision resistance further bars finding any pair of distinct inputs that hash identically. Outputs should pass statistical randomness tests so no structural clues about the input are leaked. The algorithm has to be deterministic, platform-independent, and devoid of hidden parameters or backdoors. Public scrutiny and reliance on sound mathematical hardness assumptions complete the criteria for a “good” hash function.

Worst-case time complexity gives an upper bound on the running time that holds for every possible input and for every sequence of random choices the algorithm might make, guaranteeing the algorithm never exceeds that time.  
Expected time complexity averages the running time over all possible outcomes of the algorithm’s internal random choices for a fixed input (often also considering the worst input), indicating the mean performance rather than the absolute maximum.  
Thus, worst case promises a strict performance ceiling, while expected case reflects typical behavior when randomness is taken into account.

For a randomized algorithm, worst-case time complexity is an absolute upper bound on running time that must hold for every input and for every possible outcome of the algorithm’s random choices.  
Expected time complexity fixes the input, takes the average of the running time over all possible outcomes of the algorithm’s random bits, and reports that mean value.  
Thus, worst-case guarantees “it will never take more than T(n) steps,” while expected guarantees “on average it takes E[n] steps, although some runs may take longer.”  
Worst-case bounds are deterministic and risk-free but can be pessimistically large.  
Expected bounds are often smaller and lead to faster practical performance, yet they allow a non-zero probability of unusually slow executions.  
Choosing between them depends on whether an application values strict predictability (favoring worst-case) or average speed with occasional variability (favoring expected).

Worst-case time complexity of a randomized algorithm is the maximum running time taken over all possible inputs and over all possible outcomes of the random choices, so it gives a guaranteed upper bound that holds for every single run.  
Expected time complexity fixes any particular input (often even the worst input) and reports the average running time when the algorithm’s internal random bits are drawn according to their probability distribution; it is an expectation, not a per-run guarantee.  
Thus, worst-case complexity protects against both adversarial inputs and unlucky random choices, whereas expected complexity only protects against adversarial inputs while accepting that some random outcomes may still be slow.  
Because the expectation is usually much lower than the absolute maximum, randomized algorithms often achieve good expected bounds (e.g., O(n log n) for randomized quicksort) even when their worst-case bound is poor (O(n²)).

Worst-case time complexity takes the maximum running time over all inputs and over all possible random choices, guaranteeing the algorithm never exceeds that bound.  
Expected time complexity fixes a specific input, averages the running time over the algorithm’s random choices, and reports that mean value, so the bound holds only on average.  
Thus an algorithm with expected O(f(n)) time may occasionally run much longer than f(n), while one with worst-case O(f(n)) time never exceeds c·f(n) steps.  
Worst-case bounds are adversary-proof and input-independent; expected bounds assume randomness is unbiased and uncorrelated with the input.  
Analytically, expected bounds exploit probability and linearity of expectation, whereas worst-case bounds require upper-bounding every random branch.  
In practice, expected-time randomized algorithms are often faster on typical runs, while worst-case bounds provide stronger but sometimes pessimistic guarantees.

Worst-case time complexity of a randomized algorithm is an upper bound on the running time that holds for every possible combination of input and random choices; it guarantees the algorithm will never exceed that time.  
Expected time complexity is the average running time taken over all possible outcomes of the algorithm’s internal random choices for a fixed input, i.e., the expected value of the runtime distribution induced by the randomness.  
Thus, worst-case treats the randomness adversarially, while expected time treats it probabilistically.  
An algorithm can have a high worst-case complexity yet low expected complexity if the costly paths are reached with very small probability.  
Conversely, if an adversary can force the algorithm into its bad random branches, only the worst-case bound protects you.

In a randomized algorithm, worst-case time complexity is an unconditional upper bound: no matter which random choices the algorithm’s coin flips make and no matter which input is supplied, the running time never exceeds that bound (e.g., ≤ cn² steps).  
Expected time complexity is a probabilistic guarantee: for any fixed input, you average the running time over all possible outcomes of the algorithm’s random choices, and the resulting expectation is the stated bound (e.g., E[T] = cn log n).  
Thus worst-case complexity provides a strict guarantee on every single execution, while expected complexity promises good performance on average but allows individual runs to be slower.  
Randomized quicksort illustrates this: with an adversarial input, some pivot sequences still incur O(n²) time (worst case), yet the expected time over all pivot choices is O(n log n).  
Because expected bounds rely on probability, additional analysis (e.g., tail bounds) is often given to show that very slow executions are unlikely even though they are possible.

Worst-case time complexity of a randomized algorithm is the maximum number of steps it can ever take, over every possible input and every possible sequence of random choices, so it is an absolute upper bound that never fails.  
Expected time complexity fixes the input, averages the running time over the algorithm’s own random bits, and reports that mean value; it therefore guarantees only that the algorithm is fast on average, not that every single execution is fast.  
Consequently, an algorithm can have an excellent expected time (e.g., O(n)) yet still admit rare executions that take much longer (e.g., O(n²)), which would be captured by the worst-case bound.  
Worst-case bounds are stronger but sometimes pessimistic; expected bounds are weaker but often more realistic for practical performance when the randomization is internal and trusted.
